deck_name: Pure Orcust (Tryout Duel - Time Travel 2019 | OCG TOSS Format)
deck_excerpt: They're back like they never left!
deck_description:
Play this in Master Duel's July Tryout Duel - Time Travel 2019 event! The event's banlist is based on the OCG's October 2019 banlist.

Have fun!

Chaos Monsters Dominate the Field

A compact Lightsworn package is great for fueling the graveyard. Grind out value until Black Luster Soldier and Chaos Sorcerer come down to solidify your lead.

 

Chaos Betrayer likes being milled.

Thunder Dragon puts a Light in your graveyard and gives you free discard fodder.

Chaos Witch can put herself in the Graveyard, and gets value when she's banished for the Chaos bosses.

Snipe Hunter is pretty good, and he can toss Light and Dark monsters into the graveyard.

Mystic Tomato can float into itself, Snipe Hunter, Witch of the Black Forest, or Necro Gardna/Bacon Saver.

Charge of the Light Brigade can search Garoth or Jain for beaters, Lyla for spell/trap removal, Ehren for defender removal, or Ryko for catch-all removal. Also fuels the graveyard!

Gold Sarcophagus finds your 1-of Black Luster Soldier or a Chaos Sorcerer.

The Warrior Returning Alive can retrieve Black Luster Soldier or one of your Lightsworn beaters.

My Body as a Shield is nice to have after you stick a Chaos boss on the field.

Twin Twisters and Magic Jammer have the added upside of putting Light and Dark monsters in your graveyard.

 

Points Breakdown (100/100)

Black Luster Soldier - Envoy of the Beginning x1 - 30

Chaos Sorcerer x2 - 20

Charge of the Light Brigade x2 - 14

Witch of the Black Forest x1 - 10

Chaos Betrayer x1 - 7

Chaos Witch x1 - 7

Compulsory Evacuation Device x2 - 4

Forbidden Chalice x1 - 2

Lyla, Lightsworn Sorceress x1 - 1

Necro Gardna x1 - 1

Book of Moon x1 - 1

Mystical Space Typhoon x1 - 1

Twin Twisters x1 - 1

Wiretap x1 - 1

Sidegrades: Solar Recharge, D.D. Warrior Lady, Allure of Darkness, Chaos Zone, Magical Merchant, other Lightsworn monsters
The wicked god, The gods of destruction

deck_excerpt: Light and Darkness Ritual deck using Branded package
deck_description:
Well, New deck time.  Yes, this build is crazy expensive, but there are clear places to cut the cost, mainly Harmonia, Sloth and Crown.  Those 8 cards would save several Hundred USD.  Otherwise its just waiting for Black Chaos to come down from its current $55-60 price point.  The main goal on T1 is to get Mind Shuffle face up with Griffoh or Black Chaos, Ragged Records and Fallen of the White Dragon both also get to Griffoh, the latter by making Crimson King to search Crimson Call, which in turn searches Griffoh.  Once you do that you search and pitch Skull Archfiend to dump Light and Darkness Ritual and add Magician of Dark Chaos - Black Chaos, use the ritual's own effect to add itself and Skull Archfiend back, then banish Griffoh or Dark Chaos to pay the ritual cost for Dark Chaos.  Mind Shuffle will add BLS - Soldier of Light and Darkness on the oponent's turn then tag in and out between them to interact with the opponent.  Celtic Mystic comes up in certain hands to serve as a Graceful Chairity and another way to summon your innitial Ritual monster.

Beyond the earlier mentioned Crimson King line, Fallen of White Dragon does a lot of its usual stuff, just now with the added option of the finally released The Three Champions Swordsouls... who do nothing for the actual swordsoul deck that makes it worth playing over Chixao, but does a lot for Branded.  I'm not really using the search effect, Cartesia and Quem are great, but don't really add anything needed here, the targeted negate and destroy is nice, though to get the destroy you need and Ecclesia or Albaz in Grave, which is just how you summon it anyway, but Ecclesia is in hand and Albaz is removed by something like a Bystial or an opposing Skull Archfiend you lose part of the effect.

 

Beyond that there isn't much to say about what is in the deck, the staples are largely the things you'd expect.  I will note I have experimented with a small package of Dark Magician of Destruction, Gaze of Timaeus and Dragoon, but cut it becuase I was legitmately hard drawing the Gaze more often than I was actually activating, the deck as presently constructed isn't really concerned about summoning Black Chaos t1, which is a necessary step to utilize this package since you should never be banishing Dark Chaos for this.  This package will be revisted in September when the Limit Over Collection cards are imported in Magnificent Monsters and we get the Dark Magician, The Pharoh's Servant and Dark Magical Curtain or whatever Konami elects to localize them as, at which point we should see a Dark Magician build of this deck become prominent and will be able to access Gaze much more consistently.

Deck Notes

Card Change

  • Scrap-Iron Scarecrow → Corpse Seller Crow

    • https://www.masterduelmeta.com/articles/news/jun-29-2026/BETB

General Thoughts

  • Milling comes pretty easily now.

  • Going second still sucks.

    • Pot of Sloth, Sphere Mode, and Evenly Matched all try to mitigate this.

    • However, you generally lose a game if you have to start second in a bo3.

Ashtra Engine

  • Ashtra really makes you think.

    • You have to remember what each card does, and then on top what the game state is and which synchro/effects are best for you.

    • Typical line:

      • Field spell (and hopefully opening with some sendable traps and some good ones).

      • Use the two Level 7s and the Level 8 from the Field Spell.

      • Make Zombie Vampire (maybe S:P if your opps graveyard starts triggering). 

      • ALWAYS run pot of d before to not get ashed! (or bait with another ashtra card)

  • Ashtra's grind game complements Paleo wonderfully.

  • Lost Wind is in the side deck.

    • It can be bounced back by Ashtra.

    • Useful if you are struggling to summon the level 9 synchro to start your recursion.

    • With Corpse Seller Crow, though, I usually feel fine.

  • Arc Light returns Ashtra to the hand to start everything again if it gets completely removed from the game state somehow.

  • Pot of Desires rarely banishes the Ashtra Level 8s needed for Zombie Vampire.

    • Even if it does, it usually is not a huge problem.

Corpse Seller Crow

  • Lets you mill a few cards.

  • Sets something new, which is nice.

  • If it resolves, you generally end up winning by turn three.

    • (Paleo usually wins on turn three or later anyway.)

Other Card Notes

  • Mannequin Cat can almost always hit an Attribute now.

  • Can run Undying Legion.

    • However, level 7s are for synchroing which is better.

  • Fiend Griefing is useful against graveyard-focused decks like Branded.

Points of Debate

  • Am I playing too few Paleo cards?

  • Can you ever have too much milling?

    • Corpse Seller Crow felt fine.

    • It felt especially strong after turn three.

    • Maybe there is something else the deck should be doing instead.

  • What is the correct ratio of Ashtra traps?

  • When should I be worried about Ashtra monsters being removed/ how to prevent?

Please ask any questions you may have!

deck_excerpt: quasar, angelechy, synchron, junk, synchro, stardust
deck_description: Sometime ago I've posted an Angelechy Quasar deck. After some matches, I found myself bricked sometimes or losing all my play after 1 missplay due to lack of attention. Anyway, after those tests, I've decided to change some things, like the addition of Monster Reborn and Designator of Crossout to fix missplays/recover a destroyed Synchro by some effect and have one more way to add cards from deck without being stopped respectively. It was already very easy to make Quasar when your hand was good with the other version of the deck, but I added Crimson Dragon, Sifr and Crystal Clear too to have even more easy to call boss monsters. On the side deck, you still having other ways to play with Snake-Eyes Doomed and Vengeance, Nullgainer to get Witness easier if you want it, Red Supernova as other option to Crimson effect, etc. Like the last time, I'd like to remember I'm just a Quasar fan and now a new Angelechy fan too, I don't have too much experience building decks, I'm just trying to make 2 things I like be good together. Still accepting tips to make the deck better :) (Btw, english isn't my native language, so I'm sorry for any gramatical mistake and I hope I expressed myself right)

deck_name: Yugi Muto - Reflection Mirror Dark Magician Control
deck_excerpt: A Link Evolution-ready Yugi upgrade that turns Reflection Mirror into streamlined Dark Magician control.
deck_description:
This build modernizes Yugi Muto's Reflection Mirror theme for Yu-Gi-Oh! Legacy of the Duelist: Link Evolution while keeping Dark Magician as the centerpiece.

 

The main plan is to loop Dark Magical Circle, Eternal Soul, Magician Navigation, and Soul Servant to maintain pressure while using Spellbook cards and Magicians' Souls to keep the hand moving. The Extra Deck adds Timaeus fusion threats, Rank 7 utility, and Link finishers so the deck can convert control into game-ending pushes.

 

Built from the Yugi Muto Legacy of the Duelist page as a competitive, character-faithful upgrade for the Link Evolution card pool.

deck_name: Yugi Muto - Reflection Mirror BLS Gaia Ritual
deck_excerpt: A faster Reflection Mirror variant built around Black Luster Soldier, Gaia pressure, and ritual consistency.
deck_description:
This Link Evolution-compatible variant upgrades the Reflection Mirror/Gaia side of Yugi's card pool into a Black Luster Soldier ritual strategy.

 

Impcantations and Manju find ritual access, Beginning Knight and Evening Twilight Knight add pressure and removal, and Gateway to Chaos ties the BLS and Gaia pieces together. The Extra Deck is tuned for generic Link, Rank 8, and Gaia utility plays when the ritual line opens extra bodies.

 

The goal is a more competitive Yugi-flavored build without losing the Gaia and Black Luster Soldier identity.

deck_name: Yugi Muto - The Final Duel Magnet Block Dragon
deck_excerpt: A competitive Magnet Warrior shell using Block Dragon, Ties of the Brethren, and Rank 4 utility.
deck_description:
This deck upgrades Yugi Muto's The Final Duel list into a focused Magnet Warrior control-combo deck for Link Evolution.

 

Alpha, Beta, Gamma, and Delta load the field and graveyard, while Ties of the Brethren creates strong early boards backed by Koa'ki Meiru Guardian and Infinite Impermanence. Block Dragon gives the deck grind power and explosive Link/Xyz follow-up, with Berserkion and Valkyrion preserving the anime boss monster identity.

 

It is built to feel like Yugi's final duel strategy, but trimmed into a cleaner competitive shell.

deck_name: Yugi Muto - Rise of Skull Servants Lightsworn Wight
deck_excerpt: A mill-heavy Wight build that pushes King of the Skull Servants into one-shot territory fast.
deck_description:
This is a competitive upgrade of Yugi's Rise of Skull Servants challenge deck, leaning into the Lightsworn/Wight graveyard engine.

 

Charge of the Light Brigade, Raiden, Uni-Zombie, Wightprince, and Wightprincess fill the graveyard quickly so King of the Skull Servants becomes a real finisher. The Zombie package adds extension through Mezuki, Doomking Balerdroch, and Necroworld Banshee, while the Extra Deck gives the deck Link, Synchro, and Rank 3 routes.

 

The result is still recognizably Skull Servants, just tuned to play at a much sharper Link Evolution power level.

deck_name: Yugi Muto - Rise of Skull Servants Zombie World Wight
deck_excerpt: Zombie World control meets Wight aggression for a stronger Link Evolution Skull Servant list.
deck_description:
This Link Evolution version turns Rise of Skull Servants into a Zombie World Wight deck with a stronger control backbone.

 

The Wight core builds huge King of the Skull Servants attacks, while Uni-Zombie, Shiranui Solitaire, Mezuki, Necroworld Banshee, and Doomking Balerdroch create recurring disruption. Zombie World plus Super Polymerization gives the deck interaction into established boards, and the Extra Deck supports both Super Poly removal and generic Zombie/Link pressure.

 

It keeps the Skull Servant identity while giving the deck more resilience and disruption.

deck_description:
An Assault mode deck sublimated with 2 sub-engines 1st being Stardust Synchron (and friends 6 cards)  and a 6 card (main deck) Albaz and Ecclesia package.

 The core of the deck is here it is 19 main deck and 4 extra deck.

You have 6 normal summons Psi-Reflector and Assault Sentinel which gets you Psi-Reflector.

You also have 6 searchers in Crimson Blader/Assault Mode and Duel Evolution - Assault Zone that can be used to start your plays.

But you have 5 soft bricks(in Assault Lich, Assault Beast, Assault Counter, and Assault Mode Activate)  and 2 HARD bricks (Stardust Dragon/Assault Mode).

In the extra deck you need 2 Stardust Dragon, 1 Crimson Blader, and Nightwinged Cleric, this is the minimum needed for this core you should play in the extra, you can cut the 3rd Stardust Dragon if you want.

Next is the extender so a lot of the time you have an extra searcher, this is where Assault Sonic Warrior comes in it lets you grab Stardust Synchro which is a great discard for Nightwinged Cleric you may then Tribute Assault Sonic Warrior for Stardust Synchro and do its little combo as you WILL be making a Stardust Dragon, so Stardust Illumination can summon Stardust Trail from the deck

For the extra deck of this one I've made space for Accel Synchro Stardust Dragon and Crystal Wing Synchro Dragon to make use of Stardust Illumination GY effect to lower Accel to 7 and use Accel to bring back Psi-Reflector to make Crystal Wing


So this Might be the wrong thing to spend most of my points on, but I've spent 67 points (and 7 of my Extra deck cards) on Albaz and Ecclesia cards but like Ecclesia and the Dark Dragon and Fallen of the White Dragon are just kinda crazy cards. Both Ecclesias can tag into White Albaz with is an 8 Synchro and both Ecclesias can tag into an og Fallen of Albaz to Fusion on either turn. These cards represent a lot of board-breaking and turn 3 potential the deck plays really well under the "lock", but 67 is a lot and White Albaz is still money and without it the most expensive card would be Stardust Dragon/Assault Mode at 2 bucks a pop so if you would like to play more hand traps and have a better side that's up to you.

deck_excerpt: Control the board with smug energy and endless Spell recycling!
deck_description:
Overview

This is a Pure Witchcrafter deck focused on resource management, locking down the opponent's monster effects, and out-grinding them through the endless recycling of Spell cards. By keeping the engine purely Witchcrafter, the deck maximizes its consistency with Spellcaster synergy and maintains a high count of native Spell cards to fuel our Smug Queen, -Witchcrafter Madame Verre.

Key Strategies & Combo Lines

  • The Core Engine: Use the low-level Smug Apprentices (-Witchcrafter Schmietta, -Witchcrafter Pittore, and -Witchcrafter Potterie) to tribute themselves and instantly tag out into your high-level bosses depending on the matchup.

  • The Boss Monsters:

    • -Witchcrafter Madame Verre: Your primary floodgate. She can negate all face-up monsters your opponent controls and boost your Spellcaster's ATK/DEF by revealing different Spells in your hand.

    • -Witchcrafter Haine: Provides targeted quick-effect destruction for face-up cards and gives your other Spellcasters untargetable protection.

    • -Witchcrafter Vice-Madame: (If playing the Fusion version) The ultimate engine driver that triggers whenever a Spell card or a Spellcaster monster effect is activated.

  • Spell Recycling Loop: All "Witchcrafter" Spells (like -Witchcrafter Creation, -Witchcrafter Holiday, and -Witchcrafter Unveiling) return to your hand during the End Phase if you control a Witchcrafter monster. This ensures you always have discard fodder for your monster effects next turn.

Strengths & Weaknesses

Pros:

  • Excellent grind game; you rarely run out of resources in the mid-to-late game.

  • Strong multi-monster negation via Madame Verre.

  • Highly customizable Tech choices due to the high Spell count (e.g., -Reasoning, -Magician's Restage).

Cons:

  • Vulnerable to hand traps like -Ash Blossom & Joyous Spring or -Called by the Grave on your normal summon.

  • Can brick if you draw too many high-level monsters and no discard outlets.

It is a fun pick for players who like patient, reactive games. You are not trying to combo off immediately; you are setting a trap, trading cleanly, and turning every interrupted play into Lambda, Omega, Zeta, or another awkward problem.

deck_excerpt: winda is a crutch you don't need
deck_description:
Seeing new gen players read El Shaddoll Construct is hilarious. Ratios are pretty solved at this point. I've seen some people cut Falco but I like it for the grind. I play Genius in case I lose the die roll. The deck is pretty good going second anyway, with Shaddoll Fusion and Lev Shaddoll dump N'tss to break boards. You only need 1 of each Spell/Trap because of how searchable and recyclable they are (3 Fusion is just for going second but you could play only 1 and side the other 2 if you want). Cartesia + Fusion Deployment is standard, another way to fuse. Be careful though, you can't make Apkallone or fusion Wendi with her, as they are Level 6. The Wake Cup cards are insane for this deck. Kuro adds Wendi and both it and Earl discard names for effect which triggers them. Earl also lets you reset your names in End Phase and potentially can be used to break boards with it and fusion Wendi. Sol and Luna is the MVP, especially if you make a half board. The hand traps are mainly for the Attributes, a LIGHT for Construct or an extra EARTH for Mesh in case you can't get to main deck Grysta.

Extra Deck is 3 Construct and 3 Mesh because they are your whole game plan. 2 Apkallone, 1 to dump off Lev and 1 to summon on your opponent's turn. I play 2 Shek and 2 Wendi in the Extra Deck because Fairy Tail Luna is an annoying card otherwise. Granguignol for Fusion Deployment and N'tss/Garura for Lev dumps if you already have combo.

The Side Deck is really whatever. It could definitely use some work. Either Kumungous (EARTH) or Thunder King (LIGHT) is fine because you need a Kaiju, Chaos Hunter is theoretically to counter Shifter (it hasn't come up for me), and the rest is generic good stuff.

deck_excerpt: Infinite omni-negate one-card combo. Dragunity / Superheavy Samurai / Earth Machine
deck_description:
Speedroid Terrortop is a one-card combo that leads to an infinite once-per-chain omni-negate, using Mist Valley Apex Avian (summoned by Simorgh, Bird of Sovereignty) and Mist Valley Thunderbird (equipped to Simorgh, Bird of Sovereignty using Union Carrier). Simorgh, Bird of Sovereignty protects them from targeting effects. Also, after the first opponent's turn, if they didn't manage to break your board, you can summon Barrier Statue of the Stormwinds, which will protect you from Kaiju-like effects. If you want to try this combo in decks with other deck masters, your deck master's domain must contain Wind and Machine monsters. Any 2 monsters, including a tuner, can start the combo.

You can see the one-card combo here: https://www.masterduelmeta.com/combo/6a4b2b8b87c0deb271544d21. If you know a way to make the combo with fewer steps, please leave a comment in the section at the bottom of this page.

So, because the combo only needs 9 cards in the main deck and 8 cards in the extra deck, you can use your creativity to fill the rest. This deck uses protection, board breakers, ways to unbrick hands, and a lot of draw spells to increase consistency. I'm testing the new Train cards, but because Night Train Blue Traveler locks you into machines, you can only do the combo after you summon Simorgh, Bird of Sovereignty. I think more Dragunity extenders might work better.

The superheavy samurai archetype provides this deck a pendulum summon to summon the bricks you may draw, and allows you to summon Baronne de Fleur to protect your combo. If you have either Superheavy Samurai Prodigy Wakaushi or Superheavy Samurai Motorbike, the combo is also Nibiru, the Primal Being proof: https://www.masterduelmeta.com/combo/6a4c3f255b45d741ff9297fb. Otherwise, you can also normal summon Superheavy Samurai Wagon or Superheavy Samurai Soulpiercer, and they'll search Superheavy Samurai Prodigy Wakaushi for you, using Superheavy Samurai Scarecrow. You have a 35% chance of drawing one of them. If you draw them after playing a board breaker or a draw spell, don't worry, just use them for the discard cost of other effects. Don't forget you have consistent infinite negates and other plays, so a few bricks really aren't that big of an issue.

Other than Kaijus on your first opponent turn, your board can also get broken if your opponents chain board breakers to your negates (which is why other negates are useful) and if they play cards like Super Polymerization, Dark Ruler No More, and Forbidden Droplet, which you cannot respond to. To avoid losing to those cards, this deck uses floodgates like Imperial Order, Anti-Spell Fragrance, and most importantly, Angelica's Angelic Ring. You can equip it to Mist Valley Apex Avian, and it will negate those cards you can't respond to, as long as you negate every other spell before them (you may also choose to use other negates on quick-play spells activated in chains so that you don't waste this effect).

Angelica's Angelic Ring is searchable with Hidden Armory, Return of the Duelist, and Armory Call. Without considering your draw spells (which you'll also activate after the combo), you have a 48% chance to draw one of those cards in your first 6 cards. If you're not happy enough with those chances, you can also add Magician's Right Hand (which works the same as long as you have a spellcaster), Secret Village of the Spellcasters, Magician's Left Hand, Foolish Burial Goods, and Painful Choice, now you'll have a 72% chance of drawing at least one of them on your starting hand (not considering any draw cards), but then you also have a 24% chance to draw two too, which is not necessarily good.

Made to Order Mermaid Outfit Outfitter and Superheavy Samurai Stealthy are great, with so many spell staples for card draw, boardbreakers, and generic starters like Foolish Burial and Last Will. You can also run Double Spell if you really like those cards.

 

Alternatives:

Apollousa, Bow of the Goddess: I wish there was enough space to include this.

Stardust Dragon - Victim Sanctuary: Another option to go into with Superheavy Samurai Prodigy Wakaushi to protect your plays, instead of Karakuri Steel Shogun mdl 00X "Bureido" and Baronne de Fleur.

Wynn the Wind Charmer, Verdant, Majesty Pegasus, the Dracoslayer: The spellcaster package to block your opponents from using spells.

Book of Eclipse, Illusion Gate, Raigeki, Special Hurricane: Board breakers are never too many in this format, even more if you are playing against high-power decks. If you manage to include Super Polymerization, it's also a good option.

The Forceful Sentry, Confiscation, Exchange, Trap Dustshoot, Appointer of the Red Lotus, Contract with Don Thousand: Those hand rips are usually not worth it in multiplayer games, but this deck can really benefit from using them. You can remove their board wipes and quick spells, and get information about what else they have. They also get much stronger if you play politically and manage to identify which player has access to those board wipes before activating the card. Contract with Don Thousand can be used to see your opponent's hands, and, if they are locked out of spells from Secret Village of the Spellcasters, stop them from summoning their deck masters.

Speedroid Den-Den Daiko Duke: It's another card to search with the deck master on follow-up turns. Can also summon Speedroid Fuki-Modoshi Piper from the graveyard.

Harpie Conductor: If you manage to summon a lot of monsters, but can't conclude your Dragunity combo for some reason, you can use this card to summon Simorgh, Bird of Sovereignty.

Non-Fusion Area, Dimensional Barrier, Mask of Restrict, Magic Deflector: The first two are floodgates to stop Super Polymerization specifically. Mask of Restrict is used to protect yourself from Kaijus. Magic Deflector can be used to stop one of your opponents (it's much better with the cards from the discard package). I don't think any of those are worth the slot.

Qliphort Genius, Spell Canceller: Another way to stop your opponent from playing spells. I find it hard to activate, and you lose your chance after you summon Simorgh, Bird of Sovereignty.

Kashtira Unicorn, Pressured Planet Wraitsoth, Therion Discolosseum: Extenders that can also be used as interruption and removal bait.

Small World 

Planet Pathfinder 

Symphonic Warrior Guitaar, Symphonic Warrior Miccs: An extra normal summon can be useful depending on the archetypes you're running. Can be searched with Gear Gigant X and complements the scale from Superheavy Samurai Monk Big Benkei nicely.

More Machine cards. More Dragunity cards.

Angeclechy is great but has little to no main deck presence and Asutra is awesome with little extra deck presence... both use Sycnhros so what could go wrong?

This list uses the Asutra engine for control and Angelechy to provide massive value. Opening e4 lets us get a potent turn 0 while also setting up for our Ashutra engine on our turn. A fun interaction that can get absurd value is Mikoto with Knight, granting advantage and burn for every monster effect played by our opponent which we can set one up with Pawn!

Pain points with the build are:

6 Field Spells. The two field spells do compete with eachother for value in all honesty however, you can get all the value from our Asutra field spell and then use e4 on our opponent's turn to get our Angelechy field spell. Our Angelechy Field also serves to convert extra field spells into more pawns and backrow.

Low Non-Engine. You could replace the Toybox engine for more non-engine cards for going second but this version really relies on our busted handtraps even if we don't have many of them.

Overall a fun combination which is fun to mess around with. There may be some potential with further testing!

deck_name: Strings - Slime Revival Paleo Frog Slifer [Low Rogue]
deck_excerpt: LOW ROGUE BUILD - Strings' Slime Revival rebuilt as Paleo Frog control with Slifer as the anime payoff.
deck_description:

Consistency: Strong - Swap Frog, Dupe Frog, Pot of Extravagance, and Paleozoic traps keep the deck moving.

Ceiling: Solid - Toadally Awesome and Paleozoic bodies control the table while Slifer gives the deck its Strings payoff.

deck_name: Strings - Trail of Slime Wetlands Paleo [Low Rogue]

deck_excerpt: Yubel still packs a punch nearly 2 years later!
deck_description:
Yubel was a highly tiered deck that unites multiple fiend-based strategies together and can still pack a competitive punch to this day! Known under its other name "fiend link," it centres on generating immense advantage by abusing Yubel's not-once-per-turn effect to summon herself upon destruction, then contact fusing from the GY for "Phantom of Yubel," a monster negate! This allows the deck to create absurdly strong boards under the protective umbrella of Yubel, warding off turn-ending threats such as Nibiru, Droll, Mulcharmies and Maxx "C!" 

This iteration of Yubel has been updated to include modern engines such as Dark Magician of Destruction's The Zombei Vampire (consistency booster) and Dragoon (omni-negate). It also includes Fiendsmith, which is basically full Yubel combo and a ton of link material with follow-up! A full uninterrupted Yubel board consists of 10+ interruptions, a gamestate that all meta decks must respect. Even if Yubel is interrupted, its ability to push through handtraps and the half-boards that she can put up are competent as well!

With Yubel essentially at full power with all her engine cards unbanned/unlimited by previous banlists, she is ready to dominate Masterduel ladder just like the Duel Spirit Dimensions!

GLHF Duelists!

Want to see the deck in action? Scroll to the very bottom for the Youtube video!
Il Deck delle Sacre Bestie con i nuovi supporti

deck_name: Seto Kaiba - Chaos MAX Blue-Eyes [High Rogue]
deck_excerpt: HIGH ROGUE BUILD - Kaiba's Legendary Blue-Eyes rebuilt as a Chaos MAX OTK deck with Rank 8 backup.
deck_description:
Power: High Rogue - the flagship Kaiba build, focused on fast Chaos MAX pressure backed by modern Blue-Eyes consistency.

Consistency: Strong - Melody, Bingo Machine, Trade-In, Advanced Ritual Art, and the Impcantation package find ritual pieces quickly.

Ceiling: Strong - Chaos MAX threatens immediate double-piercing OTKs, while Rank 8 and Blue-Eyes Extra Deck tools give backup pressure.

Recovery: Moderate - Monster Reborn and repeat Blue-Eyes bodies help after trades, but ritual hands still want careful sequencing.

Going first/second: Prefers going second to clear a path and end the duel, but can sit behind Hope Harbinger-style Extra Deck pressure when forced first.

Choke points: Stop Melody or ritual access, remove Manju before it converts, and keep defense-position monsters off the board against Chaos MAX.

Key cards: Blue-Eyes Chaos MAX Dragon, Blue-Eyes White Dragon, Blue-Eyes Alternative White Dragon, Chaos Form, Advanced Ritual Art, The Melody of Awakening Dragon.

Key combos: Melody finds Blue-Eyes plus Alternative; Advanced Ritual Art sends Blue-Eyes for Chaos MAX; spare Level 8 dragons become Rank 8 pressure.

deck_name: Seto Kaiba - Ultimate Dragon Rank 8 [Low Rogue]
deck_excerpt: LOW ROGUE BUILD - The Ultimate Dragon theme rebuilt as Blue-Eyes fusion pressure with Rank 8 finishers.
deck_description:
Power: Low Rogue - a stronger Ultimate Dragon tribute that keeps Kaiba's fusion identity while adding real Rank 8 lines.

Consistency: Solid - Sage, Stone, Melody, Trade-In, Dragon Shrine, and Cards of Consonance keep dragons moving through hand and graveyard.

Ceiling: Strong - Neo Blue-Eyes Ultimate Dragon and Rank 8 closers can end games quickly when the setup sticks.

Recovery: Solid - Return of the Dragon Lords, Monster Reborn, Stone, and Dragon Spirit of White give the deck a real grind plan.

Going first/second: Comfortable going second into battle pressure, with enough Extra Deck utility to make a respectable first-turn board.

Choke points: Interrupt Sage or Melody, banish graveyard dragons before revival cards resolve, and stop Polymerization or Dragon's Mirror turns.

Key cards: Blue-Eyes Ultimate Dragon, Neo Blue-Eyes Ultimate Dragon, Blue-Eyes Alternative White Dragon, Sage with Eyes of Blue, Trade-In.

Key combos: Sage finds Stone; Stone loads Blue-Eyes access; two Level 8 dragons make Draglubion or Hope Harbinger; fusion spells turn stocked dragons into Ultimate Dragon threats.

deck_name: Seto Kaiba - Lair Virus Blue-Eyes [Low Rogue]
deck_excerpt: LOW ROGUE BUILD - Crushing Victory becomes Lair of Darkness Virus control with Blue-Eyes as the Kaiba payoff.
deck_description:
Power: Low Rogue - a control-heavy Kaiba build that makes Crush Card Virus and Blue-Eyes matter together.

Consistency: Solid - Lair of Darkness, Ahrima, Lilith, Melody, Trade-In, and Dragon Shrine find both the trap engine and dragon pressure.

Ceiling: Strong - Virus traps can rip apart the opponent's hand or board while Blue-Eyes and Rank 8 monsters close the game.

Recovery: Moderate - Diabolos and revival cards help rebuild, though trap-heavy hands need the right matchup.

Going first/second: Strongest going first, where Lair plus Lilith can set up live Virus disruption before the opponent builds momentum.

Choke points: Remove Lair of Darkness, negate Lilith, and force Virus traps before they hit meaningful cards.

Key cards: Crush Card Virus, Lair of Darkness, Lilith, Lady of Lament, Darkest Diabolos, Blue-Eyes White Dragon, The Fang of Critias.

Key combos: Lair turns opposing monsters DARK; Lilith accesses Virus traps; Critias converts Crush Card into Doom Virus Dragon; Level 8 dragons pivot into Rank 8 pressure.

deck_name: Seto Kaiba - Thunder Dragon Chaos [High Rogue]
deck_excerpt: HIGH ROGUE BUILD - Summoning Dragons rebuilt as Thunder Dragon Chaos Link with a Kaiba dragon core.
deck_description:
Power: High Rogue - the most competitive Kaiba-adjacent shell here, using Thunder Dragon and Chaos engines from the Link Evolution era.

Consistency: Strong - Thunder Dragon names, Gold Sarcophagus, Allure of Darkness, Batteryman Solar, and Melody create steady access to bodies.

Ceiling: Very strong - Thunder Dragon Colossus, Titan, Guardragon links, Levianeer, and Rank 8 pressure can overwhelm casual rogue tables.

Recovery: Strong - Thunder Dragon recursion, Fusion recycling, and Chaos Dragon Levianeer give the deck follow-up after disruption.

Going first/second: Plays well first with Colossus and link pressure, while Levianeer and Titan help break boards going second.

Choke points: Stop Gold Sarcophagus or Batteryman Solar, banish the wrong Thunder names, and interrupt Guardragon link progression.

Key cards: Thunder Dragon Colossus, Thunder Dragon Titan, Chaos Dragon Levianeer, Thunder Dragondark, Gold Sarcophagus, Blue-Eyes White Dragon.

Key combos: Gold Sarcophagus banishes a Thunder name; Thunder bodies climb into links; Chaos monsters convert LIGHT/DARK graveyard setup into removal and pressure.

deck_name: Seto Kaiba - ABC Obelisk Union [Low Rogue]
deck_excerpt: LOW ROGUE BUILD - The Tormentor becomes ABC Union control with Obelisk as the Kaiba boss monster payoff.
deck_description:
Power: Low Rogue - ABC gives the deck real interaction while Obelisk keeps the Battle City Kaiba identity intact.

Consistency: Solid - Union Hangar, Terraforming, gadgets, Galaxy Soldier, and ABC pieces give repeatable machine access.

Ceiling: Strong - ABC-Dragon Buster provides quick disruption, Cyber Dragon Infinity adds negation, and Photon Sanctuary helps tribute into Obelisk.

Recovery: Solid - Union Scramble and floating Union pieces rebuild after removal, though banish effects can limit follow-up.

Going first/second: Prefers going first to establish ABC or Infinity, then pivots into Obelisk when the duel slows down.

Choke points: Remove Union Hangar, stop B-Buster Drake searches, banish ABC pieces, and interrupt Galaxy Soldier before Infinity appears.

Key cards: ABC-Dragon Buster, Union Hangar, B-Buster Drake, Galaxy Soldier, Photon Sanctuary, Obelisk the Tormentor.

Key combos: Union Hangar equips missing pieces; ABC pieces contact into Dragon Buster; Galaxy Soldier makes Infinity; Photon Sanctuary supplies tribute material for Obelisk.

deck_name: Seto Kaiba - Critias Lair Traps [Low Rogue]
deck_excerpt: LOW ROGUE BUILD - The Legendary Critias rebuilt as Lair trap control with real Critias fusion payoffs.
deck_description:
Power: Low Rogue - trap-control first, anime-fusion second, giving Critias the best practical shell without losing the theme.

Consistency: Solid - Ahrima and Lair find the DARK tribute engine, while Trap Trick and three Fang of Critias copies make the trap plan show up.

Ceiling: Solid - Virus disruption, Tyrant Burst Dragon, Mirror Force Dragon, and Borrelsword give the deck varied ways to swing games.

Recovery: Moderate - Diabolos and trap density help grind, but Critias fusion lines still depend on drawing the correct pieces.

Going first/second: Prefers going first to set Virus traps and Critias targets, then punishes attacks or overextension.

Choke points: Remove Lair, banish or negate Fang of Critias, and force trap activations before the fusion payoff is available.

Key cards: The Fang of Critias, Crush Card Virus, Tyrant Wing, Mirror Force, Lair of Darkness, Lilith, Lady of Lament.

Key combos: Fang plus Crush Card makes Doom Virus Dragon; Fang plus Tyrant Wing makes Tyrant Burst Dragon; Lair and Lilith keep the trap engine live.

deck_name: Seto Kaiba - Blue-Eyes Synchro Rank 8 [Low Rogue]
deck_excerpt: LOW ROGUE BUILD - Wrath of the Dragon rebuilt as classic Blue-Eyes Synchro and Rank 8 pressure.
deck_description:
Power: Low Rogue - a faithful Blue-Eyes dragon deck with stronger Synchro, revival, and Rank 8 lines.

Consistency: Strong - Sage, Stones, Melody, Trade-In, Cards of Consonance, and Dragon Shrine keep Blue-Eyes access flowing.

Ceiling: Strong - Spirit Dragon, Azure-Eyes, Crystal Wing, Hope Harbinger, Draglubion, and Numeron Dragon give several boss-monster routes.

Recovery: Strong - White Stone of Ancients, Return of the Dragon Lords, Silver's Cry, and Monster Reborn make the deck resilient in longer games.

Going first/second: Can go first with Spirit Dragon or Rank 8 protection, but still enjoys going second with Alternative and big dragon combat.

Choke points: Stop Sage, banish Stones before the End Phase, interrupt Melody, and remove graveyard dragons before revival spells resolve.

Key cards: Blue-Eyes Spirit Dragon, Azure-Eyes Silver Dragon, Blue-Eyes Alternative White Dragon, Sage with Eyes of Blue, The White Stone of Ancients.

Key combos: Sage finds Stones; Stones convert graveyard setup into Blue-Eyes access; Level 8 bodies make Rank 8 monsters; Spirit Dragon tags into Synchro utility.

deck_excerpt: New version of a full charmers deck
deck_description: New version of a full charmers deck
This a small change for a blitzclique ryzeal deck 45 max cards

The line is:

-Do something that makes a Level 8 Synchro (P.U.N.K. engine, Normal Summon White Albaz, etc, etc)

-Synchro Summon Nullgainer, Nullgainer search the new Overframe.

-Special the Overframe, place Rook/Knight in your backrow and summon a level 2 Token.

-CL 1 Knight to grab the field spell, CL 2 Rook to put the King in your backrow.

-New Chain, King grabs Disturbance.

-Make a Level 10 Synchro with the Token and Witness (I'd suggest Chaos Angel).

-Nullgainer effect reborn Witness. Send Nullgainer and Witness to summon Ultimya Tzolkin.

-Set the Field Spell, trigger Tzolkin to summon any Level 7 or 8 Dragon Synchro. You have options like AFD here to get any other Field Spell in the game, Crystal Wing for a monster negate, or Bramble Rose for more effect damage.

-Activate Field Spell, use the effect by pitching Disturbance to place 2nd Knight and Summon 1st Pawn. You don't need to keep the Pawn around, you can Synch it off with any body you have. I'd advise making Crimson Dragon here, so that you can tag it out by targeting Tzolkin to Summon Blazar or another omni-negate.

-Disturbance GY effect, search E4. Set E4.

-Pass Turn.

Endboard is: Tzolkin, Blazar, and a rando Dragon Synchro, all in your MMZ.

x2 Knight, Rook, King, Field Spell, E4 in your backrow.

In Draw, activate E4 to activate a Field Spell from Deck, Summon 2nd Pawn, and place 3rd Knight.

Opponent must pay 1500 LP on res of each effect, and has to play through Blazar and the rando Dragon Synchro. This 5 total card effect activations. The number gets harsher if you decide to end on Bramble Rose or Assualt Black-Winged instead.

On the Ashtra Engine

Akasuna the Battle Ashtra is an insane card with a versatile toolkit.

  1. He can special summon himself from your hand by revealing and then returning a set card you control to the hand or extra deck. He's practically a free special summon with all the traps this deck plays, and he can return one of the monsters you've flipped face-down with Destructive Daruma Karma Cannon. He also works well with Big Welcome Labrynth, which can banish itself from your graveyard to target and return a monster your opponent controls to the hand if you control a level 8 or higher fiend.
  2. Akasuna's quick effect can flip all your opponent's monsters face-down by sending either a trap from your hand or a face-down trap you control to the graveyard. You can send Transaction Rollback, The Black Goat Laughs, or a spare Big Welcome Labrynth to get some additional value from their graveyard effects.
  3. His third effect can set Ashtra Cursed Roar directly from your deck.

Ashtra Cursed Roar is equally as versatile.

  1. Its first effect can special summon itself as a level 1 tuner before synchro summoning 1 monster. Pairing Cursed Roar with Akasuna, Lovely Labrynth of the Silver Castle, or Lady Labrynth of the Silver Castle lets you synchro summon Ashtraja Susanoo, a card we'll discuss shortly. You can also special summon Cursed Roar to synchro summon Chaos Angel with your other monsters, since this effect does not require Cursed Roar to be used as synchro material.
  2. Its second effect targets and destroys a monster your opponent controls if you have an Ashtra monster on your field or in your graveyard. It provides reliable removal.

Ashtraja Susanoo is the final Ashtra card this deck plays, and it's no less crazy than the rest.

  1. If Susanoo is synchro summoned, you can banish any number of traps from your graveyard to banish that many cards on the field. This effect does not target and can nuke entire fields.
  2. His second effect targets up to two Ashtra monsters and/or traps in your banished zone or graveyard to recycle or set each card. If you targeted two cards with this effect, Susanoo returns to the extra deck. It's an excellent way to recycle your resources while letting you synchro summon Susanoo again to wipe the field a second time.

Some Tips

You can return the card set by Triple Tactics Thrust to your hand with Akasuna the Battle Ashtra's effect, letting you search for and use Triple Tactics Talent on your first turn even if your opponent doesn't control a monster.

Lady Labrynth of the Silver Castle, Trap Holic, and Triple Tactics Thrust can access Ashtra Cursed Roar.

Unchained Soul Lord of Yama and Muckraker From the Underworld can revive Akasuna and Ashtraja Susanoo, while Unchained Soul of Rage can recycle all four monsters in this package.

deck_excerpt: The Great Pumpking gets some help from his zombie friends for a game of Domain!
deck_description: A Zombie deck for Domain format, starring Pumpking and friends!

Some Tips

First Penguin can discard 1 card and reveal Ashtraya Mikumari-no-Mikoto in your extra deck to search for Mikumari the Sealing Deity Ashtra, then flip itself face-down. Mikumari the Sealing Deity can then reveal the face-down First Penguin, return it to your hand, and special summon herself from your hand.

The Fallen engine currently has little synergy with the Ashtra archetype. The only common link between the two archetypes is Mikumari-no-Mikoto, which currently doesn't lead to very impactul Ashtra plays. However, the Fallen engine is still a great synchro engine overall.

Any of this deck's Ashtra traps can special summon itself as a level 1 tuner and synchro summon on your opponent's turn.

A level 1 Ashtra trap + First Penguin = Formula Synchron

Formula Synchron allows you to draw 1 card when it is synchro summoned and enables a synchro summon during your opponent's main phase, giving you additional interaction on their turn.

Formula Synchron + any level 4 non-tuner monster in this deck = Brionac, the Magical Ice Dragon

Brionac can discard 1 card to target and return a card on the field to the hand if it is synchro summoned, but it's unlikely that you would summon it in the first place. This card can be replaced with something else.

deck_excerpt: LOW ROGUE BUILD - A fast Seeker-style Exodia draw deck using Royal Magical Library, Bamboo Sword loops, and Chicken Game pressure.
deck_description:
Power: Low Rogue - fast and dangerous for an Exodia strategy, but still fragile into modern disruption.

 

Consistency: Medium-high for an Exodia build; Royal Magical Library, the Bamboo Sword engine, Chicken Game, Upstart Goblin, Into the Void, and Magical Mallet dig aggressively, but hands without Library or enough draw chains can stall out.

 

Ceiling: High when uninterrupted - the deck can win immediately by assembling all five Exodia pieces in hand, with Called by the Grave helping against key hand traps.

 

Recovery: Low; if the first big draw turn is stopped, Reckless Greed and spent spells make rebuilding difficult.

 

Going first/second: Best going first. Going second can steal games if the opponent has no lock or negate wall, but established boards make the combo much weaker.

 

Choke points: Royal Magical Library, Golden Bamboo Sword resolving, Droll & Lock Bird, Ash Blossom on draw/search effects, and removal on the Bamboo/Chicken Game setup.

 

Key cards: Exodia the Forbidden One, Royal Magical Library, Golden Bamboo Sword, Cursed Bamboo Sword, Chicken Game, Into the Void, Called by the Grave.

 

Key combos: Library plus repeated spell activations builds counters into draws; Cursed Bamboo Sword finds Golden or Broken Bamboo Sword; Golden Bamboo Sword turns equips into more cards; Chicken Game, Terraforming, Upstart, and Into the Void compress the deck until Exodia is assembled.

 

This is a legal Link Evolution-era take on Seeker's infamous Cheating Exodia deck: no illegal extra Exodia limbs, just a pushed draw engine built to feel like the Rare Hunter trying to see the whole deck at once.

deck_excerpt: CASUAL BUILD - A defensive Exodia stall deck that buys turns with Battle Fader, Waboku, Threatening Roar, and Hope for Escape.
deck_description:
Power: Casual - functional and flavorful, but slower than the dedicated draw-combo version.

 

Consistency: Medium; Battle Fader, Swift Scarecrow, Waboku, Threatening Roar, and Swords of Revealing Light protect the hand while Cardcar D, Upstart Goblin, Pot of Duality, Jar of Greed, and Hope for Escape dig for Exodia.

 

Ceiling: Moderate; it can grind into a surprise Exodia finish if it survives several turns and converts life-point gaps into Hope for Escape draws.

 

Recovery: Medium-low; the deck can chain defensive cards for a while, but loses momentum when repeated stall pieces are forced early or removed before battle.

 

Going first/second: Prefers going first to set traps, though Battle Fader and Swift Scarecrow make going second less helpless than most stall decks.

 

Choke points: Backrow removal before the Battle Phase, trap negation, Droll & Lock Bird, Ash Blossom on draw/search effects, and pressure that avoids Battle Phase damage prevention.

 

Key cards: Exodia the Forbidden One, Hope for Escape, Battle Fader, Swift Scarecrow, Cardcar D, Emissary of the Afterlife, Waboku, Threatening Roar.

 

Key combos: Cardcar D turns safe turns into extra cards; Emissary of the Afterlife can search normal Exodia limbs when it leaves the field; Battle Fader, Swift Scarecrow, Waboku, and Threatening Roar buy the turns needed for Hope for Escape to draw into pieces.

 

This is the more anime-faithful Seeker build: slow, sneaky, and built around surviving long enough for the coming of Exodia to feel inevitable in casual games.

deck_name: Rex Raptor - Red-Eyes Lost World Dino [High Rogue]
deck_excerpt: HIGH ROGUE BUILD - Rex keeps the Red-Eyes dinosaur theme while Lost World, Oviraptor, and Ultimate Conductor Tyranno do the real hunting.
deck_description:
Power: High Rogue - the strongest identity-safe Rex build, using a proven Link Evolution-era Dinosaur core while keeping a small Red-Eyes package.

 

Consistency: Excellent; Fossil Dig, Souleating Oviraptor, Miscellaneousaurus, Lost World, Dragonic Diagram, and Terraforming give the deck many ways to reach starter lines.

 

Ceiling: Excellent; Ultimate Conductor Tyranno backed by Babycerasaurus, True King lines, Dolkka/Laggia access, and Borrelsword pushes hard through casual and non-meta boards.

 

Recovery: Strong; Oviraptor, Giant Rex, Miscellaneousaurus, Monster Reborn, and Red-Eyes Darkness Metal Dragon give follow-up after the first exchange.

 

Going first/second: Flexible. Going first can make Evolzar interruption or establish Lost World pressure; going second can break boards with UCT, Pankratops, and side-deck blowouts.

 

Choke points: Fossil Dig or Souleating Oviraptor being stopped early, Miscellaneousaurus being answered before protection matters, Lost World token lines being interrupted, and Double Evolution Pill being negated.

 

Key cards: Souleating Oviraptor, Miscellaneousaurus, Babycerasaurus, Ultimate Conductor Tyranno, Lost World, Double Evolution Pill, Red-Eyes Black Dragon.

 

Key combos: Oviraptor plus Babycerasaurus starts the Dino engine into board presence; Miscellaneousaurus protects Dinosaur effects and summons Jurrac Aeolo; Double Evolution Pill turns graveyard setup into UCT; Carboneddon or Black Stone keeps Red-Eyes live without overloading the deck.

 

This is a competitive Link Evolution-era rebuild of Rex's Red-Eyed Dinosaurs: the Red-Eyes cards are here for the anime signal, while the Dinosaur engine carries the games.

deck_name: Rex Raptor - Evolzar Dino Control [Low Rogue]
deck_excerpt: LOW ROGUE BUILD - Evoltile and Evolsaur cards upgraded with modern Dino consistency to make Dolkka, Laggia, and Evo-Singularity matter.
deck_description:
Power: Low Rogue - stronger than pure Evolsaur, but still more theme-bound than the streamlined Dinosaur build.

 

Consistency: Strong; Fossil Dig, Evo-Diversity, Oviraptor, Miscellaneousaurus, and the Babycerasaurus package help the deck find both Dino starters and Evol access.

 

Ceiling: Strong; Evolzar Dolkka and Evolzar Laggia give real interaction, Evo-Singularity can convert graveyard setup into surprise Xyz pressure, and UCT gives the deck a clean finisher.

 

Recovery: Solid; Oviraptor, Giant Rex, Lost World, and Evo-Singularity provide follow-up, though hands built around Evoltile pieces can run out of pressure if the first Xyz is answered.

 

Going first/second: Prefers going first to land Dolkka/Laggia or set Evo-Singularity. Going second relies on UCT, Pankratops, and side-deck cards to force through boards.

 

Choke points: Stop Evo-Force, Evo-Diversity, Souleating Oviraptor, or Babycerasaurus triggers; banishing key graveyard material weakens Evo-Singularity and Double Evolution Pill.

 

Key cards: Evolzar Dolkka, Evolzar Laggia, Evoltile Najasho, Evo-Force, Evo-Diversity, Evo-Singularity, Souleating Oviraptor, Ultimate Conductor Tyranno.

 

Key combos: Najasho plus Evo-Force summons Evolsaur bodies into Rank 4 Evolzars; Oviraptor plus Babycerasaurus supplies extra Dinosaur bodies; Evo-Singularity turns an Evoltile and Evolsaur in grave into Dolkka, Laggia, or Solda at trap speed.

 

This is the Rex challenge-deck upgrade: still recognizably Rise of the Evolsaur, but tuned so the Evolzar bosses show up often enough to matter in casual rogue games.

deck_name: Rafael - Guardian Eatos Noble Arms [Low Rogue]
deck_excerpt: LOW ROGUE BUILD - Rafael's Guardian Eatos deck rebuilt with a Noble Arms equip-control shell for casual rogue tables.
deck_description:
Power: Low Rogue - a functional equip-control Rafael deck, but still more identity-bound than a pure Noble Knight list.

 

Consistency: Solid; Heritage of the Chalice, Glory of the Noble Knights, Medraut, Borz, Connector, and Reinforcement of the Army give the deck real starter density.

 

Ceiling: Strong for casual rogue play; Isolde and the Noble Knight Xyz package create pressure while Guardian Eatos, Seal of Orichalcos, and the Guardian equips keep Rafael's anime identity visible.

 

Recovery: Solid; Noble Arms re-equip naturally, Gwenhwyfar gives extra grind, and Heritage/Glory help rebuild after removal.

 

Going first/second: Usually wants to go first to establish equipped bodies or Isolde access, but it can go second by forcing trades with Noble Arms, Eatos pressure, and side-deck board breakers.

 

Choke points: Stop Medraut or Borz before equips resolve; remove Isolde before it converts equips into material; backrow removal can break Noble Arms loops; Seal of Orichalcos should usually be held until after Extra Deck plays.

 

Key cards: Guardian Eatos, Guardian Dreadscythe, The Seal of Orichalcos, Heritage of the Chalice, Noble Knight Medraut, Noble Knight Borz, Isolde, Two Tales of the Noble Knights.

 

Key combos: Medraut plus a Noble Arms equip starts the Noble Knight engine; Borz loads or searches key equips; Connector into Aqua Dolphin gives hand knowledge and Isolde access; Guardian Eatos can apply early pressure when the graveyard is clear, while Seal of Orichalcos turns established boards into bigger threats.

 

This is a Link Evolution-era upgrade of Rafael's Guardians of Orichalchos: not pure Guardian nostalgia, but a stronger equip-warrior shell that still feels like Rafael.

deck_excerpt: LOW ROGUE BUILD - Rafael's Super Soldier challenge deck upgraded with Impcantation search and Chaos pressure.
deck_description:
Power: Low Rogue - stronger and smoother than the original challenge deck, but still constrained by Super Soldier ritual requirements.

 

Consistency: Strong; Manju, Gateway to Chaos, Impcantation Chalislime, Candoll, Talismandra, Super Soldier Synthesis, and Allure of Darkness all help find the correct ritual pieces.

 

Ceiling: Strong for casual rogue play; Super Soldier backed by Beginning Knight and Evening Twilight Knight can banish, attack, and pressure life quickly, while Envoy of the Beginning and Soldier of Chaos add Chaos finishers.

 

Recovery: Medium; Impcantations and ritual spells can rebuild, but losing the first ritual push without a follow-up can leave the deck resource-light.

 

Going first/second: Better going second or into simplified boards, but can go first with hand knowledge, follow-up ritual setup, and side-deck interruption.

 

Choke points: Stop Manju, Gateway to Chaos, Chalislime, or the first ritual spell; banish key Light/Dark materials; disrupt the graveyard before Beginning Knight and Evening Twilight Knight grant bonus effects.

 

Key cards: Black Luster Soldier - Super Soldier, Beginning Knight, Evening Twilight Knight, Manju of the Ten Thousand Hands, Gateway to Chaos, Super Soldier Synthesis, Impcantation Chalislime, Black Luster Soldier - Envoy of the Beginning.

 

Key combos: Chalislime discards a ritual piece to summon Candoll or Talismandra; Gateway and Manju find missing BLS pieces; Super Soldier Synthesis uses exact Light/Dark materials; Beginning Knight and Evening Twilight Knight give Super Soldier extra removal and battle pressure.

 

This version keeps Rafael's The Super Soldier identity while giving it enough search density to play like a real Link Evolution-era ritual deck.

deck_name: PaniK - Dark Illusion Lair Virus [Low Rogue]
deck_excerpt: LOW ROGUE BUILD - PaniK's dark castle control deck revised with Super Poly, Instant Fusion, and a real Field Spell package.
deck_description:
Power: Low Rogue - a stronger PaniK-style control deck using Lair of Darkness and Virus traps, now revised with real Fusion access and a cleaner Field Spell package.

 

Consistency: Solid; Ahrima, Terraforming, Set Rotation, Metaverse, Lair of Darkness, Lilith, Tour Guide, and Allure of Darkness give the deck several ways to find engines and pressure pieces.

 

Ceiling: Strong for casual rogue play; Lair turns opposing monsters into tribute fuel, Lilith finds trap pressure, Virus cards can strip hands or boards, and Super Polymerization turns DARK-heavy boards into Fusion removal.

 

Recovery: Medium; Diabolos recurs naturally, Burning Abyss cards replace themselves, Lair generates tokens, and Instant Fusion/Verte give the Extra Deck more practical access than the first version had.

 

Going first/second: Prefers going first to establish Lair plus traps, but can now go second more credibly with Super Polymerization, Instant Fusion lines, and side-deck board breakers.

 

Choke points: Remove Lair before Virus traps resolve; stop Lilith before it sets the key trap; interrupt Tour Guide before the Rank 3 line; stop Verte before it sends Super Polymerization; banish Diabolos instead of destroying it.

 

Key cards: Castle of Dark Illusions, Lair of Darkness, Yami, Set Rotation, Lilith, Lady of Lament, Darkest Diabolos, Lord of the Lair, Super Polymerization, Instant Fusion, Predaplant Verte Anaconda, Deck Devastation Virus, Eradicator Epidemic Virus.

 

Key combos: Ahrima or Terraforming finds Lair; Set Rotation can set Lair while using Yami as the backup PaniK field; Lilith under Lair tributes an opponent's monster to set a Virus trap; Tour Guide starts the Burning Abyss Rank 3 package; Verte converts spare monsters into Super Polymerization access; Instant Fusion can put Barox or another low-Level Fusion on board for pressure or material.

 

This is the revised Link Evolution-era upgrade of PaniK's The Dark Illusion deck: the old castle-and-shadows identity remains, but the Fusion and Field Spell pieces now actually support the game plan.Power: TEST EDITOR SYNC

deck_excerpt: LOW ROGUE BUILD - PaniK's Pumprincess stall deck upgraded with Zombie World, Balerdroch, and pressure traps.
deck_description:
Power: Low Rogue - a functional Zombie World control deck with a Pumprincess stall-burn package, stronger than the original challenge list but still identity-bound.

 

Consistency: Solid; Uni-Zombie, Shiranui Solitaire, Gozuki, Necroworld Banshee, Foolish Burial, Terraforming, and One for One all help reach Zombie World or the graveyard setup.

 

Ceiling: Strong for casual rogue play; Zombie World plus Doomking Balerdroch gives recurring interruption, while Rivalry of Warlords can lock many boards under the right conditions.

 

Recovery: Strong; Mezuki, Doomking, Glow-Up Bloom, Zombie Master, Jack-o-Bolan, and Vampire Sucker let the deck keep rebuilding after trades.

 

Going first/second: Usually wants to go first to establish Zombie World, Rivalry, or Balerdroch, but it can go second into slower boards with Zombie recursion and side-deck breakers.

 

Choke points: Stop Uni-Zombie or Shiranui Solitaire before the graveyard is loaded; remove Zombie World before Rivalry matters; banish Doomking or Mezuki; clear Pumprincess before it turns combat math awkward.

 

Key cards: Pumprincess the Princess of Ghosts, Zombie World, Doomking Balerdroch, Necroworld Banshee, Glow-Up Bloom, Uni-Zombie, Mezuki, Rivalry of Warlords, Vampire Sucker.

 

Key combos: Solitaire summons Uni-Zombie to load Banshee or Mezuki; Banshee turns on Zombie World from the graveyard; Glow-Up Bloom finds Doomking while Zombie World is active; Zombie World plus Rivalry restricts many opposing boards; Pumprincess adds the slow haunted-house pressure that keeps the PaniK theme intact.

 

This is a Link Evolution-era upgrade of Tricks of Pumprincess: it keeps the haunted stall flavor while giving the deck a real Zombie World control backbone.

Mikumari the Sealing Deity Ashtra searches for Yoa the Plague Deity Ashtra.

Yoa searches for Ashtrashen - Gateway to the Worlds Beyond or Ashtra Cursed Roar.

Cursed Roar can special summon itself as a level 1 tuner, then it allows you to synchro summon. You can use this effect to synchro summon on your opponent's turn. Alternatively, you can activate its destruction effect if you control an Ashtra monster or have 1 in your graveyard.

One of the best level 8 synchros you can summon is Adamancipator Risen - Dragite, which can negate your opponent's spell/trap or effect if you have a water monster in your graveyard. Kashtira Ogre and Mikumari will satisfy this activation condition.

deck_excerpt: LOW ROGUE BUILD - Odion's Temple of the Kings trap-monster deck uses Serket, Waking the Dragon, and reactive traps.
deck_description:
Power: Low Rogue - an Odion-style trap-monster control deck built around Temple of the Kings, Mystical Beast of Serket, and punishing backrow pressure.

 

Consistency: Solid; Temple of the Kings, Pot of Extravagance, Pot of Duality, Card of Demise, Trap Trick, and A Cat of Ill Omen help the deck find its engine or the right trap for the matchup.

 

Ceiling: High for casual rogue tables; Temple plus Serket can cheat out Naturia Exterio or The Last Warrior from Another Planet, while Waking the Dragon threatens huge punishments if opponents blindly clear backrow.

 

Recovery: Medium; trap monsters keep bodies on board, Paleozoic Dinomischus trades cleanly, and the Extra Deck gives finishing power, but the deck still depends on keeping enough backrow alive.

 

Going first/second: Prefers going first to set Temple, trap monsters, and counter traps. Going second is supported by Sphere Mode, Dark Ruler No More, Evenly Matched, and Twin Twisters from the side.

 

Choke points: Remove Temple before Serket resolves; avoid destroying unknown backrow into Waking the Dragon; stop Trap Trick before it finds the exact answer; pressure the deck before Card of Demise and trap monsters can stabilize.

 

Key cards: Temple of the Kings, Mystical Beast of Serket, Waking the Dragon, Statue of Anguish Pattern, The Phantom Knights of Shade Brigandine, Quantum Cat, Swamp Mirrorer, Trap Trick, Solemn Strike.

 

Key combos: Temple of the Kings lets Odion activate a trap the turn it is set; Temple plus Serket sends both to summon Naturia Exterio or The Last Warrior; trap monsters become bodies for Link plays; Statue of Anguish Pattern turns each trap monster into removal.

 

This is a Link Evolution-era upgrade of Odion's Temple of the Kings deck: the old Serket and living-trap identity stays, but the deck now has real control tools and explosive punishment cards.

deck_excerpt: HIGH ROGUE BUILD - Odion's Challenge Deck rebuilt as Necrovalley stun with Royal Tribute, Tombs, and Supernaturalist.
deck_description:
Power: High Rogue - a serious Odion Challenge Deck upgrade using Necrovalley, Gravekeeper search power, Imperial Tombs, and anti-graveyard pressure.

 

Consistency: Strong; Gravekeeper's Commandant, Necrovalley Throne, Pot of Extravagance, Pot of Duality, Recruiter, and Headman give the deck reliable access to Necrovalley and its follow-up pieces.

 

Ceiling: High; Necrovalley shuts off graveyard strategies, Royal Tribute can rip monster-heavy hands apart, Imperial Tombs protects established boards, and Supernaturalist searches or protects the field setup.

 

Recovery: Solid; Recruiter replaces itself, Headman revives key Gravekeepers, Gravekeeper's Stele recovers names, and Supernaturalist keeps the Necrovalley engine moving in longer games.

 

Going first/second: Stronger going first with Necrovalley plus traps, but the side deck gives going-second tools through Dark Ruler No More, Evenly Matched, Cosmic Cyclone, and Super Polymerization.

 

Choke points: Stop Commandant or Throne before Necrovalley is online; remove Necrovalley before Imperial Tombs becomes live; negate Spiritualist before Supernaturalist is made; force traps before Royal Tribute or Rivalry can lock the game.

 

Key cards: Necrovalley, Gravekeeper's Commandant, Necrovalley Throne, Gravekeeper's Spiritualist, Gravekeeper's Supernaturalist, Royal Tribute, Imperial Tombs of Necrovalley, Necrovalley Temple, Rivalry of Warlords.

 

Key combos: Commandant searches Necrovalley; Throne searches the missing Gravekeeper or grants an extra Normal Summon; Spiritualist fuses into Supernaturalist while Necrovalley is active; Supernaturalist searches Tombs, Temple, or follow-up; Necrovalley plus Royal Tribute can immediately decide slower games.

 

This is the competitive version of Odion's Gravekeeper challenge deck: it keeps the tomb-guardian identity, but upgrades it into a focused anti-meta Necrovalley control strategy.

deck_excerpt: LOW ROGUE BUILD - Pegasus' classic cartoon deck upgraded with Toon Kingdom, Red-Eyes Toon lines, and a Bickuribox nod.
deck_description:
Power: Low Rogue - a classic Pegasus Toon upgrade that keeps the Car-tooned/Bickuribox flavor while using Toon Kingdom and Red-Eyes Toon Dragon to make the deck actually function.

 

Consistency: Solid; The Black Stone of Legend, Red-Eyes Toon Dragon, Toon Dark Magician, Toon Table of Contents, Toon Kingdom, Comic Hand, Pot-style draw spells, and Terraforming give the deck multiple ways to reach its Toon engine.

 

Ceiling: Good for casual rogue tables; protected Toon Kingdom pressure, Comic Hand steals, Toon Briefcase disruption, and Toon Dark Magician access can steal games quickly once Kingdom sticks.

 

Recovery: Medium; Toon Kingdom protects bodies, Mimicat can recycle a payoff, and Red-Eyes Toon Dragon rebuilds presence, but losing Kingdom at the wrong time still hurts.

 

Going first/second: Prefers going first with Kingdom plus Briefcase, but can go second with Comic Hand, Toon Cyber Dragon, Super Polymerization from the side, and direct Toon damage.

 

Choke points: Remove Toon Kingdom before committing removal; stop Toon Table of Contents if it is the only access point; answer Red-Eyes Toon Dragon before it snowballs; clear Comic Hand targets before Pegasus can turn them against you.

 

Key cards: Toon Kingdom, Toon Table of Contents, Red-Eyes Toon Dragon, Toon Dark Magician, Comic Hand, Toon Briefcase, The Black Stone of Legend, Bickuribox.

 

Key combos: Black Stone summons Red-Eyes Toon Dragon; Red-Eyes Toon Dragon summons Toon Dark Magician; Toon Dark Magician accesses more Toon pressure; Toon Table finds Kingdom or Comic Hand; Polymerization keeps Bickuribox as the source-deck showpiece.

 

This is the Link Evolution-era version of Car-tooned: stronger, smoother, and still unmistakably Pegasus.

deck_excerpt: LOW ROGUE BUILD - Relinquished and Millennium-Eyes control backed by a small Toon World package.
deck_description:
Power: Low Rogue - a Pegasus control deck combining Relinquished ritual pressure, Millennium-Eyes Restrict interaction, and a compact Toon World package.

 

Consistency: Solid; Manju, Impcantation Candoll, Impcantation Talismandra, Pre-Preparation of Rites, Preparation of Rites, and Toon Table of Contents give the deck many ways to reach its ritual or Toon pieces.

 

Ceiling: Strong for casual rogue games; Relinquished absorbs problem monsters, Millennium-Eyes Restrict punishes hand traps and graveyard monster effects, and Toon Kingdom gives Pegasus a protected pressure plan.

 

Recovery: Medium; Preparation recycles Relinquished, Relinquished Fusion extends interaction, and Illusionist Faceless Magician helps recover Restrict pieces, but the deck can still run out if its ritual engine is interrupted repeatedly.

 

Going first/second: Flexible; going first aims to establish Millennium-Eyes or Relinquished pressure, while going second can absorb or steal monsters and side into board breakers.

 

Choke points: Stop Manju or Pre-Preparation before the ritual line is complete; banish Relinquished Fusion targets; remove Toon Kingdom before the Toon bodies matter; force Millennium-Eyes before committing key monster effects.

 

Key cards: Relinquished, Black Illusion Ritual, Millennium-Eyes Restrict, Thousand-Eyes Restrict, Relinquished Fusion, Millennium-Eyes Illusionist, Toon Kingdom, Toon Table of Contents.

 

Key combos: Manju searches the missing ritual piece; Impcantations assemble Relinquished without spending the normal summon; Relinquished Fusion makes Millennium-Eyes Restrict; Instant Fusion gives quick access to Thousand-Eyes or Millennium-Eyes; Toon Table moves the deck into Kingdom pressure.

 

This is the Link Evolution-era version of Entering Toon World: Pegasus still hypnotizes and absorbs monsters, but the deck has enough search power to do it consistently.

deck_excerpt: LOW ROGUE BUILD - the cleanest Pegasus Toon build, using Red-Eyes Toon Dragon, Toon Dark Magician, Kingdom, and Comic Hand.
deck_description:
Power: Low Rogue - the strongest Toon-focused Pegasus build here, centered on Red-Eyes Toon Dragon, Toon Dark Magician, Toon Kingdom, Comic Hand, and direct-damage pressure.

 

Consistency: Strong for Toons; The Black Stone of Legend, Toon Table of Contents, Toon Kingdom, Mimicat, Red-Eyes Toon Dragon, Toon Dark Magician, and Terraforming all help the deck reach its main plan.

 

Ceiling: Good; protected Toon monsters can attack directly, Comic Hand turns opposing bosses into Pegasus's monsters, Mimicat steals value from the opponent's graveyard, and Rank 7/Link options give the deck extra finishing routes.

 

Recovery: Medium; Red-Eyes Toon Dragon and Toon Dark Magician rebuild boards, Mimicat recovers stolen value, and Kingdom protection makes removal awkward, but Kingdom removal remains the biggest weakness.

 

Going first/second: Wants to establish Kingdom and Toon Briefcase going first, but can pressure second with Comic Hand, Toon Cyber Dragon Extra Deck lines, Mimicat, and side-deck board breakers.

 

Choke points: Remove or negate Toon Kingdom; stop The Black Stone of Legend before Red-Eyes Toon Dragon lands; interrupt Toon Dark Magician before it summons or searches; avoid leaving premium monsters for Comic Hand.

 

Key cards: Red-Eyes Toon Dragon, Toon Dark Magician, Toon Kingdom, Toon Table of Contents, Comic Hand, Mimicat, Toon Briefcase, The Black Stone of Legend.

 

Key combos: Black Stone summons Red-Eyes Toon Dragon; Red-Eyes Toon Dragon summons Toon Dark Magician; Toon Dark Magician searches Comic Hand or summons another Toon; Kingdom protects the board; direct attacks close games before slower decks stabilize.

 

This is the clean Link Evolution-era Return to Toon World build: less nostalgia filler, more actual Toon pressure.

deck_excerpt: HIGH ROGUE BUILD - Pegasus' Madolche challenge deck upgraded into pre-Salon Petingcessoeur combo with Tiaramisu pressure.
deck_description:
Power: High Rogue - a strong Link Evolution-era Madolche combo deck using Petingcessoeur, Anjelly, Hootcake, Messengelato, Chateau, Ticket, and Queen Tiaramisu.

 

Consistency: Strong; Magileine, Anjelly, Petingcessoeur, Hootcake, Chateau, Ticket, Terraforming, and PSY-Framegear Gamma give the deck reliable starters and protection for its first push.

 

Ceiling: High; Queen Tiaramisu provides non-targeting shuffle removal, Teacher Glassouffle protects and recycles, Chocolat-a-la-Mode extends pressure, and Fresh Sistart protects the Madolche spell/trap setup.

 

Recovery: Strong; Chateau and Ticket recycle monsters, Glassouffle puts resources back, and Madolche monsters naturally return to the deck instead of staying spent in the graveyard.

 

Going first/second: Can build a protected resource loop going first, but is also comfortable going second with Tiaramisu removal, Raigeki, Mind Control, Evenly Matched, Dark Ruler No More, and Twin Twisters from the side.

 

Choke points: Stop Anjelly or Petingcessoeur before the engine reaches Hootcake/Messengelato; remove Chateau before recycling starts; interrupt Tiaramisu before it shuffles cards; use graveyard disruption carefully because Madolche can benefit from clean grave states.

 

Key cards: Madolche Petingcessoeur, Madolche Anjelly, Madolche Magileine, Madolche Hootcake, Madolche Messengelato, Madolche Chateau, Madolche Ticket, Madolche Queen Tiaramisu, Madolche Teacher Glassouffle.

 

Key combos: Magileine searches Anjelly or Petingcessoeur; Anjelly summons Hootcake; Hootcake summons Messengelato; Messengelato searches Chateau or Ticket; Petingcessoeur makes the Rank 4 Madolche line; Tiaramisu shuffles opposing cards and opens direct pressure.

 

This is the competitive Pegasus Challenge Deck version: still sweet, still theatrical, but much closer to a real rogue combo deck.
Been testing at my locals as a fun deck and it can be surprising match up.

Mitsurugi Kashtira Horus is a powerful competitive deck that combines the consistency of Horus, the board control of Kashtira, and the resilience of Mitsurugi. It can establish strong first-turn boards, break through interruptions going second, and maintain excellent follow-up with its overlapping engines. With access to powerful Rank 8 Xyz Monsters and multiple forms of disruption, the deck offers consistency, flexibility, and the ability to compete with the top decks of the current format.

deck_name: Mako Tsunami - Sea Stealth Fisherman [Low Rogue]
deck_excerpt: LOW ROGUE BUILD - Mako's Legendary Ocean deck rebuilt around Fisherman, Citadel Whale, Sea Stealth Attack, and WATER control.
deck_description:
Power: Low Rogue - a Mako-style WATER control deck using A Legendary Ocean, The Legendary Fisherman II/III, Citadel Whale, Sea Stealth Attack, and reactive trap pressure.

 

Consistency: Solid; Warrior of Atlantis searches A Legendary Ocean, Pot of Extravagance and Pot of Duality dig for setup, and the deck has multiple WATER bodies that work under the field spell.

 

Ceiling: Strong for casual rogue tables; Sea Stealth Attack protects WATER monsters and turns battle into removal, Citadel Whale searches the trap, and Fisherman III can punish slower monster-heavy boards.

 

Recovery: Medium; Salvage and Moray of Greed help recycle or convert WATER monsters, Sea Stealth Attack can protect key pieces, and Extra Deck links/Rank 4s give fallback pressure.

 

Going first/second: Prefers going first with A Legendary Ocean plus Sea Stealth Attack or floodgate pressure, but can go second with Gameciel, Dark Ruler No More, Evenly Matched, and Twin Twisters from the side.

 

Choke points: Remove A Legendary Ocean before the level-reduction and WATER pressure matter; stop Citadel Whale before it finds Sea Stealth Attack; force Sea Stealth Attack early; remove Barrier Statue or Fisherman before the control shell locks tempo.

 

Key cards: A Legendary Ocean, Warrior of Atlantis, The Legendary Fisherman II, The Legendary Fisherman III, Citadel Whale, Sea Stealth Attack, White Howling, Torrential Tribute, Gameciel, the Sea Turtle Kaiju.

 

Key combos: Warrior of Atlantis searches A Legendary Ocean; Citadel Whale searches Sea Stealth Attack; Sea Stealth Attack protects WATER monsters and banishes them to dodge removal; A Legendary Ocean makes Fisherman and WATER bodies easier to deploy.

 

This is the Link Evolution-era upgrade of Mako's Legendary Ocean deck: still a sea-duelist identity build, but with real control pressure instead of mostly vanilla sea monsters.

deck_excerpt: LOW ROGUE BUILD - Mako's Gishki challenge deck upgraded into Zielgigas ritual combo with Salvage and Rank 10 finishers.
deck_description:
Power: Low Rogue - a Gishki ritual combo deck built around Gishki Zielgigas, Gishki Aquamirror, Shadow/Vision search lines, Salvage loops, and Rank 10 pressure.

 

Consistency: Strong for an older ritual shell; Gishki Abyss, Gishki Shadow, Gishki Vision, Beautunaful Princess, Preparation of Rites, Salvage, and Impcantation pieces give the deck many ways to assemble ritual access.

 

Ceiling: Good; Zielgigas can draw and clear cards, multiple Level 10 bodies enable Gustav Max/Juggernaut Liebe lines, and Gustkraken or Mind Augus add disruption and recursion angles.

 

Recovery: Solid; Gishki Aquamirror recycles itself, Salvage returns WATER searchers, Zielgigas draws into follow-up, and the deck can rebuild ritual access after the first push.

 

Going first/second: Flexible but happier going second, where Zielgigas pressure, Rank 10 finishers, and side-deck board breakers can crack established fields. Going first usually aims for resource setup rather than a hard lock.

 

Choke points: Stop Gishki Abyss before it searches; banish or negate Gishki Aquamirror; interrupt Shadow or Vision before they replace themselves; stop Salvage after the first searcher exchange; remove Zielgigas before it converts into Rank 10 pressure.

 

Key cards: Gishki Zielgigas, Gishki Aquamirror, Gishki Abyss, Gishki Shadow, Gishki Vision, Beautunaful Princess, Salvage, Preparation of Rites, Impcantation Chalislime.

 

Key combos: Beautunaful Princess summons Gishki Abyss; Abyss searches Shadow or Vision; Shadow searches Aquamirror; Vision searches Zielgigas; Aquamirror ritual summons Zielgigas; repeated Level 10 bodies turn into Gustav Max, Super Dora, or Juggernaut Liebe.

 

This is the Link Evolution-era upgrade of Mako's Ritual of the Gishki challenge deck: still a WATER ritual deck, but far more focused on reliable Zielgigas pressure.

deck_name: Mai Valentine - Harpie Hunting Ground [Low Rogue]

deck_excerpt: LOW ROGUE - Link Evolution Gearfried Warriors using Isolde, Red-Eyes Fusion, and equip utility.

deck_name: Joey Wheeler - Lord of the Red Ritual [Low Rogue]
deck_excerpt: LOW ROGUE - Lord of the Red ritual control using Red-Eyes names and Impcantation support.
deck_description:

deck_excerpt: While it's hot IRL with record breaking heatwaves... It's also about to become an INFERNO in ladder with Fire King!
deck_description:
While it's hot IRL with record breaking heatwaves... It's also about to become an INFERNO in ladder with Fire King!

Simplify your opponent's gamestate with 24 handtraps then purify them with your phoenix flames! While the pure Fire King may seem deceptively simple and therefore, weak - it is surprisingly potent in Masterduel's meta. Nearly every card triggers floating effects either from hand, deck or GY. Special summons can be played minimally in this deck to combat Maxx C/Mulcharmies or the deck can combo off with Promethean Princess to a Fire Link 4 fairly competently for a more powerful board. High impact handtraps like Nibiru and Droll barely affect the gameplan of the Fire King strategy since they can be played "low-to-the-ground" while advancing their gamestate. Fire Kings can also generate a ton of advantage and ATK power extremely quickly. The deck also has natural ways to combat the ever common backrow floodgates through Fire King Sky Burn. Fire King monsters on the field can also reliably dodge targeting effects because quick effect in-archetype cards like Sky Burn, Circle of Fire Kings and High Avatar Kirin all destroy your monsters.

Piloted correctly, Fire Kings basically has no "bad match-ups" in today's metagame, since it basically has answers to everything. Played "pure," Fire Kings might not have as much power as Elfnote, Branded, Maliss, or Mitsurugi, but it makes up for it with its versatility, adaptability and grindgame. All in all, Fire Kings played as a "pure" deck remains a solid meta choice for Masterduel ladder! 

GLHF Duelists!

Want to see the deck in action? Scroll to the very bottom for the Youtube video!
