deck_excerpt: There's more than one powerful Winged-Beast deck in Edison. Bring on the Harpies!
deck_description:
DARK-Winged Harpies

There are a few different ways to build Harpies in Edison, but this version has been the strongest for me so far. It's still very much a work in progress, and the side deck is mostly a collection of cards I'm occasionally testing, including a more chainable Spell/Trap package to minimize collateral damage. Even so, the deck has proven to be very powerful, with access to both explosive turns and strong control plays. It can brick occasionally and sometimes gets outpaced by faster decks, but it definitely has the tools to compete.

If you try the deck let me know how it goes, and if you have suggestions just leave a comment. You can also add me on Twitter/X to chat about the deck. My handle is @VeeMerk

  • The Harpie package is kept fairly lean, giving you access to all of your key Harpie cards while boosting ATK and supporting a copy of Hysteric Party.
  • The Blackwing package acts as a second engine. Special Summoning Sirocco is especially important for loading DARK monsters into the GY for Dark Simorgh while also keeping Icarus Attack live. Gale is also an incredible one-card answer to a wide range of threats.
  • Hunter Owl has been one of the biggest surprises. Running three copies has felt fantastic, since having two on the field creates an attack lock while both get to 2000 ATK.
  • The real star of the deck, though, is Swallow's Nest. It's live with almost every monster you play and opens up so many lines. That's also why I run two copies each of Raiza and Dark Simorgh. Swallow's Nest lets you special summon into another copy of either monster in a pickle, making the deck far more resilient than it looks at first glance.
A quick brew of what Ars Magna might be like when it releases. Mostly a brainstorm, ratios could probably use some work.

deck_excerpt: HIGH ROGUE BUILD - Orichalcos-backed True Draco stun with Master Peace, triple Diagram, no Extra Deck, and legal floodgate control.
deck_description:
Power: High Rogue (8.4/10) - This is a much stronger legal version of the Dartz idea: The Seal of Orichalcos becomes the character-defining field spell, while True Draco supplies the actual competitive control shell. Current-list legal Master Peace and triple Dragonic Diagram raise the ceiling sharply, and the empty Extra Deck keeps The Monarchs Erupt live. It stays below top-tier because it is still a trap-heavy stun deck that can lose tempo going second and has to manage field-spell overlap between Diagram and Orichalcos.

 

Consistency: Very strong. Dragonic Diagram, Terraforming, True Draco Heritage, Disciples of the True Dracophoenix, Pot of Duality, and Card of Demise give the deck reliable access to a Draco monster plus tribute fodder. The Seal of Orichalcos is kept at two copies so the Dartz identity shows up often without crowding out Diagram.

 

Ceiling: High. Master Peace backed by The Monarchs Erupt, Skill Drain, Rivalry of Warlords, Lose 1 Turn, True Draco Apocalypse, and True King's Return can lock many casual and rogue boards out of meaningful play. The Seal of Orichalcos adds pressure and protection while keeping the deck committed to its no-Extra-Deck plan.

 

Recovery: Strong. Heritage and Disciples turn used continuous cards into draws, Disciples recycles True Draco cards, and True King's Return keeps Tribute Summons coming on the opponent's turn. Diagram also converts spent cards into fresh Draco access.

 

Going first/second: Best going first. Open Diagram or a Draco spell/trap, Tribute Summon Ignis, Majesty, Dinomight, or Master Peace, then layer Erupt, Skill Drain, Apocalypse, Return, Rivalry, Lose 1 Turn, or Judgment. Going second, the side deck brings Dark Ruler No More, Evenly Matched, Twin Twisters, Harpie's Feather Duster, Inspector Boarder, and Lancea.

 

Choke points: Remove Dragonic Diagram before it searches, stop the first True Draco Tribute Summon, clear The Monarchs Erupt before committing monster effects, or force Master Peace to be summoned with fewer protection types. Back-row removal is strongest if it hits Apocalypse, Return, or the floodgate before they can be tributed for value.

 

Key cards: The Seal of Orichalcos, Dragonic Diagram, Master Peace, the True Dracoslaying King, Ignis Heat, Majesty Maiden, Dinomight Knight, True Draco Heritage, Disciples of the True Dracophoenix, True Draco Apocalypse, True King's Return, The Monarchs Erupt, Skill Drain, Rivalry of Warlords, Lose 1 Turn.

 

Key combos: Diagram destroys a True Draco spell or trap to search a Draco monster, while the destroyed card removes an opposing card. Heritage or Disciples can be tributed for a Draco summon, then replace themselves with draws or recursion. Return revives a Draco and enables an opponent-turn Tribute Summon. Apocalypse halves an opposing monster and can be tributed for removal. Master Peace becomes difficult to answer when summoned using both spell and trap cards, especially behind Erupt or Skill Drain. Orichalcos turns the whole board into a blunt Dartz-style pressure plan without needing the Extra Deck.

deck_name: Dartz - Serene Aroma Plant Control [Low Rogue]
deck_excerpt: LOW ROGUE BUILD - Link Evolution Aromage Plant control with Jasmine, Gardening, Winds traps, and legal side-deck breakers.
deck_description:
Power: Low Rogue (6.8/10) - This is a cleaner and fully current-legal Link Evolution Aroma build. It keeps Dartz's serene-control angle through life gain, garden fields, and Plant recursion, but it stays Low Rogue because Aroma still needs several pieces online before it starts snowballing and it lacks the later Plant/Aroma support that would raise the ceiling.

 

Consistency: Solid. Aromage Jasmine, Aromage Laurel, Lonefire Blossom, Aroma Gardening, Aroma Garden, Super Solar Nutrient, One for One, and Monster Reborn give the deck multiple ways to establish Plant bodies and life-gain triggers. Moving Gardening to three and trimming the weaker beatdown cards makes the opener less clunky.

 

Ceiling: Moderate. The strongest boards use Aromaseraphy Jasmine to convert Plants into searches, Blessed Winds and Humid Winds to keep the engine looping, Dried Winds to turn life gain into removal, and Synchro/Link options such as Sweet Marjoram, Naturia Beast, F.A. Dawn Dragster, Black Rose Dragon, Crossrose Dragon, and Borrelsword Dragon.

 

Recovery: Good. Blessed Winds recycles and revives Aroma pieces, Humid Winds can search follow-up, Rose Lover and Monster Reborn bring back Plants, and Aroma Garden/Gardening keep generating life-gain triggers after trades. The deck is much weaker if its continuous spells/traps are removed before they resolve value.

 

Going first/second: Best going first. Set up Aroma Garden or Gardening with Jasmine, then layer Blessed Winds, Dried Winds, Humid Winds, Infinite Impermanence, or hand traps. Going second, the side deck brings Dark Ruler No More, Evenly Matched, Nibiru, Twin Twisters, Droll, Feather Duster, and Pankratops.

 

Choke points: Stop Lonefire Blossom or Aromaseraphy Jasmine before they search, remove Jasmine before the life-gain draw engine starts, hit Blessed Winds/Humid Winds with back-row removal, or banish Rose Lover and Spore before they become follow-up. Decks that can clear continuous cards cleanly are the hardest matchups.

 

Key cards: Aromage Jasmine, Aromage Laurel, Aromage Marjoram, Lonefire Blossom, Aroma Garden, Aroma Gardening, Blessed Winds, Dried Winds, Humid Winds, Aromaseraphy Jasmine, Aromaseraphy Sweet Marjoram, One for One, Super Solar Nutrient, Rose Lover, Spore.

 

Key combos: Lonefire Blossom turns into Jasmine, Laurel, or another Plant starter. One for One summons Laurel or Spore to make Aromaseraphy Jasmine lines. Aroma Garden or Gardening gains LP, triggering Jasmine draws and Aroma effects. Blessed Winds sends or revives Aroma cards while also gaining LP. Dried Winds turns those LP gains into monster removal. Aromaseraphy Jasmine tributes a Plant to search the missing Plant piece, then Sweet Marjoram and the Winds traps turn the board into a grindy control setup.

deck_excerpt: The mixture of zombies, vampires and plants come alive!
LOW ROGUE BUILD - Vampire-flavored Zombie World control using searchable Vampire tools, Doomking pressure, and Super Polymerization removal.
deck_description:
Power: Low Rogue (6.9/10) - This is stronger and fully TCG-legal, but it stays Low Rogue because the Vampire package asks for extra setup compared with the cleaner Bonz Doomking build. The deck keeps its identity through Familiar, Retainer, Fraulein, Scarlet Scourge, Vampire Kingdom, Vampire's Desire, Vampire Domination, Vampire Sucker, and Dhampir Vampire Sheridan while still leaning on Zombie World as the competitive backbone.

 

Consistency: Medium-high. Shiranui Solitaire and Uni-Zombie are still the best starters, while Necroworld Banshee, Terraforming, Foolish Burial, One for One, Vampire Familiar, and Vampire Retainer give the deck several ways to reach either Zombie World or the Vampire engine. Book of Life was cut for cards that either interact immediately or advance the Vampire plan more cleanly.

 

Ceiling: Solid. The best games combine Zombie World with Doomking Balerdroch, Super Polymerization into Dragonecro Nethersoul Dragon, Vampire Domination as a searchable counter trap, and Vampire Sucker value from repeated Zombie revivals. Ash Blossom gives the deck real early interaction without leaving the Zombie theme.

 

Recovery: Good. Doomking returns while a Field Spell is active, Mezuki and Monster Reborn rebuild bodies, Jack-o-Bolan turns discards into revival pressure, and Familiar or Retainer can come back by sending Vampire cards. Vampire's Desire adds another searchable way to revive or set levels for Vampire lines.

 

Going first/second: Best going first with Zombie World, a loaded graveyard, Doomking, Vampire Domination, Rivalry, or Ash Blossom backing the setup. Going second, three Super Polymerization plus Zombie World can convert opposing monsters into Dragonecro, while the side deck brings Dark Ruler No More, Evenly Matched, Nibiru, Artifact Lancea, Twin Twisters, and Harpie's Feather Duster.

 

Choke points: Stop Solitaire or Uni-Zombie before Banshee and Bloom reach the grave, remove Zombie World before Doomking can return, banish Familiar or Retainer before they recycle themselves, or force Vampire Domination offline by clearing the Vampire monster. The Vampire cards are powerful once they stick, but they are more vulnerable to disruption than the pure Doomking shell.

 

Key cards: Zombie World, Doomking Balerdroch, Necroworld Banshee, Glow-Up Bloom, Uni-Zombie, Shiranui Solitaire, Vampire Familiar, Vampire Retainer, Vampire Fraulein, Vampire Scarlet Scourge, Vampire's Desire, Vampire Domination, Vampire Sucker, Dhampir Vampire Sheridan, Super Polymerization, Dragonecro Nethersoul Dragon.

 

Key combos: Solitaire summons Uni-Zombie, Uni-Zombie sends Banshee or Bloom, and Banshee turns on Zombie World so Bloom can reach Doomking. Familiar searches Vampire monsters, Retainer searches Vampire's Desire, Vampire Kingdom, or Vampire Domination, and both can revive themselves by sending Vampire cards. Zombie World plus Super Polymerization turns opposing monsters into Dragonecro material. Vampire's Desire can revive a Vampire or adjust levels for Dhampir Vampire Sheridan, giving the deck a more obvious Vampire payoff without abandoning the Zombie World control plan.

deck_excerpt: HIGH ROGUE BUILD - Bonz's strongest TCG-legal Zombie World control deck, looping Doomking with Super Polymerization and recursive Zombie pressure.
deck_description:
Power: High Rogue (8.0/10) - This remains Bonz's flagship build because Zombie World, Doomking Balerdroch, Super Polymerization, and recursive Zombie bodies form a genuinely powerful control shell. It is now fully TCG-legal with Called by the Grave and Rivalry of Warlords reduced to one copy each, while still staying inside the Yu-Gi-Oh! Legacy of the Duelist: Link Evolution card pool.

 

Consistency: High. Uni-Zombie, Shiranui Solitaire, Gozuki, Necroworld Banshee, Zombie World, Terraforming, Foolish Burial, One for One, and Pot of Extravagance give the deck many routes into Zombie World plus grave setup. Pot of Extravagance helps offset the loss of extra Rivalry copies without pulling the list away from its Zombie engine.

 

Ceiling: High for a character-identity control deck. The strongest openings establish Zombie World with Doomking Balerdroch live, then layer Ash Blossom, Infinite Impermanence, the single legal Rivalry, Super Polymerization, Vampire Sucker value, PSY-Framelord Omega, Shiranui Shogunsaga, or Borrelsword Dragon as follow-up.

 

Recovery: Very strong. Doomking returns every Standby Phase while a Field Spell is up, Mezuki revives key Zombies, Jack-o-Bolan turns discards into pressure, Monster Reborn extends, and Vampire Sucker converts Zombie revivals into advantage. Necroworld Banshee also makes Zombie World difficult to keep off the table for long.

 

Going first/second: Best going first by setting Zombie World, loading Doomking, and backing it with hand traps or traps. Going second, three Super Polymerization turn Zombie World into removal through Dragonecro Nethersoul Dragon, while Ash Blossom, Infinite Impermanence, Dark Ruler No More, Evenly Matched, Nibiru, Artifact Lancea, Twin Twisters, and Harpie's Feather Duster cover common problem boards.

 

Choke points: Stop Uni-Zombie or Shiranui Solitaire before the grave is loaded, banish Necroworld Banshee before it can protect Zombie World access, remove Zombie World before Doomking can return, or banish Doomking, Mezuki, and Glow-Up Bloom before they generate follow-up. Pot of Extravagance should also be answered early if you need to keep the Bonz player from finding the field-spell engine.

 

Key cards: Doomking Balerdroch, Zombie World, Necroworld Banshee, Glow-Up Bloom, Uni-Zombie, Shiranui Solitaire, Mezuki, Gozuki, Jack-o-Bolan, Vampire Sucker, Super Polymerization, Dragonecro Nethersoul Dragon, Rivalry of Warlords, Pot of Extravagance.

 

Key combos: Solitaire summons Uni-Zombie, Uni-Zombie sends Banshee or Bloom, and Banshee turns on Zombie World so Bloom can reach Doomking. Zombie World plus Doomking creates repeat negation or banish pressure whenever Zombie effects activate. Zombie World plus Super Polymerization turns opposing monsters into Dragonecro material. Jack-o-Bolan discards Zombies for setup, then revives during the opponent's turn to trigger Vampire Sucker or keep Doomking lines active. The single Rivalry is no longer the whole plan, but with Zombie World it can still lock many boards into awkward positions.

deck_excerpt: LOW ROGUE BUILD - Zorc-focused Ritual pressure with a cleaner Impcantation engine, Demise/Ruin backup lines, and legal board breakers.
deck_description:
Power: Low Rogue (6.8/10) - This is much stronger and fully TCG-legal, but it stays Low Rogue because Dark Master - Zorc is still a dice-based Ritual payoff rather than a reliable modern win condition. The identity is clearer now: Zorc is the headline boss, Impcantations are the engine, and the small Demise/Ruin package gives the deck a more practical backup plan without turning it into the separate Demise/Ruin build.

 

Consistency: Strong for a theme-first Ritual deck. Impcantation Chalislime is now maxed at three, Candoll and Talismandra find the missing Ritual half, Manju adds another normal-summon searcher, and Pre-Preparation of Rites can find either Zorc plus Contract with the Dark Master or the classic Demise/Ruin package. Preparation of Rites, Impcantation Inception, and Pot of Extravagance give the deck more ways to keep hands moving.

 

Ceiling: Moderate. Zorc can remove opposing monsters and threaten damage, Demise can clear boards when the hand lines up, Ruin gives a battle-focused follow-up body, and the Extra Deck offers legal Rank 4, Rank 8, and Link options once the Impcantation lock is no longer on the field. The ceiling is real, but it is less deterministic than the stronger Bonz Zombie World decks or the cleaner Demise/Ruin Ritual build.

 

Recovery: Medium-high. Impcantation Inception can recycle itself, Preparation of Rites can recover Ritual Spells, Pre-Preparation reloads named Ritual pairs, and Monster Reborn gives another way to reuse a boss after it trades. The deck still has to pace its Ritual summons carefully because Zorc and Demise both consume resources to hit the field.

 

Going first/second: Best going second into casual and non-meta boards, where Zorc, Demise, Lightning Storm, Dark Ruler No More, Evenly Matched, and Nibiru can simplify the game before the Ritual bodies attack. Going first, the deck leans on Ash Blossom, Called by the Grave, Bagooska, Hope Harbinger, and side cards rather than a true hard lock.

 

Choke points: Stop Chalislime, Candoll, Talismandra, or Manju before the missing Ritual piece is searched. Ash Pre-Preparation if the hand depends on a named Ritual pair. Remove Impcantation bodies before they become Ritual material, or interrupt the Ritual Summon before Zorc or Demise can apply pressure. Banishing Inception also hurts the deck's grind.

 

Key cards: Dark Master - Zorc, Contract with the Dark Master, Impcantation Chalislime, Impcantation Candoll, Impcantation Talismandra, Manju of the Ten Thousand Hands, Pre-Preparation of Rites, Preparation of Rites, Impcantation Inception, Demise, King of Armageddon, Ruin, Queen of Oblivion, Pot of Extravagance, Lightning Storm.

 

Key combos: Pre-Preparation searches Contract with the Dark Master plus Dark Master - Zorc. Chalislime discards a Ritual card to summon Candoll or Talismandra from the Deck, which searches the missing Ritual Spell or Ritual Monster. Candoll plus Talismandra gives both halves of a Ritual Summon while also supplying tribute material. Pre-Preparation can also pivot into End of the World plus Demise or Ruin when Zorc is not the right payoff. After the Impcantation bodies leave the field, Rank 4s like Bagooska, Dugares, Exciton, or Tornado Dragon and Rank 8s like Hope Harbinger, Heliopolis, or Dingirsu become useful follow-up tools.

deck_excerpt: LOW ROGUE BUILD - Demise and Ruin Ritual pressure with a cleaner Impcantation engine, stronger consistency, and legal board-breaking tools.
deck_description:
Power: Low Rogue (7.2/10) - This is a stronger, cleaner, fully TCG-legal version, but it stays Low Rogue because Demise/Ruin Ritual still needs assembled pieces and has a weaker going-first plan than true High Rogue decks. The identity is now sharper: Demise and Ruin are the payoff, Impcantations are the engine, and the list no longer leans on the Cosmo Queen and Advanced Ritual Art brick package.

 

Consistency: High for a theme-first Ritual deck. Impcantation Chalislime is now maxed at three, backed by Candoll, Talismandra, Manju, Pre-Preparation of Rites, Preparation of Rites, Cycle of the World, End of the World, and Impcantation Inception. Pot of Extravagance adds extra digging because the Extra Deck is useful but not central to the deck's main plan.

 

Ceiling: Moderate-high. Demise, Supreme King of Armageddon can clear boards, Ruin, Supreme Queen of Oblivion threatens heavy battle damage, the Level 4 Demise and Ruin Rituals help unlock protected effects, and Lightning Storm gives another board-breaking card before committing Ritual resources. Rank 4 and Rank 8 Extra Deck options are there for hands that clear the Impcantation lock.

 

Recovery: Medium-high. Impcantation Inception recycles itself, Preparation of Rites can recover Ritual Spells from the GY, Pre-Preparation can reload the original Demise or Ruin package, and Bookstone or Penciplume recover Ritual pieces after the first push. The deck still has to manage resources carefully because big Ritual summons consume multiple cards.

 

Going first/second: Best going second, where Demise, Lightning Storm, Dark Ruler No More, Evenly Matched, and Nibiru help crack boards before Ruin or Supreme Queen pushes damage. Going first, the deck can set up Bagooska, Hope Harbinger, or simply hold Ritual follow-up with Called by the Grave and side cards depending on the matchup.

 

Choke points: Stop Chalislime, Candoll, Talismandra, or Manju before the missing Ritual half is searched. Ash or negate Pre-Preparation if the Bonz player is low on names. Remove Impcantation bodies before Cycle of the World can use them as tribute material, or interrupt the Ritual Summon before Demise resolves its board clear.

 

Key cards: Demise, Supreme King of Armageddon, Ruin, Supreme Queen of Oblivion, Demise, King of Armageddon, Ruin, Queen of Oblivion, Demise, Agent of Armageddon, Ruin, Angel of Oblivion, Impcantation Chalislime, Impcantation Candoll, Impcantation Talismandra, Manju of the Ten Thousand Hands, Cycle of the World, End of the World, Pre-Preparation of Rites, Preparation of Rites, Impcantation Inception, Pot of Extravagance.

 

Key combos: Chalislime discards a Ritual card to summon Candoll or Talismandra from the Deck, which searches the missing Ritual Spell or Ritual Monster. Pre-Preparation finds End of the World plus original Demise or Ruin. Cycle of the World summons the Supreme forms because they count as the original names in hand or on field. Using the Level 4 Demise or Ruin Rituals as material lets Supreme King avoid the LP payment or lets Supreme Queen gain its stronger battle pressure. After Impcantation bodies leave the field, Rank 4s like Bagooska, Dugares, Exciton, or Tornado Dragon and Rank 8s like Hope Harbinger, Heliopolis, or Dingirsu become useful follow-up options.

deck_name: Bandit Keith - Metalzoa Ancient Gear Train [Low Rogue]
deck_excerpt: LOW ROGUE BUILD - Metalzoa identity backed by Ancient Gear, Lightning Storm, and Train OTK lines.
deck_description:
Power: Low Rogue (6.9/10) - This is much stronger than a pure Metalzoa deck, but it stays Low Rogue because Zoa, Metalzoa, and Metalmorph are still slower identity cards rather than a modern competitive engine. The Ancient Gear and Train shell gives the deck real OTK pressure, while the Keith package keeps the theme visible.

 

Consistency: Medium. Ancient Gear Wyvern, Ancient Gear Catapult, Geartown, Ancient Gear Fortress, Terraforming, and Ancient Gear Ballista give the deck several ways to start playing. Flying Pegasus Railroad Stampede, Heavy Freight Train Derricrane, Bullet Train, Urgent Schedule, and Machina Fortress add Machine bodies and Rank 10 access. The trade-off is that Metalzoa, Zoa, Metalmorph, and the larger Ancient Gear monsters can still create clunky hands.

 

Ceiling: High for a Low Rogue theme deck. Chaos Ancient Gear Giant, Power Bond, Overload Fusion, Ancient Gear Fusion, Limiter Removal, Gustav Max into Juggernaut Liebe, and Borrelsword Dragon can all end games quickly. Ballista also gives the deck a strong damage line by searching Geartown and reducing an opposing monster to 0 ATK.

 

Recovery: Medium. Ancient Gear Fortress and Geartown help convert destroyed cards into more Machines, Super Express Bullet Train can recover follow-up, and Machina Fortress gives the deck a discard outlet that can come back. The deck can rebuild after one exchange, but if the first push loses Geartown, Catapult, or its fusion spells without resolving, it can run out of clean pressure.

 

Going first/second: Best going second. The deck wants to break a board, force through Geartown or Catapult, and turn large Machines into lethal damage. Going first, it can make slower setups with Ancient Gear Howitzer, Number 81: Superdreadnought Rail Cannon Super Dora, or Ancient Gear Fortress protection, but it is not a dedicated control deck. Going second, the side deck brings Dark Ruler No More, Evenly Matched, Nibiru, Twin Twisters, Droll & Lock Bird, and Harpie's Feather Duster.

 

Choke points: Stop Ancient Gear Wyvern before it searches, negate Ancient Gear Catapult, remove Geartown before it can be destroyed profitably, or interrupt Ancient Gear Ballista before it turns the hand into a full push. Hitting Power Bond, Ancient Gear Fusion, Overload Fusion, or Flying Pegasus can also stop the OTK turn from converting into damage.

 

Key cards: Metalzoa, Zoa, Metalmorph, Ancient Gear Wyvern, Ancient Gear Catapult, Geartown, Ancient Gear Fortress, Ancient Gear Ballista, Ancient Gear Reactor Dragon, Ancient Gear Fusion, Power Bond, Heavy Freight Train Derricrane, Super Express Bullet Train, Gustav Max, Juggernaut Liebe.

 

Key combos: Catapult destroys Geartown to summon Ancient Gear bodies and trigger Geartown at the same time. Ballista searches Geartown, then can destroy one of your own cards to make an opposing monster 0 ATK and open an OTK. Hunting Hound, Power Bond, Ancient Gear Fusion, or Overload Fusion turn the Machine pile into Chaos Ancient Gear Giant. Flying Pegasus plus a Level 10 Machine makes Gustav Max, which can burn for 2000 and then overlay into Juggernaut Liebe for game-ending pressure. Metalzoa is not the most efficient payoff, but it is the signature Keith card that gives the deck its identity.

deck_name: Bandit Keith - Zera Impcantation Megalith [Low Rogue]
deck_excerpt: LOW ROGUE BUILD - Zera the Mant upgraded with Impcantation search power, Megalith utility, Benten/Orange Light interaction, and legal Rank 4/8 tools.
deck_description:
Power: Low Rogue (6.9/10) - This is the strongest identity-safe version I can make from the Yu-Gi-Oh! Legacy of the Duelist: Link Evolution card pool while keeping Zera the Mant as the face of the deck. It is much cleaner and more interactive than the casual version, but Zera is still an old-school Ritual boss, so Low Rogue is the honest label.

 

Consistency: Strong for a classic Ritual strategy. Pre-Preparation of Rites directly finds Zera the Mant and Zera Ritual, while Manju, Impcantation Chalislime, Candoll, Talismandra, Preparation of Rites, Benten, and Pot of Extravagance help assemble ritual access without relying on post-Link Evolution cards.

 

Ceiling: Moderate-high. The deck can present Zera backed by Demise removal, Megalith Bethor pops, Megalith Aratron protection, Herald of Orange Light disruption, and legal Extra Deck tools like Bagooska, Gallant Granite, Dugares, Hope Harbinger, Heliopolis, Dingirsu, and the Knightmares.

 

Recovery: Medium. Impcantation Inception can recycle itself, Megalith Portal recovers Ritual Monsters after summons, Monster Reborn converts traded bosses into follow-up, and the Megalith package gives the deck a better grind plan than pure Zera Ritual would have.

 

Going first/second: Going first, aim to keep a live Herald of Orange Light line with Benten or Manju, or make a Rank 4/8 utility monster after resolving your Ritual plays. Going second, Demise, Bethor, Dingirsu, Heliopolis, Dark Ruler No More, Evenly Matched, Twin Twisters, and Harpie's Feather Duster give the deck real board-breaking tools.

 

Choke points: Stop Chalislime, Manju, or Candoll before the missing Ritual piece is found. Removing Zera Ritual or Impcantation Inception from circulation slows the deck down, and forcing Impcantations to stay on board can lock the pilot out of Extra Deck lines.

 

Key cards: Zera the Mant, Zera Ritual, Impcantation Chalislime, Impcantation Candoll, Impcantation Talismandra, Manju of the Ten Thousand Hands, Cyber Angel Benten, Herald of Orange Light, Megalith Och, Megalith Bethor, Megalith Aratron, Pot of Extravagance.

 

Key combos: Pre-Preparation searches Zera plus Zera Ritual. Chalislime reveals a Ritual Monster to summon Candoll or Talismandra and find the missing spell or monster. Tributing Benten for a Ritual Summon searches Manju or Herald of Orange Light. Megalith Och and Aratron let the deck ritual summon during either Main Phase, while Bethor turns Ritual Monsters in the GY into removal. Pot of Extravagance improves opening quality because this build does not depend on one specific Extra Deck card.

deck_name: Bandit Keith - Scrap Orcust Combo [High Rogue]
deck_excerpt: HIGH ROGUE BUILD - Keith-style Machine combo where Scrap Recycler and Wyvern turn into Orcust, Dingirsu, and Topologic pressure.
deck_description:
Power: High Rogue (8.3/10) - This is the strongest identity-safe version of the Bandit Keith Scrap Orcust concept I can make from the Link Evolution card pool. It is fully TCG-legal and avoids banned modern shortcuts like Halqifibrax, Auroradon, Apollousa, Borreload Savage Dragon, Knightmare Mermaid, and Knightmare Gryphon, but Scrap Recycler into Scrap Wyvern plus Orcust recursion is still a serious rogue-level Machine combo shell.

 

Consistency: Strong. The deck has multiple ways to reach its engine through Scrap Recycler, Scrapyard, Scrap Factory, Terraforming, Scrapstorm, Foolish Burial, One for One, Orcustrated Return, and the small Danger package. Recycler is the best starter because it sends O-Lion, Orcust Harp Horror, Orcust Knightmare, World Wand, or Brass Bombard depending on the hand. Danger monsters and Orcustrated Return also help turn awkward Orcust names into live graveyard resources.

 

Ceiling: High. Scrap Wyvern converts Scrap bodies into Scrap Golem and Recycler pressure, while Harp Horror, Orcust Knightmare, World Wand, Cymbal Skeleton, Galatea, Dingirsu, Babel, and Crescendo give the deck real interaction. I:P Masquerena into Unicorn, Dingirsu protection, Topologic Bomber Dragon, Topologic Zeroboros, Borrelsword Dragon, Scrap Dragon, and PSY-Framelord Omega give the Extra Deck enough legal threats without relying on banned boss monsters.

 

Recovery: Excellent. Orcust cards naturally recycle themselves, Galatea converts banished Machines back into deck resources, Dingirsu protects the board or sends a problem card, and Cymbal Skeleton can revive an Orcust link after the first wave is answered. Scrap Factory, Scrap Golem, Monster Reborn, Gizmek Orochi, and the Danger bodies also help the deck keep playing after trades.

 

Going first/second: Best going first. The ideal plan is to establish Galatea, Dingirsu, Babel or Crescendo, with I:P, Topologic, or Borrelsword follow-up depending on the hand. Going second, the side deck brings Dark Ruler No More, Evenly Matched, Nibiru, Twin Twisters, Droll & Lock Bird, and Harpie's Feather Duster so the combo engine has room to start.

 

Choke points: Stop Scrap Recycler before it sends the key graveyard piece, negate Scrap Wyvern before it resolves, remove Scrap Factory before the destroy trigger converts into Scrap Golem, or banish Harp Horror, Orcust Knightmare, World Wand, or Cymbal Skeleton before Galatea can turn them into follow-up. Removing Orcustrated Babel also matters because it turns the Orcust engine back into slower main-phase effects.

 

Key cards: Scrap Recycler, Scrap Wyvern, Scrap Factory, Scrap Golem, Scrap Chimera, Orcust Harp Horror, Orcust Knightmare, Orcust Brass Bombard, World Legacy - "World Wand", Orcust Cymbal Skeleton, Galatea, Dingirsu, Orcustrated Babel, Orcust Crescendo, Topologic Zeroboros.

 

Key combos: Scrap Recycler sends O-Lion or an Orcust name to start the graveyard chain. Scrap Wyvern revives and destroys a Scrap monster, which triggers Scrap Factory and summons Scrap Golem or another Recycler. Harp Horror summons Knightmare or Cymbal Skeleton, Knightmare sends World Wand, World Wand extends into Galatea, and Galatea sets Babel or Crescendo. Babel lets Orcust graveyard effects happen on either turn, which makes Dingirsu, Longirsu, Topologic Bomber Dragon, and Topologic Zeroboros much more threatening. One for One can access Brass Bombard, giving Orcust-heavy hands another way to put a Machine body on the field.

deck_excerpt: LOW ROGUE BUILD - Arkana's Ectoplasmer burn-control plan tightened with Souls, Soul Servant, Circle, Navigation, and legal side-deck breakers.
deck_description:
Power: Low Rogue (6.4/10) - This is cleaner and fully TCG-legal, but it stays Low Rogue because Ectoplasmer, Dark Magic Curtain, Stealth Bird, and Malice Doll of Demise are still slower theme payoffs rather than a modern rogue engine.

 

Consistency: Better than the old version. The deck now maxes Magicians' Souls, Magician's Rod, Dark Magical Circle, and Soul Servant, while Pot of Extravagance digs for the control pieces. Dark Magic Curtain is kept as a one-card Arkana button instead of a repeated brick.

 

Ceiling: Moderate. The main plan is Dark Magician control backed by Ectoplasmer burn, Stealth Bird chip damage, Infinite Impermanence, Solemn Judgment, Eternal Soul, and Magician Navigation. Secrets of Dark Magic gives a small Fusion ceiling through The Dark Magicians when the hand lines up.

 

Recovery: Solid for a theme-first burn deck. Eternal Soul and Navigation keep Dark Magician available, Soul Servant reloads follow-up, and Malice Doll of Demise can come back after being sent by Ectoplasmer or Infected Mail.

 

Going first/second: Best going first. Open with Circle, Eternal Soul, Navigation, Judgment, Impermanence, Messenger, or Ectoplasmer, then force the opponent to answer multiple small sources of pressure. Going second, the side deck brings Dark Ruler No More, Evenly Matched, Cosmic Cyclone, Nibiru, Droll, and Feather Duster.

 

Choke points: Remove Ectoplasmer before the End Phase, banish Malice Doll before it can return, stop Rod or Soul Servant from finding the Dark Magician engine, or clear Eternal Soul before Circle can turn Dark Magician summons into banishes.

 

Key cards: Ectoplasmer, Dark Magic Curtain, Dark Magician, Magicians' Souls, Magician's Rod, Soul Servant, Dark Magical Circle, Eternal Soul, Magician Navigation, Stealth Bird, Malice Doll of Demise, Infected Mail.

 

Key combos: Ectoplasmer tributes Malice Doll for burn, then Malice Doll can return on the next Standby Phase. Souls sends Dark Magician to the GY for Eternal Soul and Soul Servant lines. Rod searches Circle, Eternal Soul, Navigation, or Curtain. Circle plus Eternal Soul turns every Dark Magician summon into removal. Infected Mail lets a theme monster poke directly before being sent, which can also enable Malice Doll's return.

deck_name: Arkana - Dark Flare Knight Fusion [Low Rogue]
deck_excerpt: LOW ROGUE BUILD - Arkana's Dark Flare Knight fusion plan upgraded with Souls, Soul Servant, Secrets, Cross-Sheep, and legal side-deck breakers.
deck_description:
Power: Low Rogue (6.7/10) - This is stronger and fully TCG-legal, but it stays Low Rogue because Dark Flare Knight, Mirage Knight, and Knight's Title are still theme-forward payoffs rather than modern rogue end boards.

 

Consistency: Stronger than the old version. Elemental HERO Prisma, E - Emergency Call, Keeper of Dragon Magic, King of the Swamp, Magicians' Souls, Soul Servant, Fusion Conscription, Polymerization, and Secrets of Dark Magic all help reach Dark Magician material or a Fusion spell.

 

Ceiling: Solid. Dark Flare Knight remains the signature goal, while The Dark Magicians, Dark Magician the Dragon Knight, Dark Cavalry, Quintet Magician, and Cross-Sheep give the deck more practical Fusion pressure when the exact Mirage Knight line is not available.

 

Recovery: Moderate to good. Magicians' Souls and Soul Servant keep cards moving, Magicalized Fusion converts graveyard materials into a late Fusion, and Fusion Conscription or Keeper can reload missing materials. The deck still suffers if Dark Magician access or the graveyard is cut off.

 

Going first/second: Flexible, but it usually wants to assemble a Fusion line before committing. Going first, lean on The Dark Magicians, Dark Cavalry, Cross-Sheep extension, and Infinite Impermanence. Going second, the side deck brings Dark Ruler No More, Nibiru, Evenly Matched, Cosmic Cyclone, Droll, and Feather Duster.

 

Choke points: Stop Keeper before it finds Polymerization, stop Prisma before it loads Dark Magician, interrupt King of the Swamp or Fusion Conscription before materials line up, or banish the graveyard before Magicalized Fusion resolves.

 

Key cards: Dark Flare Knight, Mirage Knight, Dark Magician, Elemental HERO Prisma, Keeper of Dragon Magic, King of the Swamp, Magicians' Souls, Soul Servant, Secrets of Dark Magic, The Eye of Timaeus.

 

Key combos: Prisma reveals Dark Flare Knight to send Dark Magician and become a named material. King of the Swamp searches Polymerization or substitutes for Flame Swordsman. Keeper turns a discard into Fusion access. Magicians' Souls loads Dark Magician for Soul Servant, Timaeus, or Magicalized Fusion. Cross-Sheep rewards successful Fusion summons with extra material for follow-up.

deck_excerpt: LOW ROGUE BUILD - TCG-legal Orichalcos Warrior Stun using Gouki/Connector bodies, The Seal, Boarder, Stormforth and Spell Canceller.
deck_description:
Power: Low Rogue (6.9/10) - This is stronger and fully TCG-legal, but it stays Low Rogue because The Seal of Orichalcos forces a real choice between Extra Deck pressure and the stun plan. The deck can steal games, but it is not as smooth as a dedicated modern rogue engine.

 

Consistency: Moderate to strong. Neo Space Connector, Gouki Suprex, Gouki Twistcobra, Reinforcement of the Army, Terraforming, Pot of Extravagance, and Gouki Re-Match give the deck several openers while still keeping The Seal visible.

 

Ceiling: Solid. Without banned Isolde or Apollousa lines, the ceiling is no longer fake-combo high, but Gouki bodies still climb into legal Link pressure and the Seal plan can make ordinary stun monsters hit much harder.

 

Recovery: Solid. Gouki searches replace material, Gouki Re-Match rebuilds bodies, Monster Reborn extends after trades, and Pot of Extravagance helps find a second wave. The deck is weaker if its Gouki names are banished before they can search.

 

Going first/second: Going first, aim for Inspector Boarder, Spell Canceller pressure, or a Gouki setup backed by Ash Blossom and Effect Veiler. Going second, Stormforth can tribute an opposing monster into Spell Canceller, while the side deck brings Evenly Matched, Nibiru, Cosmic Cyclone, Lancea, Droll, and Feather Duster.

 

Choke points: Stop Neo Space Connector before Aqua Dolphin checks the hand, stop Gouki Suprex or Gouki Re-Match before bodies snowball, remove The Seal before boosted stun monsters take over combat, or answer Spell Canceller before it blanks key spell lines.

 

Key cards: The Seal of Orichalcos, Neo Space Connector, Gouki Suprex, Gouki Re-Match, Inspector Boarder, Spell Canceller, The Monarchs Stormforth, Pot of Extravagance.

 

Key combos: Connector summons Aqua Dolphin to clear or reveal interaction before committing. Gouki bodies link off to search the next Gouki name, then Re-Match revives two names for another push. The Seal boosts Boarder or Spell Canceller into harder-to-answer threats. Stormforth tributes an opposing monster to land Spell Canceller without needing to win combat. Do not activate The Seal before an Extra Deck line unless you are choosing the stun route, because it locks your Extra Deck plan once it is active.

deck_name: Alister - Buster Blader Dragon Lock [Low Rogue]
deck_excerpt: LOW ROGUE BUILD - Alister's Dragon Buster lock, cleaned for TCG legality with Whelp/Prologue access, Extravagance draw power, and Buster Dragon pressu
deck_description:
Power: Low Rogue (7.4/10) - This is stronger and fully TCG-legal, but it stays Low Rogue because it is still a trap-led Buster Blader lock deck that prefers going first and can stumble when Prologue or Whelp is stopped.

 

Consistency: Strong. Buster Whelp, Prologue of the Destruction Swordsman, Trap Trick, Cards of Consonance, Emblem of Dragon Destroyer, One for One, Foolish Burial, and Pot of Extravagance give the deck several ways to reach the lock pieces or dig for interaction.

 

Ceiling: Strong when the lock resolves. Buster Dragon plus Buster Blader, the Dragon Destroyer Swordsman can shut off monster effects and punish Dragon-converted boards, while Dragon Buster Destruction Sword adds Extra Deck lock pressure when it sticks.

 

Recovery: Solid. Prologue, Destruction Sword Memories, Emblem, Monster Reborn, extra Buster Dragon copies, and graveyard fusion lines let the deck rebuild after the first lock is broken, though banish-heavy disruption still hurts.

 

Going first/second: Best going first with Whelp or Trap Trick into Prologue, backed by Ash Blossom, Effect Veiler, Infinite Impermanence, or DNA Surgery. Going second, the side deck brings Evenly Matched, Nibiru, Cosmic Cyclone, Artifact Lancea, Droll, and Feather Duster to simplify boards before setting up the lock.

 

Choke points: Stop Buster Whelp before it searches, stop Prologue before it resolves, banish Buster Blader or Memories before the graveyard fusion line, or remove Buster Dragon before Dragon Destroyer converts the matchup. Spell/trap removal on set Prologue also matters.

 

Key cards: Buster Whelp of the Destruction Swordsman, Prologue of the Destruction Swordsman, Destruction Sword Memories, Buster Blader, the Dragon Destroyer Swordsman, Buster Dragon, Trap Trick, Pot of Extravagance, Infinite Impermanence.

 

Key combos: Whelp searches Prologue. Prologue sends Destruction Sword Memories and Buster Blader to make Buster Dragon, then Memories banishes materials to summon Dragon Destroyer. Trap Trick acts as extra Prologue access. DNA Surgery or Buster Dragon turns opposing monsters into Dragons, making Dragon Destroyer much harder to attack through. Pot of Extravagance digs for starters while the extra deck runs duplicate lock pieces to absorb the banish cost.

deck_excerpt: AKA Old Control Deck meets New One.
deck_description:
So the general idea behind the deck is that the Asutra cards synergize rather well with Fydraulis Harmonia, what with the Synchros; Fydraulis has some natural synergy with the Dogmatika cards as it also sends Extra Deck monsters and the Dogmatika can provide a Normal Summon to give the Asutra a Set card to go off among so much.

So in more depth:

Asutra Yoa and Mikumari are your main Asuta monsters to see. Yoa is very important as it sets up your Level 8 Synchro as well as the other Asutras by setting a Trap from deck with Mukmari being another way to get to Yoa while being a better Synchro Material. If you need to search with the Field Spell, search Mikumari (since you'll need a second Set card to summon her) then Yoa.

If you feel the Synchro isn't necessary, that you're gonna get Droll'd or that you're playing under Nibiru: Go for Akasuna in most situation, then Kuzuha. Akasuna is more useful in most case as a Book of Eclipse on a 3000 ATK body and the ability to set up for the Level 9 Synchro for a mass disruption is great. Kuzuha is more of an OTK/kill push. You need a good number of Set cards to even get one pop (which given F&V is so prevelant, can be an issue), though the GY Set meshes well with Akasuna.

I run two of each Synchro since I wanna make sure I have extra access to them. You can run 1 of both to make some more room in the Extra Deck, which can be nice for certain packages I'll mention at the end.

The Field Spell is a 3 of to get to the Asutra side of the Deck. Believe it or not, this side is the one with the real firepower. Book of Eclipse, non-targeting/non-destruction send and a mass non-targeting banish is a lot stronger than a restricted targeting banish with some Extra deck help. It also has great synergy with F&V+Albion, as Albion's End Phase set can trigger the Field's bounce effect.

As for the Traps: I run two for each because they're all pretty necessary for the deck to function and they all have their niche cases. Roar is the best as a simple monster pop, Domain is a good insulator against board breakers and Poison can bring back a Asutra like Akasuna or even Yoa for another interruption. You'll be mostly using these to Synchro Summon, though.

For the Dogmatika cards: Three Eccelsia is a must. The card is the only normal summon in the whole Deck and it greatly helps the Asutra side of things. Cool thing is that since Eccelsia's Extra Deck lock is only for your turn, it's a non-issue for the Asutra cards.

Three Fleur Thunderbolt for a Turn 0 option, one that doesn't really affect the Deck since Punishment's lock being two turns only lasts for the opponent's turn 1 and your turn 1, where you don't do Extra Deck summons. And even if you can't use Punishment, it can still be used later for an Asutra summon. Also a free 2500 ATK body helps push damage.

1 Lawbringer for a combination of an extra ED interruption and recycling for F&V/Punishment. It's also a Turn 0 interrupt by that logic but not as flexible as Thunderbolt.

3 Nadir Servant to help get to Eccelsia with the hand summons being an option if you already have her, with the ED send either fetching F&V. fetching one of the hand summons/Punishment, setting up Black Dragon Eccelsia or even a generic ED breaker like Golden Cloud Beast or Packmister. Again, it's ED lock means nothing for this deck.

3 F&V (AKA The Falled And The Virtuous). Just in case you don't know why this card's so good: It's a pop for any face-up card on the field that can also be Quick-Play Monster Reborn if you have an Eccelsia on field or Grave. This includes the Synchro. It's also very searchable, especially in this deck with Dogmatika Eccelsia, and can be searched out by Albion after being sent.

3 Punishment. Like I said before, this card is basically the prototype for F&V. While true that it's a Trap that needs to be set to work and it's ED lock can screw you over if you don't sequence it well: It's still a searchable monster pop that can net you extra advantage, which becomes even better through Thunderbolt giving you more Turn 0 options. And again: it gives the Asutra cards another shot at being summoned.

For non-engine: Three Imperm because out of the common Trap Card hand traps, it's the only one that doesn't screw with the Deck. And two Ash Blossom, because it still gums up hands. A third could be useful with better ratios.

For the Extra, aside from the Asutras I covered:

2 Albion, 1 Titanklad, 1 Devours The Dogma and 1 Black Dragon Eccelsia as the F&V/Dogmatika package. Titanklad is here as another way to search/summon Eccelsia or the hand summons for extra grind. Same with Devours, except it can add F&V in exchange for no possible Eccelsia summon. Albion is obvious, with the extra synergy with the Field Spell. And Black Dragon Eccelsia unlocks F&V's second effect and can recover resources while also poking holes in boards.

For Punishment/Fydralius: Golden Cloud Beast, Engimaster Packbit, Psy-Framelord Omega and Despian Luluwalilth. The first two are differing forms of disruption, Omega recycles itself and another card for a minor but infinite recursion and Lulu is another way to access Eccelsia or Thunderbolt.

The two odd ducks left are Dracoberserker and Nacy Dragon Mech as side grades to the Asutra synchros. Need a banish in response to a monster effect instead of a GY send? Go Dracoberserker. Need negation with Eccelsia, Golden Cloud and/or Fyrdralius in grave? Navy Dragon Mech.

The side doesn't contain any actual side deck cards (kind of) but instead has options for the deck:

-OG Fleur for more damage and more monster hate. Doesn't help Turn 0 unless you have her other self or Lawbringer. Kind of underwhleming.

-Stardust Victim Sanctuary for another part of the Fydralius/Punishment package. You could do the OG for protection against breakers, obviously, but I wanted to highlight Stardust Warrior as a Summon negate. You could also do Stardust Spark if you wanted, though I'd say it's the weakest of the three.

-N'Tss. Classic Punishment target. You can swap out one of the other odd ducks for this, though it's a bit more clunky.

-Ultimaya. This is an edge case but Fydralius is, again, a Tuner. If you have one of the Level 7 Asutras on field with it, you can pivot into Ultimaya. And since you WANT to Set cards, Ultimaya's effect can fetch any Level 7 or 8 Dragon Synchro. Crystal Wing is just the most blatant.

-Dogmatika Maximus. The Extra Deck rip can really screw over certain decks...but F&V is almost omni-present so not the best idea.

-Encounter as an extra Trap set off Thunderbolt. I hope Konami prints another generally good Dogmatika trap.

-Aggregator for a negate off of Punishment. This also represents the other more generic Punishment targets.

-Albaz for using Titanklad or Devours for a Super Poly.

-Hope Harbinger and Dingirsu. This deck runs a lot of 8s that you could pivot into one of these two, who can insulate you against F&V and breakers.

-Fuwalos and Nibiru for other handtrap options.

I am not a good deckbuilder, that much is obvious. You probably only need 1 of each Asutra trap, you could probably cut a Field Spell and Nadir, cut Lawbringer and Dracoberseker/Navy for better options. This deck is also not the best Abyss-related mix with Asutra. The White-Albaz package gives you better access to 8s, better Albaz breaking and is smaller for more non-engine/First Penguin which does a lot of similar stuff for this deck. But I found a surprising amount of synergy here. I like decks that go 1 for 1 with a good amount of flexibility. And I think anyone else who likes this gameplay will like this deck.

Also kind of a crime more people aren't trying anything Asutra.

(P.S. Sorry if this sounds incomprehensible or anything. I am typing this at like 2 AM and I'm not a competitive player so...my insight might be garbage.)

deck_excerpt: Your graveyard isn't a pile. It's a loop.
deck_description: Every Orcust monster banishes itself from the graveyard to trigger the next piece, and Galatea shuffles the spent cards back into the deck so you can burn through the same resources twice. Girsu starts the whole machine from a single Normal Summon. Orcustrated Babel turns the entire engine into Quick Effects, meaning Dingirsu's non-targeting, non-destruction removal and Cymbal Skeleton's revival plays can fire on your opponent's turn, not just yours. The catch — and the skill test — is the DARK lock: commit to the Orcust chain and every summon after it must be DARK, so sequencing decisions before that point of no return separate a good Orcust pilot from a great one.

deck_excerpt: One card, two Synchros, zero apologies.
deck_description: A Synchro deck built on a single elegant trick: every Swordsoul monster hands you a free Tuner Token the moment it activates, so you're never more than one card away from a Level 8 or Level 10 boss monster. Tenyi Spirits ride shotgun — the Tokens satisfy their "non-Effect monster" condition, so your whole hand can unload for free the instant a Swordsoul fires. Chixiao's on-summon search chains into Longyuan for a second Synchro in the same breath, while Chengying turns every banish on the field — yours or theirs — into a ticking removal clock. Consistent, resilient to hand traps thanks to three separate starters, and genuinely rewards understanding chain order over raw power level.

This deck rarely needs to activate the effects of light or dark monsters or monster effects in the hand, graveyard, or banished zone. As a result, it can run Dominus Spiral and Songs of the Dominators instead of The Fallen & The Virtuous to disrupt your opponent. The current main deck Ashtra monsters, Kashtira Unicorn and Kashtira Fenrir all have inherent special summons from the hand, allowing the deck to function without worrying about the Dominus cards' restrictions.

Essentially, your goal is to summon as many of your Fairy Tail ladies out and attack directly by utilizing your field spell. Also, we have a dragoon package for all your negating needs as well as accesscode for destroying any pesky cards. on top of that, we have three super poly targets besides your fairy tail fusion monsters.

A standard combo looks like:

Summon Luna, add Matchgiru, summon Matchgiru, search Fairy Tail Ball, activate Ball, search Fairy Tail Tales, summon Wiccat, activate Wiccat, dump Tails of the Fairy Tails and Once Upon a Fairy Tail, trigger both spells effects, equip Tails of the Fairy Tails to Wiccat, and set Once Upon a Fairy Tail, activate Once Upon a Fairy Tail, summon a Fairy Tail from your gy (and hand if you have one), activate Tails of the Fairy Tails, summon Weaver, Weaver summons Rella, Rella discards a spell to equip Tails of the Fairy Tails, special summon Teller using Tails of the Fairy Tails.

gl;hf duelists.

This deck excels at flooding the board with materials, returning your opponent's cards to the hand/deck, and even can just flat out skip your opponent's turn if you get enough materials.

A sample combo looks like: Summon Castor, Castor summons Cygnian, Cygnian searches Caduceus, Caduceus summons itself and searches Constellar Tellarknights, use Caduceus and Castor to summon Tellarknight Constellar Delteros, add and summon Unukalhai, Unukalhai dumps Lyran, activate Constellar Tellarknights and summon the dumped Lyran, add Skybridge, use Cygnian to enhance it's level and Lyran's or Unukalhai's, use the two leveled up monsters to summon Stellarknight Constellar Diamond, use Constellar Tellarknights to summon Exstellarknight Constellar Ptolemaeus Omega7, pass.

Tournament: European WCQ Genesys 2026 – July 10th 2026

Placement: Top 32
Don't want your opponent to play? Your at the right place.

Guess which rogue deck is Fiendsmith accessible via the fusion monster? Plunder Patroll. Start your combos with Fiendsmith or Plunder Patroll (just don't revive Goldenhair immediately) and create a nightmare for your opponent.

Since you pretty likely to have a Link or Fusion in your GY, you can play Rextremende if you want. Big Caeser is just there for extra negates. Feel free to sub out Luce or one of your other ED for him/Rex.

GL;HF

I'm not really sure how Orcust fell off. It's so good still. Load your Graveyard with materials using Horus, go Zombie Vampire for more loading, and then just go full on bananas by dismantling their field and making Hecahands jealous with Enlilgirsu's blatant thievery.

The deck that refuses to die, Konami giving us Fydraulis Harmonia was a horrible mistake that will end in blood and tears. Use Fydraulis on your opponent's turn and sent Enigmaster Packbit, Wind Pegasus, or Malong to activate a variety of effects while still getting a Level 7 tuner out of the deal. And if anyone is asking, "why Chaos Angel?", remember that Stone Sweeper is a level 5 monster that special summons whenever there is a field spell. Any of your level 5 synchros fill the gap and make it so monsters you control cannot be destroyed by battle. Pair that with Loudness War...you get the idea.
if you know how to IMPROVE this deck PLEASE let me know!

deck_excerpt: Familiar-Possessed - Dharc bridges directly into Fairy Tail giving the deck NINE more 1-card starters!
deck_description:
The entire premise of using Charmers with Fairy Tail is that Familiar-Possessed - Dharc searches Matchgiru with his absurd summoning condition (inherent summon from deck) giving the deck NINE more 1-card starters! Every card that searches Spellbook of the Grand Circle and Grand Circle itself is full Fairy Tail combo, and more! This makes the strategy hyper consistent, giving Fairy Tail a total of 18 starters in a 44 card deck!

I can hear you asking, "why should I use Charmers over the more popular variants like Nekroz or Magistus?" Well, the benefit of using Charmers is that not only do they function as a starter, but they are incredible boardbreakers and extenders thanks to Charmers of the Grand Circle being effectively uninteractable on-fusion summon thanks to Spellbook of the Grand Circle! The card reads "... your opponent cannot activate cards or effects when a monster is Fusion Summoned this way" and Charmers of the Grand Circle can non-target bounce up to 4 cards your opponent controls! This fixes the inherent weakness that Fairy Tail has with its weak ability to go second, enabling the strategy to comfortably play into established boards! 

Charmers can also do cool things like "soulcharge" monster reborn up to 4 spellcasters from the GY, fusion summon Quintet Magician to blow up your opponent's entire field, etc. 

Here is the Fairy Tail combo with Spellbook of the Grand Circle: 

1. Activate Spellbook of the Grand Circle to add Dharc the Dark Charmer with any 3 Charmers from deck then shuffle back 2 cards (you can shuffle back garnets with this effect). 

2. Banish Spellbook of the Grand Circle from the GY to fusion summon Charmers of the Grand Circle using Lyna + 3 Charmers in hand. 

3. Resolve Charmers of the Grand Circle's effect to search for Possessed Partnerships, Awakening of the Possessed, and summon Dharc from the GY, and summon any Charmer from the GY. 

4. Activate Awakening of the Possessed. 

5. Contact fuse Magistus Chorozo using the extra Charmer (not Dharc) + Charmers of the Grand Circle. 

6. Send Dharc the Dark Charmer + Magistus Chorozo to the GY to summon directly from deck Familiar-Possessed - Dharc. 

7. CL1 Awakening of the Possessed draws 1 card, CL2 Familiar-Possessed - Dharc searches for Matchgiru.

8. Summon Matchgiru from hand since you control a 1850 spellcaster. 

9. Matchgiru searches for Fairy Tail Ball. 

10. Activate Fairy Tail Ball to search for Fairy Tail - Luna.

11. XYZ summon Fairy Tail - Wiccat using Matchgiru + Familiar-Possessed - Dharc. 

12. Activate Wiccat's effect detaching 2 materials to send from deck to GY Tails of the Fairy Tails and Once Upon a Fairy Tail. 

13. Once Upon a Fairy Tail activates to set itself when sent to GY from deck. 

14. Activate Tails of Fairy Tails effect to equip itself to Wiccat and grant an additional normal summon to summon Fairy Tail - Luna. 

15. Luna searches for Genia of the Ring. 

16. Activate Tails of the Fairy Tails to fusion summon Weaver of the Fairy Tails using Wiccat + another spellcaster (if you normal summoned Spellbook Magician of Prophecy earlier to search for your spellbook cards, use him, or use any extra spellcaster you have like a charmer, try not to use Luna on your field). 

17. Activate Weaver's effect to special summon Fairy Tail - Rella from deck. From here, you get to decide on what to do. you can use to discard a spell and re-equip Tails of Fairy Tails from GY to fusion summon Teller of Fairy Tails, or you can XYZ summon Alchemic Magician to set Mistaken Arrest (basically Exodia in GENESYS format) from deck during the End Phase! If you have enough material, you can summon both Teller and Alchemic!

18. Set Possessed Partnerships and any other cards (like Super Poly for example). 

Your endboards feature a mix of Teller's omni-negate, Mistaken Arrest, draw 1 (or draw 3 if you can get Spellbook of Knowledge), Possessed Partnership's pop, Luna's bounce, Fairy Tail Ball's unique negate, Weaver's negate, Once Upon a Fairy Tail's "soulcharge" summon effect and the protection of Wiccat in the GY! Your endboards are multi-layered and hard to crack! 

Charmer Fairy Tail may not be the strongest variant of Fairy Tail, but it sure makes up for it with it's consistency and powerful boardbreaking ability, patching up the main inherent weaknesses of Fairy Tail. Together, both archetypes create the ultimate spellcaster combination in the GENESYS format! 

GLHF Duelists!

Want to see the deck in action? Scroll to the very bottom for the Youtube video!

deck_excerpt: DoomZ, Power Patron, Bystial, Super Poly
deck_description:
41 Card list due to FORBIDDEN CROWN (i only have one)

 

If i had multiple, i would cut engine cards, or drop SHIFTER and MAGNAMUT into the side deck.

Since the deck has multiple lines of play, i can only hint at some things you can do with the cards.

Generall Strategy:

-GRAFLAREIO is the most important tool in the deck since it searches any Doomz spell/trap, then can destroy 1 card on the field NON TARGETING.

-You want to get to GRAFLAREIO with either normal summon elara or amalthe. Destroy GRAFLAREIO with DOOMZ RAIDERS if possible for the double summon of 2 minions, 1 from deck and 1 from deck/graveyard. This should result in you getting 2 4s and make SPRINGANS MERRYMAKER to overlay for SARGAS to get THERION KING REGULUS into your hand for your first omni negate. You can change this line, by summoning a POWER PATRON DOOMZ with 1 of your special summons, but it depends on cards used and how you started your combo.

-Another general strat if you have only DOOMZ CHANGE in hand is to use it to destroy POWER PATRON DOOMZ to get access to any doomz card you are missing or looking for. If you have 1 of the level 4 normal summons already,  you dont need to do that and instead use it with DRASTEA on board ( equiped with DOOMDURGE for the destroy quick effect) to get DOOMZ DESTRUCTION destroyed and chain the DOOMDURGE quickeffect to destroy it on the same chain. This results in you getting the droll like trap, as well as any doomz card. 

-Both lvl 4s equiped with a equip spell are starters since their quickeffects let you rank up into the Rank 4 DIACTORUS and then into GRAFLAREIO.

-ADDRASTEA is an insane card that usually going first is searched by ELARA and equiped to it to start the combos.

-If you hard draw ADDRASTEA going second you can euqip it to an enemy minion and still use its effect to destroy the equip to special a Doomz monster from deck. If resolved this way u usually win the game tbh.

-Drastea is your 1 card way to the DRASTRIUS that is an insane piece of endboard. With it being a quickeffect you can use its effect to overlay with it on your opponents turn to get a free minion equip. Since you usually aim for the trap to be active in your opponents draw phase you dont use this tech going first though.

-DRASTEA´s prime target to destroy and summon itself is usually any power patron attached to the REGULUS since it doesnt need the equip to get the omni off. Otherwise POWER PATRON DOOMZ is usually pretty good. 

-Most of the time you want to equip DOOMDURGE to DRASTEA then go into the DOOMZ CHANGE combo.

-If i had i would play SECRETERION as SUPER POLY target instead of the 2nd DIACTORUS since all your normals are DRAGONS and so you only need 1 spellcaster. The floodgate effect criples Branded and effectively kills ELFNOTE since most of its engine are spellcasters.

For the POWER PATRON portion of the deck:

-Access to VIDOLIUM with exactly 1 JUPREDO in the grave will always lead to a free VARUDRAS, since you can destroy VIDOLIUM to summon the JUPREDO and with VIDOLIUMS pendulum effect you can search the field spell that banishes a card from your hand and summons another JUPREDO for the two 10 materials. If you only have access to one or if you are going second, JUPREDOs onfield effect can be usefull to go into JUPITER THE POWER PATRON to try and push for game through crown with the 2 possible equip spells. Since DOOMDURGE can make the monster attack directly you can cheese games through full boards.

-BYSTIAL MAGNAMUT is not only a reasonable handtrap to play this format, it also is a searcher for your maindeck DOOMZ monsters.

-The deck can play under DIMENSION SHIFTER, even though you dont have access to the power patron side of the deck, and shifter kills some decks straight up. 

-CALLED BY THE GRAVE has the same reasoning like shifter, but is more usefull IMO since you can get hit hard by handtraps going first if you only have 1 starter in hand. 

GENERAL ENDBOARD:

Varudras, the Final Bringer of the End Times 

DoomZ XII End - Drastrius equiped with DoomZ Destruction and having target protection by  DoomZ Command "D.O.O.M.D.U.R.G." 

Therion "King" Regulus and Gigantic "Champion" Sargas 

 

 

 

EXTRA DECK:

-DROLL kills decks.

-PURULIA is fine, i would cut it if i see a better meta call though.

-SANTA CLAWS is not searchable like in Fiendsmith decks, but is an insane kaiju in the current meta since it is only a lvl 6, therefore it cant be used by Light and Darkness decks to MINDSHUFFLE. Also it can be used multiple times, unlike other kaijus or lava golemn.

-SPHERE MODE is still good enought to play, simply for the fact that you can out 3 monsters and you can still normal summon.

-Backrow hate is essential against some decks.

-Solemns fix the only real weakness in the deck since it doesnt have any answers to DRNM or FORBIDDEN DROPLET ( the most broken card in the current format).

For siding patterns:

-Going first you cut FUWALOS for the SOLEMNS and maybe cut MAGNAMUT for TRIPLE TACTICS TALENT.

-Going 2nd, depending on what you need , you either side in the breakers or the Kaijus to push through boards. Droll is a great side in if you know it kills your opponents gameplan. Even though i dont like the card its pretty good sometimes.

IF you feel like needing more nonengine you can cut 1 DRASTEA and 1 or 2 copies of PROHIBITED POWER PATRON PORTAL - TERMINUS since you can access it via JUPREDO. Some lists even cut down on 2 DOOMZ CHANGE. I like the card in my start hand since you can play around ash with it more. 

Overall a really fun, but complicated deck that can play a surprising ammount of non-engine if you are willing to drop some of the consistency pieces. Going second can be incredibly fun since a lot of your effects can destroy a card on the field when used. This also makes the deck kinda weird going first. Its endboard is pretty strong but the lines play akward if you dont have monsters on the opponents field to abuse some effects. 

I dont run the FNV package since SPOLY hits decks harder IMO. Either is fine. Forbidden Crown is insane in the deck since you can use JUPITER and DOOMDURGE to go for an 8500 damage direct hit if you play it right.

You get 3500 base damage + JUPITERs effect (+3000 damage if you have XYZ under it) + 1000 and direct attack via DOOMDURGE + 1000 via CERUVIM = 8500 direct attack.

Alright so the main changes from the tcg one:
1. Branded is still your worst nightmare, but now with it running more rampant than in the tcg, you have to be careful when you send stuff.
2. Dogma for chuckles when you summon albaz.
3. Chimera and fiendsmith millenium make the deck way better than generaiders, but generaiders still aren't bad. The side deck just contains more options. If running kash package, run more Hecahands Godos (I would go 2 at max) then you can cut the generaider stuff, and run millenium and a small fiendsmith package.
If you really want to run generaiders, then just know you might brick a bit. **This is the smallest possible generaider package**
Between vampire guy and pilgrim reaper I have no idea what is better.
I do know however, that if you run reaper, then you should probably be running Nightmare Apprentice, as you want to be milling your generaider fairy.
 Other things to consider: black goat, cutting some of the heca spells/ traps replacing non engines to what you'd like etc.

Please ask any questions!

deck_description:
COMBO explained : (Press READ MORE)

--> This is a deck to play VS other Noob decks ... but with super fun thematical combos.

1) Don't put Cocoon of Ultra Evolution, Ultimate Great Insect and Insect Queen... Because they outshine your REAL gameplay : Weevil Great Moth. This is a NOOB deck, don't get tempted to put super strong cards that oushine your Anime deck's logic.

2) This deck is mean to be played against other noob decks. They have no real removal. Noob decks outplays with Equiped cards, Skull/Graceful Dices, Needle Wall, Needle Ceiling. --> Fun cards, not instant removal.

Basic monsters should have 1600 atk max
A nice Fusion/Ritual monster should have Between 1900 and 2300 atk

3) A monster with 2600 atk --> Should be hard to get. A Cocoon of Evolution should have a nice chance to survive 4 to 6 turns.


COMBOS for this deck :

1) Petit Moth + Cocoon = Great Moth

2) Nothing else should outshine it. Your other insects have 500 atk ... + 1500 with Bio-Insect armor ... Because a 2000 atk monster is GREAT monster for a Noob deck.

3) Use Graceful Charity (x3), even if it's not allowed --> To put small insect in your graveyard

4) Use Insect Neglect and Bio-Insect Armor --> To Banish your insects and protect your Cocoon

5) Use Insect Invitation --> To draw, with the help of your Banished insects

6) Use Aerosol to destroy all insects --> Insect Invitation turn the opponent's monster into Insects

7) Use Forbidden Lance on your Cocoon --> To save him from your Aerosol

8) Your 2600 atk Great Moth + some 700 atk from equipement --> Is already a BIG BIG monster VS another NOOB deck

deck_excerpt: Runick clowns, kept as cheap as possible, although there isn't anything expensive that I'd say is a straight upgrade besides adding fuwa.

deck_description:
I've been testing a Superheavy Samurai / Power Patron / Elfnote build lately.

To me, Superheavy Samurai is the glue that holds everything together. It bridges into Power Patron, and Power Patron naturally bridges into Elfnote, making the engines flow really well together.

I know one of the biggest downsides of Superheavy Samurai is that it doesn't want Spells in the GY. Because of that, I only use Called by the Grave or Crossout Designator when I absolutely have to stop a Mulcharmy. Otherwise, I usually let my opponent use their hand traps on the Superheavy plays. If they stop me, they've often used up a good amount of their interaction. If they don't, Superheavy naturally snowballs into the Power Patron engine and eventually into Angelechy.

Another thing I really like is the flexibility Superheavy gives me. If I need to survive a turn, I can always make Bagooska as an emergency play. And if I'm going second, I can make Exciton Knight to wipe the entire board. Nobody ever sees that coming. 😂

I built this deck mainly for fun, but I honestly think it can be taken a bit more seriously too. It's actually stronger than I expected and has been performing really well in testing.

What do you guys think? How would you improve it? If you get the chance, give it a try—it's honestly a really fun deck to play!

deck_description:
IMPORTANT: This site doesn't have the card "CXYZ CHAOS BARIAN HOPE DRAGON", make sure to add that manually as it's an important card for the deck.

 

Brand new Umbral Horror deck profile with the new supports.
Make sure to always "Gorgonic Umbral Horror" or "V Umbral Horror" with "Seventh Tachyon" or whatever the searcher it is, if you miss one of them in the hand, I also suggest to search for "Shining Umbral Horror" with "Gorgonic Umbral Horror" as you can fast summon it without going for the xyz.
The ideal combo its: normal "V", search for "Thousand Umbral Horror", discard it and add the field spell, search with the field spell for "Gorgonic", special it, search for "Shining", use "V" and "Gorgonic" for "Number 104: Masquerade Vain", effect "M. Vain" search for the counter trap, use "V" effect from the GY to rank up your xyz, make "Number C104: Umbral Horror Masquerade, use its effect for banish oppo's cards, then make "CXyz Chaos Barian Hope Dragon", when you xyz summon bring back "Thousand" from the GY and xyz for "Number 106: Giant Hand", set the trap and you are good to go.

deck_excerpt: Complete your festival on an *ahem* EXCEPTIONAL SCHEDULE?
deck_description:
Would you like to complete your festival missions on an *ahem* EXCEPTIONAL SCHEDULE?

Want to know the most powerful deck in the 2026 XYZ Fusion Festival? The strategy involves a healthy mix of police forces (K9) with sizeable army of heavy artillery (Train)! Since Trains have received incredible support with the recent Masterduel selection pack, the deck is now extremely consistent and requires very few bricks, allowing these metal behemoths to mix with a supporting archetype! K9 has perfect synergy with Trains because K9 Noroi is an earth machine, and the whole deck makes rank 5s, granting the pilot easy access to Infinitrack River Stormer! River Stormer searches ANY earth machine, and you can go from there! Trains can also branch into K9 thanks to their new XYZ boss monster Superdreadnought Rail Cannon Flying Launcher (phew that's a mouthful), also searching any Earth Machine which can grab K9 Noroi from deck! Not only is EVERY K9 monster a starter, EVERY Train is a starter too because of your Extra Deck searchers! 

Train K9 has pleasantly surprised me in the 2026 XYZ Fusion Festival. It blew past the event's meta threats like Branded, Fairy Tail, Exosister and Lunalight! I completed the entire Festival's missions in record time, since I won like 80% of my duels with this ridiculous firepower! I think I'm also going to give this deck a shot in the regular Masterduel ladder!

This deck is kinda complicated with it's combo lines, so instead of me explaining the intricacies of the deck to you, have the expert Yacine teach you in his Youtube video: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=kBLEvVe-Hx8&

GLHF Duelists!

Want to see the deck in action? Scroll to the very bottom for the Youtube video!

Archfiend Kingdom é um deck de Controle/Grind Game que une o suporte moderno dos Archfiends ao motor Labrynth, criando uma estratégia autoral focada em domínio gradual da partida. Em vez de buscar combos explosivos, o deck utiliza armadilhas para interromper o oponente enquanto estabelece um exército de Arquidemônios que acumulam vantagem ao longo dos turnos.

Os Archfiends são os protagonistas, atuando como a força ofensiva e a identidade do deck, enquanto o núcleo Labrynth funciona como a infraestrutura do "Castelo Prateado", fornecendo consistência, geração de recursos e reciclagem de armadilhas. O resultado é um deck temático, resiliente e adaptável, capaz de vencer partidas por desgaste e controle do campo, valorizando decisões estratégicas e gerenciamento de recursos em vez de velocidade.

deck_description:
After playing the deck and searching for the best endboard and one card combos I can give the following advices:
ONE CARD COMBO
- Usorpation (for Highness Archfiend) for any Fiendsmith piece are the best 1 card combos alongside Highness Archfiend
Usorpation > Highness Archfiend (don't use effect on normal) > Requiem > Lacrima > Engraver > Engraver shuffle Requem > Sequence > Aerial Eater > Buio (adds Mutiny) > Make Agnum Dei with aerial eater and Sequence > Agnum Dei summons and equips to Highness > Summon Necroquip > Mutiny (Summons Fiendsmith's Lacrima) > Lacrima ADDS Highness to hand (don't special) > Make DDD High King Caesar > Normal Highness > Highness (banish Usorpation to add Royal Archfiend and Archfiend Matador) > Royal for Duke > Normal(cus of Royal) Duke > Duke sent to GY Heirness > Heirness adds Throne AND (Fervor or Ghastly Glitch) > Ritual Matador with Duke > Matador adds Strategy and Matriarch > Matriarch in Pendulum to add Throne > Strategy to place Emperor > Ritual summon Emperor form Extra by Tributing Matador and Highness (or if you have in hand other monsters it will be better to not tribute Matador) > Emperor to Special Summon Kewl Tune Reco > Reco adds Clip.

Aboslute Cinema
I have yet to test this deck, but I thought that these two had really good synergy. Will update as soon as I have a little bit of testing.
