from cygnus overule here on the site "Nothing too special. Lightsworn relies on high-rolling pretty heavily, but the simple addition of just one Phantom of Chaos and one Monster Reincarnation is absolutely insane for the deck. It allows you to use basically any effect you want, including Judgement Dragon, as long as you have that card in the GY. This is comes up rather often, especially in the mid / late-game, but if you happen to ditch a JD early on and Normal Summon the Phantom, it can be game-ending. It allows for a second use of basically anything in the deck, from Lumina to gain a board presence back, Ehren to out a sneaky facedown, Chaos Sorcerer to out an annoying card, a Tragoedia for funny plays, or a Card Trooper for quick mills. Otherwise pretty standard."

Sometimes Konami prints 1 card that makes a deck that was otherwise unviable just work. That card was General of Atlantis, as it is able to not just get Umi but also put a free body on board.

The one-card combo (+2 discards) for this deck is First Penguin, and it ends on a WATER Synchro of your choice (normally Dragite), Ocean Dragon Lord - Kairyu-Shin, Kairyu-Shin's Dark Reef to either protect kairyu-shin or to summon Electric Jellyfish, and an effective Forceful Sentry because of Magellanica itself. This is also achieved by having Revealer of the Ice Barrier + a way to Magellanica.
The combo is as follows:
1. Summon Penguin, discard and reveal Brionnac to add General.
2. Discard General to add Advent.
3. Activate Advent, then summon General from GY.
4. Use the effect of Advent to place Magellanica from your Deck.
5. Use Magellanica to increase the level of either Penguin or General by 1.
6. Synchro for Prima Donna, using Magellancia to look at our opponent's hand and rip a card from it.
7. Use Prima Donna to return the ripped card and summon Revealer from the Deck.
8. Use Revealer to discard and summon Mirror Mage from the Deck. 
9. Synchro for Dewloren, using Mirror Mage to add Freezing Chains to your hand.
10. Activate Freezing Chains, reborning Mirror Mage.
11. Use Dewloren to bounce the Magellanica and Freezing Chains.
12. Activate Magellanica, placing Kairyu-Shin on top of your Deck.
13. Use Mirror Mage, tributing Dewloren to summon 3 tokens and increase its level by 3.
14. Use Magellanica to increase Mirror Mage's level by 2.
15. Synchro for Crocodragon using Mirror Mage and 2 Levels tokens, having CL1 being Croc to draw 2 cards, one of which will be Kairyu-Shinl, and CL2 being Magellanica to rip a card from your opponent's hand.
16. Synchro for Dragite using the final token and Prima Donna, using Prima Donna to shuffle the ripped card into the Deck.
17. Tribute Summon Kairyu-Shin using Crocodragon, using Kairyu-Shin to add and then set Dark Reef.
18. On your opponent's turn, if you know they don't have anything like Ash or Dark Ruler, activate Dark Reef, sending Magellanica to summon an Electric Jellyfish and a second Kairyu-Shin from your Deck.

How to Play Around/Into Handtraps:
- Fuwalos: Tribute Summon Kairyu-Shin (and if you like, first synchro for Prima Donna so you get hand knowledge and rip a card out the opponent's hand by tributing over it)
- Purulia or Meowls: Do the combo and give them the 2 draws (Purulia:  penguin and kairyu-shin | Meowls: General and Mirror Mage)
- Nibiru: Summon Jellyfish off Prima Donna as your fifth summon, then summon kairyu-shin if possible
- Shifter: Tribute Summon Kairyu-Shin if possible (harder because goods and electric jellyfish are no longer live)
- Droll: Cry in a corner, unless you have access to Kairyu-shin and Umi, in which case you tribute summon Kairyu-Shin. (NOTE: Goods is live under Droll)
- one for one handtraps (ash, imperm, impulse): this deck can't easily play around them, but if you have revealer, or any umi card then you can either make kairyu-shin or do full combo still.

Going Second:
Use Kairyu-shin / A Legendary Ocean as threats to wipe the opponent's board, use Ice Barriers as way to combo and partially wipe boards, Thrust can essentially get you to any card you need, If not locked into waters you should make Ty-Phon after Kairy-shin (likely on top of General) and bounce the opponent's last monster. THIS DECK CANNOT EASILY OTK, but it slows the game down to a point where you will kill them in 2 turns.
- for specific match-ups, side in Gameceil and/or Medium as additional targets to search off Goods.


WATER match-up (harder as kairyu-shin is way less powerful):
- Generally: side in Arms to make Reef two negates instead of 1, and side in Lancea and Georgius (for Prima Donna and Freezing Chains respectively) to increase the combo potential (and thus power) of your end board.
- Xyz and Synchro Decks (shark, goti, etc.): use A Legendary Ocean to mess levels up for them, and back it up with WATER Synchro's and Jellyfish/Arms.
- Link and other Decks (Atlantean, Marincess, etc.): Rely more on Lancea / Gergious as a way to box out the GY. 

Backrow Match-up:
- Use Medium of the Ice Barrier as your Floodgate of choice. This is also searchable/Summonable off Magellanca and Lancea going first, and goods (and thus Ice Barrier) going second. Use Aegirine to make board sticky and hard to remove.

deck_name: Light and Darkness Ritual with Regenesis
deck_description:
After testing, here are some notes about some good synergies:
- The regenesis engine loves all the 2500 monsters from the ROLAD engine (as well as the Fydraulis Harmonia )
- Since all of the Regenesis Monsters are level 7 or above, you can bounce them back into hand with the Mind Shuffle for cost.
- This also allows us to summon Dark Magician of Destruction by banishing the Magician of Dark Chaos - Black Chaos (shuffling it back into the deck from banishment with the Skull Archfiend of Chaos ). Since we still have targets to bounce back into hand and do not need the Chaos DM to be on the field on the opponent's turn.
- After you bounce the regenesis monster back into hand during the opponent's turn (usually Regenesis Sage ), you can also discard it for the Red-Eyes Dark Dragoon cost, and you refund it on your turn end

This means correct sequencing is the most important for maximum value. Also, watch out for extra deck locks with the Celtic Mystic  and Regenesis Code, when it actually matters.

The deck has some insane recursion, and usually on turn 3 (your crackback), you will win.

One card combos everywhere in this deck

 

Yuma would be proud

deck_name: J-Roc got caught with his birds out.
deck_excerpt: Winged-Beast deck that is as close as I've been able to get to making Floowandereeze work.
deck_description: I love birds! In Yu-Gi-Oh, the birds have been struggling lately (RADIANT TYPHOON IS NOT A BIRD DECK) and this is not my favorite Genesys deck, but you'll have a much better time in Genesys than you would trying to make it work in advanced.

Nothing set in stone quite yet (other than the Millennium monsters). The basic idea is to treat Millennium as a secondary rank 8 engine to set up Zombie Vampire to both fill your graveyard with Sennet cards and normal monsters, and also hopefully find Nefertari to get your plays started if necessary.

The Millennium engine is very versatile in this deck, getting you access to a normal monster on your field while dodging most conventional hand traps via Wedju Temple placing Millennium Golem face up directly from deck. Although the Sennet cards turn normal monsters into equip spells, they don't actually care what the normal monster is being treated as for the sake of their effects, so your normals being continuous spells makes no difference for your Ritual summons. As part of the combo to make Zombie Vampire, you also make Snake Eyes Doomed Dragon by sending two face up monster cards from your backrow. This can help get normals or even your ritual monsters in the gy without activating any effects from your Sennet engine if your opponent is primed to disrupt that half of your deck.

This is a temporary list I will be revisiting to adjust. Some non-engine or the ratios of the Millennium engine are likely to change.

deck_excerpt: Here's to 1 trillion more ways to search for Medius
deck_description:
One of the biggest boons that World Chalice has gotten in the past couple of months is the reveal of the Ars Magna cards. Because the Ars Magna archetype is centralized around its (current) boss monster of Medicurius the Power Patron of Illusions, a Link Monster, their Main Deck monsters are quite beneficial for most Link Summoning Decks as they can provide either extension or potential pieces of interruption for your endboard. For World Chalice, the biggest benefit that the Ars Magna cards give is even more consistent access to Medius the Pure in addition to sometimes acting as extenders. However, you do not need to always use the Ars Magna cards as extenders in every situation as whether or not you are even able to access them in the first place, you are still able to end on the same endboard. Prior to their reveal, Medius had cards like Artmage Vandalism -Assault-, Theorealize, and Prohibited Power Patron Portal - Terminus in order to immediately search for Medius in addition to just hard opening him. With the introduction of the Ars Magna cards, that number of searches has increased with Ars Magna of Infinity and Finity, Ars Magna of Purification and Corruption, and Ars Magna - “Citrinitas”. Elfnote Tinia also falls into this category of cards that can access or search Medius as she can access Citrinitas from Deck since it is always treated as an Elfnote card.

This Deck could’ve still existed and be played the exact same way prior to the release of the Ars Magna cards, instead replacing them with other Medius outlets like the ones stated earlier in addition to cards like Diviner of the Herald and Armageddon Knight or even Elemental HERO Prisma. The issue with this approach to the Deck, especially concerning the Normal Summons, is that you are less often able to bait out interaction before committing to a play. It doesn’t help that this Deck still folds to most forms of interaction at any point during its combo, but having the ability to test the waters first makes the Ars Magna version of the Deck feel a little better to play. Not to mention that the extension provided by the As Magna monsters can maybe help you recover mid-combo if met with a less detrimental form of interaction. So, how does Medius the Pure even enable World Chalice? Normal Summoning Medius and activating his effect on Summon to Special Summon Elfnote Power Patron from Deck immediately gives you the ability to make Ib the World Chalice Justiciar. But instead of using Ib to search for a World Legacy Spell or Trap, this method uses her to search for World Legacy - “World Chalice”, making it a much more consistent form of accessing World Chalice at the cost of taking up one of your potential searches for a World Legacy Spell or Trap instead. Elfnote Power Patron, if sent to GY as Synchro Material, searches for an Elfnote card from Deck. We are going to use this effect to search for one of the 3 Level 6 Elfnote monsters from BPRO: Elfnote Lucina, Elfnote Tinia, or Elfnote Fortuna. Each of them has their own merits. Lucina searches for an Elfnote monster, getting you one of the other two for another potential extender or piece of interaction. Tinia places an Elfnote Continuous Spell from Deck face-up on your field, which notably accesses Ars Magna - “Citrinitas” which would then allow you to access the Ars Magna monsters or search for a Medius for follow-up. Fortuna places an Elfnote Continuous Trap from hand or Deck face-up on your field, which can get Elfnotes: Rhapsodia of Madness, a card that can reborn an Elfnote monster from GY and then possibly negate the effects of a face-up card your opponent controls if you control a Synchro. Obviously, we are going to go with Tinia, but the other two have their merits in this Deck as well. Then, we can use Tinia and Ib to Link Summon Rahamu, Envoy of the Sacred Tome, who notably gives an extra Normal Summon of a Level 5 or higher DARK monster without Tributing, allowing you to Normal Summon World Chalice. From there, you can proceed like normal and build-up your endboard, which usually consists of 2 World Legacy - “World Chalice”, Lib the World Key Blademaster, S:P Little Knight, W:P Fancy Ball, and a set World Legacy Landmark. 

Your combo realistically only needs Medius the Pure, but be mindful of zones. Normal Summon Medius and activate his effect to Special Summon Elfnote Power Patron from Deck. Synchro them into Ib the World Chalice Justiciar (anywhere but the center Main Monster Zone). Chain Link 1 Ib on field and Chain Link 2 Power Patron in GY. Power Patron adds Elfnote Tinia from Deck to hand and Ib adds World Legacy - “World Chalice” from Deck to hand. Special Summon Tinia to the center MMZ and then activate her effect to place Ars Magna - “Citrinitas” from Deck face-up on your field. Use the effect of Citrinitas to add Ars Magna of Infinity and Finity from Deck to hand. Link Ib and Tinia into Rahamu, Envoy of the Sacred Tome into the right EMZ. Ib triggers in GY to Special Summon another World Legacy - “World Chalice” from Deck to the second from left MMZ. Activate Rahamu’s effect to extra Normal Summon the World Chalice in your hand without Tributing to the MMZ it points to. Link this Normal Summoned World Chalice into Galatea-I, the Orcust Automaton. World Chalice Triggers in GY and Special Summons any World Chalice Normal Monster from Deck to the center MMZ and Lee the World Chalice Fairy from Deck to the MMZ underneath Rahamu. Lee triggers on Summon to add World Chalice Guardragon from Deck to hand. Activate Galatea-I’s effect, sending the WC Guardragon in your hand to the GY, to add another World Legacy - “World Chalice” from Deck to hand. Link Galatea-I and Lee into Lib the World Key Blademaster to the zone Rahamu points to. Activate Lib’s effect to set World Legacy Landmark from Deck. Link the World Chalice Normal Monster you got into Imduk the World Chalice Dragon in the zone Lib points to. Link Rahamu and Imduk into W:P Fancy Ball into the left EMZ. Imduk triggers in GY to Special Summon the World Legacy - “World Chalice” in your hand to any zone (doesn’t matter anymore). Activate WC Guardragon in GY, banishing it and targeting the World Chalice Normal Monster in your GY and Special Summoning it to a zone your Link Monster points to. Link that Normal Monster into another Imduk the World Chalice Dragon. Activate Lee’s effect in GY to send a monster from your hand to the GY to add Lee back to hand. Extra Normal Summon Lee using Imduk and Link Lee and Imduk into S:P Little Knight. On your opponent’s turn, W:P Fancy Ball and use itself and Lib to Link Summon either Perfectron Hydradrive Dragon or Charmer Quartet in Bloom. Lib would then be able to spin a card your opponent controls into the Deck. If you make Perfectron Hydradrive Dragon, you can use Landmark to banish 1 of your World Chalices in order to bring back W:P Fancy Ball and either Tinia or the 3rd World Chalice in your GY. If you make Charmer Quartet, it can bring back W:P Fancy Ball and a World Legacy - “World Chalice” from GY. Then, you can later use Landmark to banish a World Chalice to bring back 2 monsters from your GY. 

If you want to see me discuss the cards that haven’t changed in the Deck, you can look at my previous lists (Radiant Typhoon World Chalice - Radiant Typhoon World Chalice Post LOCH/LOCR). Otherwise, I am just going to talk about cards that I cut as well as new additions.

For the Normal Monsters, I am playing 1 Chosen by the World Chalice and 1 Crowned by the World Chalice. It really does not matter what 2 World Chalice Normal Monsters you are playing. You could choose to instead play Beckoned by the World Chalice, 2 copies of the same vanilla, or any combination of them. Because we don’t need to use specifically Chosen by the World Chalice as a Level 3 to Synchro with Lee the World Chalice Fairy to make Ib the World Chalice Justiciar, all you really need is at least 1 vanilla in the Main Deck to Special Summon off of the effect of World Legacy - “World Chalice”. For Lee the World Chalice Fairy, she has been cut down to 2 copies. The Radiant Typhoon cards allowed you to put 2 bodies onto the field without using your Normal Summon in order to Link Summon Rahamu, Envoy of the Sacred Tome. Lee would then be your Normal Summon to search for World Chalice to then extra Normal without Tributing with Rahamu. Since this Deck’s entire combo starts with just Medius the Pure while still being able to access World Chalice, Lee doesn’t need to be played at 3 just to ensure that you have some way to search for World Chalice at the beginning of your combo. Similar to the World Chalice Normal Monsters, you just need at least 1 copy of her in Deck to Special Summon off of World Chalice and she would then search for World Chalice Guardragon as per usual. I have already made the case for why I am playing 1 Elfnote Tinia. She accesses Ars Magna - “Citrinitas”, which either then gives you access to Medius to start your combo or access to the Ars Magna monsters for extension. Again, there are arguments for playing either Elfnote Lucina or Elfnote Fortuna instead, but Tinia just has a bit more direct synergy than them. 3 Medius the Pure. You should know what he does by now, especially since Elfnote, DoomZ, and Artmage are still making the rounds. On Normal or Special Summon, he can add or Special Summon a Power Patron monster from Deck. If he’s in your GY, you can shuffle a monster from your hand or face-up field into the Deck and then Special Summon him, but he’s banished when he leaves the field. Genuinely such a great enabler for so many Decks, especially the archetypes that are a part of the Medius lore. Being able to bring him back from GY is also insanely useful. While it can allow him to act as a potential extender, it is more important for the fact that any card that can send him from Deck to GY acts as basically a searcher for him. 2 Elfnote Power Patron. You are Synchro-locked from the Extra Deck while you control it and if it is sent to the GY as Synchro Material, you can search for an Elfnote card from Deck. Those are the only 2 effects that are relevant in this Deck for the most part, especially that second effect. Being able to search for an Elfnote after Synchro Summoning just so happens to be what makes this Deck even function in the first place. Otherwise, we’d need to have an extender in addition to Medius in our opener. But, a Level 1 Tuner that can be Summoned from Deck off of Normal Summoning the Level 4 Medius is enough for me to like it. For the Ars Magna monsters, we are playing 3 each of Ars Magna of Infinity and Finity and Ars Magna of Purification and Corruption. I do think that the Ars Magna playstyle is kind of interesting, especially since the Main Deck monsters all enable something multiplied by 3 in relation to your Power Patron Link Monsters. However, for the purposes of this Deck, they are kind of just searchers and extenders. They can be used as interruption and this Deck does technically play the enablers for that, but it is more of a cute alternative play you can make in this Deck rather than something that is actually better than or similar in power to the endboard that we are already ending on. Each Level 8 Ars Magna monster can banish themselves from hand to do something in addition to affecting something related to Power Patron Link Monsters. You are almost never going to readily benefit from the Ars Magna monsters’ effects on Power Patron Links, so you are better off ignoring them for this Deck. Infinity and Finity banishes itself from hand to search for a non-Warrior Ars Magna from Deck. Purification and Corruption searches for an Ars Magna Spell/Trap from Deck instead. They also can Special Summon themselves from banishment if a certain Extra Deck Monster Type is Special Summoned: Fusion or Links for Infinity and Finity and Synchro or Links for Purification and Corruption. Lastly, each of them have a unique 3rd effect that requires you to control a Power Patron Link Monster. Infinity and Finity targets and banishes a monster on field while Purification and Corruption targets and banishes Spell/Traps on the field up to the number of your Power Patron Link Monsters. These 3rd effects can be used as Quick Effect pieces of interruption with Ars Magna - “Citrinitas” while you control a Power Patron Link Monster, and this Deck has the ability to set this up. But again, it is just something cute you can do rather than a more powerful or equally as powerful alternative to your normal endboard. 

For Spell/Traps, the new additions are 3 Theorealize and 3 Ars Magna - “Citrinitas”. Theorealize sends Medius the Pure from Deck to GY as cost and then allows you to look at the top card of your opponent’s Deck and either place it on the top or bottom of the Deck. Other than being a way to dump Medius from Deck to GY without worrying about Ash Blossom & Joyous Spring or Dominus Purge, it gives you a peek at what your opponent is going to draw, allowing you to bottom an important piece of engine or a harmful piece of nonengine breaker. Not that crazy, but it is something cute and potentially impactful that you are able to do. You can also banish it from GY and target a face-up monster you control, declaring a Monster Type and Attribute, making that monster become that Type and Attribute until the end of your opponent’s turn. Can’t really make use of this effect in this Deck since I am not playing any of the Theorealize searchers to really make seeking out this card for this effect any more meaningful. However, in testing, I did find a use for it, which is that when your Elfnote Power Patron’s GY effect gets negated and you have Theorealize in GY, you can banish it targeting Ib the World Chalice Justiciar and declare LIGHT Fiend in order to be able to Link her off into Fiendsmith’s Requiem. Requiem and the World Chalice monster that Ib floats into would then be able to make Rahamu, Envoy of the Sacred Tome to extra Normal Summon without Tributing your World Chalice. Not that you are always going to be able to do this though since, again, we aren’t playing any of the Theorealize searchers and quite frankly, if your opponent is going to Ash something, it’s probably going to be Medius and not the GY effect of your Power Patron. Citrinitas is always treated as an Artmage, DoomZ, and Elfnote card, which is cool for making it very searchable. It turns your Ars Magna monsters’ effects into Quick Effects while you control a Power Patron Link Monster, and once while it is face-up on the field, you can add a Medius or Ars Magna monster from Deck to hand. Being able to either use this as a Medius searcher to get to your starter or use it as an Ars Magna searcher for extension is pretty cool in the context of this Deck. Not too crazy about turning your Ars Magna monsters’ effects into Quick Effects since this isn’t an Ars Magna Deck, so you can’t make use of this effect’s maximum potential. 

Not too much different in the Extra Deck. I already explained the usage for Charmer Quartet in Bloom earlier. As for Purification Power Patron, if you finagle some cards, you are able to end on this card instead of S:P Little Knight, which would then allow you to World Legacy Landmark back Ars Magna of Infinity and Finity and/or Ars Magna of Purification and Corruption from GY to use them as pieces of interruption if you also control Ars Magna - “Citrinitas” since Purification Power Patron is a Power Patron Link Monster. The other Power Patron Link Monster we are playing is Medicurius the Power Patron of Illusions. I don’t think that it is as crazy as a lot of people were saying it was on reveal. It requires 2+ monsters including a Fusion, Synchro, Xyz, or Link as material and it gains effects based on the number of monsters it points to. 1+ gives him the ability to negate the effects of all face-up monsters your opponent currently controls, also halving their ATK until the end of the turn. 2+ makes it unaffected by your opponent’s activated effects. 3 allows you to, once per opponent’s turn, Quick Effect banish him and all cards your opponent controls. This Deck can easily make Medicurius and have him pointing to 3 monsters, but the issue is that you frequently need to shotgun his effect to banish himself and your opponent’s field as your first piece of interruption. Your monsters that this card is likely to point to either have Quick Effects to perform a Link Summon or Tribute themselves to activate their effect, so keeping Medicurius pointing to 3 monsters to wait for the perfect time to banish your opponent’s board is a bit difficult to do optimally. Still, you can still make him and do all of this. It’s not the worst thing this Deck can end on. Also, if you really wanted to, you can give him more usage in the Extra Deck by playing Prohibited Power Patron Portal - Terminus and sending him from Extra Deck to GY to be able to search for Medius. 

Side Deck: 3 Gameciel, the Sea Turtle Kaiju, 3 Mulcharmy Fuwalos, 2 Pot of Sloth, 1 Harpie’s Feather Duster, 2 Triple Tactics Thrust, 1 Triple Tactics Talent, and 3 Evenly Matched. Kind of just all cards to side in going second to ensure that you are able to get to your, otherwise very interruptable, plays.

deck_description: Budget Version until I can afford Fydraulis

I created this deck for sea-themed deck enthusiasts. It’s basically the standard Atlantean Mermail combo, save for a few minor details. Once Poseidra Abyss is sent to the Graveyard, we summon two Level 3 monsters and Deep Sea Diva to then summon Rise. At this point, we have Abyss-Sting in hand and Coelicanth with its effect still available. We activate its effect to summon two Abyss-Spikes and a Greethys. We summon Monoceros and use its effect to revive Greethys from the Graveyard. We summon Abyssgaios using Coelicanth and Monoceros. We activate Greethys' effect to summon Deep Sea Diva from the Graveyard (if the opponent doesn't have enough cards in hand, don't summon anything—especially not Prince). Using Greethys and the last Abyss-Spike, summon Deep Sea Prima Donna; use her effect to return the card you banished with Deep Sea Sentinel to the opponent's hand and summon Abyssturge, whose effect lets you add Prince back to your hand. Next, use Abyss-Sting to revive one of the Level 3 monsters from your Graveyard and summon Lancea using that monster and Deep Sea Prima Donna. With Abyssturge and Deep Sea Diva (if you managed to summon it), you can choose to make either Coral Dragon or Abyssalacia. Now, during your opponent's turn, you can summon Trishula and/or Raiho, as well as Poseidra, by discarding Prince via the effect of Abysshrine.

!! Place Rise, Lancea, and Abyssgaios in the zones Neptabyss points to so it can protect them !!

deck_excerpt: Hey Konami, surely I can be trusted with Instant Fusion :3
deck_description:
As of July 2026, Masterduel has unleashed Instant Fusion into ladder by making the card limited to 1! Since then I knew I IMMEDIATELY had to play it in the best deck that it can be used it - Tearlaments! Instant Fusion immediately summons Kitkallos, supercharging your plays with mills, resources and advantage! Thanks for trusting us milling addicts with Instant Fusion Konami :3

Featuring their signature playstyle of milling to proc their graveyard effects, Tearlaments has nearly unlimited amounts of gas because of the strength of it's own archetypes! The Horus and Fiendsmith archetypes also offer incredible support thanks to the XYZ milling powerhouses like The Zombie Vampire (rank 8) and Pilgrim Reaper (rank 6). 

The ability of the Fiendsmith engine to easily link summon A Bao A Qu, The Lightless Shadow has been quite a boon for Tearlaments, since it gives the strategy usage out of the extra monsters it has on the field while it can reborn a light/dark in the GY or destroy a card! Also, if you manage to enter the next turn with A Bao A Qu intact, you're basically drawing 10+ cards with her, effectively winning you the game with such incredible follow-up. 

In this Kewl Tunes infested Masterduel meta, Tearlaments actually can play into their low impact handtraps and negates/pops due to the immense gas the strategy has. Super Polymerization also can smash through a Kewl Tunes board, enabling your milling to go through uninterrupted. Overall, I've found the milling with Tearlaments very fun, dopamine inducing and surprisingly powerful in my climb to Masters this season. Hopefully the same for yo too! And remember... 

TIARAMENTS STRONGEST~

GLHF Duelists! 

Want to see the deck in action? Scroll to the very bottom for the Youtube video!

deck_description: Casual old school GX era armed dragon deck, I've used in in Yu-Gi-Oh GX Tag Force 1

Fun lil suship deck for genesys that can play fairly low to the ceiling. Idea is to have the game wrapped up by turn 3 with a decent powered endboard even for genesys and decent OTK ability. Has the potential to end on UDF, Drillbeam, Giant Hand or Tornado Dragon, plus with the ability to draw off Suship XYZ, can often have a handtrap or a Cahlice as well. Theres probably a better way to distribute points, I'm not a genesys expert so theres probably a more optimal spread of points that allow for better cards.If 7th Tachyon ever gets pointed more than 5 points or so, this whole deck will need revising.

Other ideas for Sennet decks (e.g. Pumpking hybrid) played a lot of extra bricks that the deck cannot really afford too. So instead this version has the Striker cards to get to Cyberse Contract Witch, granting full combo without the Normal Summon and a total of thirteen starters.

A lot of Spells are played to facilitate the effect of Witch, though non-engine will of course vary based on personal preference and format. The Primite spells are useful to add an extra disruption and Normal body at the end of your turn.

Harmonia and Dragon Core Hexer can allow for access to Level 11/12 Synchro monsters with Nefertari if the game goes on long enough.

The side deck here just shows other options: the brickier Sennet cards can be played, but currently don't have much value. Nekhbet is probably the most useful if a grindy game is anticipated since it can retrieve cards, but you will never want to see Amenhotep over Nefertari. Am Duasennet is the weakest of the three traps (and thus the one you are least likely to search and set), so is cut just to avoid bricking on it.

The other cards in the side deck are ones you should play in formats where they are legal: Arc Light acting as an extra starter off Harmonia, and BLS (the Normal version - which cannot be displayed here, so the image is of the Ritual instead as a placeholder) is a better Normal monster than Blue-Eyes since it cannot be Bystial'd and is harder to Super Poly.
Use Kuribohs and other level one monsters to summon powerful monsters like Accesscode Talker, Zeus, or Kuribabylon. Let me know how I can improve it.

deck_excerpt: Ancient Gear Deck with Dreadnought tech
deck_description:
Schedule can allow you to easily get access to your powerful monsters - with it being possible to have Ultimate Golem, Chaos Giant and Liebe off just it if you go uninterrupted.

Blue Traveler is great on its own as a way to bring back a monster, but is especially good with Schedule. For example; with Schedule you can summon it and Statue, link them into Genius, then re-summon them to Genius' pointed zones - giving you a search, plus having Statue online, as well as enough material to make Ballista.

Traveler can also act as a 1.5 card combo by searching Switchyard, which searches Babeldecker, which is summoned and linked off for Clockwork, allowing Traveler to summon itself and Babel.

Ironwolf is easy to make and goes into ZEUS if needed, or can be used to allow Ultimate Golem to attack directly multiple times for game.

If preferred to you can opt out for any of the Rank 10 Dreadnoughts (aside from Flying Launcher) in order to play a copy of Dugares, who is useful in a lot of scenarios.

Loaner deck from the July 2026 Fusion x Xyz Festival+ event.

Official description:
Difficulty ☆1
A Deck which overpowers your opponent through fierce attacks.

First, use the effect of a Spell Card such as "Urgent Schedule" or a Machine Type EARTH monster to deploy Level 10 monsters, and then Xyz Summon "Superdreadnought Rail Cannon Flying Launcher".
The effect of "Superdreadnought Rail Cannon Flying Launcher" not only adds 1 Machine Type EARTH monster to your hand, but also summons 1 Machine Type monster when you Normal Summon/Set, letting you quickly build your monster ranks.
After clearing the stage with the effect destroying "Superdreadnought Rail Cannon Flying Launcher" and "Heavy Freight Train Derricrane", shave off your opponent's LP with the colossal Xyz Monster's attacks or "Superdreadnought Rail Cannon Gustav Max" card's Effect Damage.

deck_excerpt: Fairy Tail × Charmers – Draw, Control, and Overwhelm
deck_description:
This deck combines the new Fairy Tail support with the classic Charmer/Possessed engine to create a resource-heavy control strategy that snowballs every turn. The Fairy Tail monsters provide consistent extension and recovery, while the Charmers reward you for controlling multiple Attributes with powerful utility and board presence.

The heart of the deck is Awakening of the Possessed. With Fairy Tail and Charmer monsters sharing 1850 ATK, each Special Summon can trigger Awakening's draw effect while also increasing the ATK of your entire field by 300 for every different Attribute you control. Multiple copies of Awakening stack, turning a board of modest Spellcasters into massive threats capable of ending games in a single Battle Phase.

Possessed Partnerships and Spirit Charmers keep the engine running by reviving monsters, generating card advantage, and destroying opposing cards while naturally assembling multiple Attributes. Fairy Tail Ball provides an unexpected win condition by allowing direct attacks, often catching opponents off guard once your monsters have been powered up by multiple Awakenings.

This isn't a traditional combo deck or a negate-heavy strategy. Instead, it focuses on constant card advantage, recursive plays, and overwhelming pressure. Every turn builds on the last, forcing your opponent to deal with an ever-growing board of Spellcasters that refuse to stay down.

If you enjoy grind games, creative lines, and winning through synergy rather than a single boss monster, this deck offers a unique take on both the Fairy Tail and Charmer archetypes.

deck_excerpt: Might be the Jujutsu Kaisen deck
deck_description:
Everyone is here to get your Synchro monsters out, mess with the field, loop your graveyard, and play hijinks with the banishment. I don't care that it isn't "meta". When you have a trap that just flat-out stops your opponent from special summoning, you can handle a little bit of rogue deck.

If you aren't familiar with how this deck works, it's like this. 

Summon Hajun, Hajun summons Dakki, Dakki Synchros and then loops itself back onto the field where it can do that infinitely (or as long as you summon mayakashi monsters from the extra deck.) Once you get to Gashadokuro, you can start Link summoning. Now, you have a loaded GY that is prepared to jump your opponent's the second the monster above them gets destroyed.

Meanwhile, Shiranui just makes it even easier to recall your Mayakashi and just provides strong bodies on the field.

The deck does struggle with Ash Blossom. Therefore, have Called by the Grave and Crossout prepared just for it.

GL;HF duelists.

deck_excerpt: Tired of "Lose the coinflip, lose the duel?" This deck aims to fix that!
deck_description:
It's no secret Yu-Gi-Oh's metagame currently heavily favors whoever goes first. So why not try to combat that? This deck aims to break your opponent going second while also being able to use classic Fiendsmith combos, and also a nice synergy with the LADR engine.

"What is Heavy Polymerization doing here?"

Heavy Polymerization  is ANOTHER one-card Fiendsmith engine starter, with this combo:

Banish Evil, add Void Apocalypse, send Archfiend Heiress, add Usurpation, set Playtime, summon The Light Fiend... annnd go from there. All without your normal summon. It definitely is a bit bricky, but having access to a very quick Aeon that also starts your combo is very useful, plus, another card in the archetype just so happens to have Archfiend in its name...

This deck is good, obviously. It's two malleable engines mixed into one big viable deck, but still keeping an air of "I am trying for my deck to have a unique feel to it and not just running "Sky Striker Invoked Yummy Fiendsmith Etc."

deck_name: Light and Darkness Ritual, feat. Regenesis and The Smiths
deck_description:
Still testing out options.
Something that I was just messing with and did not think would work at all, but it can sometimes actually work:
Light and Darkness Ritual, Regenesis, Fiendsmith! (with a bit of Unchained)
Lots of fiends with lots of synergies. It is usually possible to bridge from one engine into another during the game, so if one of your engines is stopped, you can continue with another.
The issue is, of course, a small amount of non-engine. But these engines like to also board break while establishing themselves.
Tour Guide From the Underworld is your key to access all 3 engines:
- Doomdog Octhros summon and link off, to get to Regenesis Archfiend 
- Buio the Dawn's Light summon and XYZ into Melomelody the Brass Djinn, to get to Fiendsmith
- Access any fiend from the deck (all 3 engines):

Tour guide > SS Buio
Send both to make Unchained Soul Lord of Yama
Buio add Mutinity, Yama add Unchained Soul of Sharvara
Mutinity make Aerial Eater with Guide and Buio, and mill the fiend monster
Aerial eater + Lord Yama = Unchained Soul of Rage
Sharvara SS destroying Soul of Rage
Soul of Rage chain link 1 add the milled fiend, Yama chain link 2 remove to revive a fiend (Soul of Rage for an interruption or Aerial Eater to make Caesar with Sharvara).

Also, you can end in the same set up with Fiendsmith, starting with Engraver or Tract, and without using the normal summon,
Standard Fiendsmith combo starting with Tract until you have Lacrima and Engraver in the field
Tract GY effect, remove to make Lacrima fusion with both, Lacrima revives any of the 2.
Lacrima + the other monster = Sequence
Sequence uses Lacrima main deck and Requiem to make Aerial Eater, Aerial Eater mills the fiend monster that you can add.
Aerial Eater + Sequence = Lord Yama. Lord Yama add Sharvara, Aerial Eater revives removing Lacrima + Engraver.
The same combo than before making Soul of rage after this point.

If you have your Regenesis engine set up, you can banish the Magician of Dark Chaos - Black Chaos after summon, for the Dark Magician of Destruction. Since you can return to hand Regenesis monsters to summon out the Black Luster Soldier - Soldier of Light and Darkness.

Small world also has some nice ways to bridge with the extra cards in hand (also, remember not to banish your "1 of" engine pieces with it). Here is a cheat sheet:

https://imgur.com/a/19yj764

(generated via: https://x-x-i-l.github.io/smallworld/)

deck_description: The deck used by Yugi in the third Yugioh videogame for the Game Boy: Dark Duel Stories.

A Fluffal engine helps you draw through your deck. A Crystron engine makes synchros and provides targeted removal through Crystron Cluster.

Crystron Cluster can shuffle Crystron cards, except Crystron Cluster, from your graveyard or banished zone back into your deck to target and destroy that many cards on the field.

Crystron Smiger can banish itself from your graveyard to search for Crystron Cluster. Crystron Thystvern can banish itself from your graveyard to search for Smiger. These graveyard effects kickstart your Crystron engine.

Alternatively, Smiger and Thystvern can also target a face-up card you control, destroy it, and special summon the tuner monsters, Crystron Quan or Crystron Rion, from your deck. You'd want to destroy Toy Vendor with these effects since its graveyard effect can search for a Fluffal monster.

A short explanation of how the Fluffal engine works can be seen in this Reddit thread:

https://www.reddit.com/r/yugioh/comments/7syrsp/questioncan_someone_explain_to_me_how_to_run_a/

deck_excerpt: IT'S TIME TO FREEZE THIS FESTIVAL!!!
deck_description:
The Ice Barrier Dragons are basically at full power in this year's theme chronicle event, so it's time to FREEZE THIS FESTIVAL!!! 

The deck's entire gameplan revolves around summoning it's incredibly powerful boss monster, Lancea, Ancestral Dragon of the Ice Mountain. It can summon up to twice per turn Ice Barriers from hand, deck, extra deck of GY - and Ice Barrier has some pretty nasty floodgates that are guaranteed to freeze your opponent's gameplan solid! General Raiho is a field wide monster negate and handloop in one card, while Medium of the Ice Barrier limits your opponent to a singular spell per turn! 

This decklist is fairly consistent, with 11 one-card starters (x2 Hexa Spirit, x3 Revealer, x3 Ran Aegirine, x3 Medallion) and with multiple 2+ card starters when combining cards engine cards together. Ice Barrier's inherent weakness going second is fixed by the 18 handtraps used in this strategy. A certain combo also involves drawing 4 cards, so you can draw into a ton of handtraps while summoning floodgates to the field! 

GLHF Duelists! 

Want to see the deck in action? Scroll to the very bottom for the Youtube video!

Category: Tournament Meta Decks (TCG)

Tournament Meta Decks are current-level Meta Decks that are capable of going up against the best of the best. These are curated lists that cannot be uploaded by regular users.

Creator: Matthieu Bricard

Tournament: European WCQ 2026 – July 11th 2026

Superheavy Samurai Prodigy Wakaushi - Zombies

 

You can use Superheavy Samurai Prodigy Wakaushi, and Superheavy Samurai Soulgaia Booster to summon Chaos Ruler, the Chaotic Magical Dragon. You can then activate Superheavy Samurai Prodigy Wakaushi as a scale. So you have a guaranteed mill 5 without using your normal summon, and a free pendulum summon with scales 1-8.

If you have any effect that steals a monster from your opponent (Triple Tactics Talent, Change of Heart, Snatch Steal, Mind Control, Illusion Gate, Dharc the Dark Charmer, Gloomy), you can use the stolen monster and Chaos Ruler, the Chaotic Magical Dragon to summon The Zombie Vampire and mill an extra 4 cards. That means you have access to 1/4 of your deck. If that's not enough, you can also summon Tearlaments Kitkallos using Magical Scientist or Instant Fusion and mill another 8 with Tearlaments Merrli.

 

Alternatives:

Mitsurugi engine: Instead of using Chaos Ruler, the Chaotic Magical Dragon and stealing effects, you could run the Mitsurugi cards and access them through King of the Feral Imps, and summon The Zombie Vampire using the ritual monsters. That has the advantage of one extra omninegate, and one interaction to destroy all monsters one of your opponent controls. But you mill fewer cards, and the Mitsurugi cards use your board space, which is very important in this deck.

Brain Control: Another steal effect. By adding this one, you have a 48% chance to have a steal effect in your starting hand + draw.

Dragonecro Nethersoul Dragon: Alternative Super Polymerization target.

deck_description:
⭐ White Forest × Angelechy | Competitive Synchro | TCG 2026

🧩 Deck Concept

White Forest × Angelechy is a Synchro-focused strategy that combines the consistency of the White Forest engine with the control power of Angelechy monsters.

The White Forest cards generate advantage by sending Spells and Traps to the GY, enabling easy extension and recursive plays. The Angelechy engine then converts those resources into powerful Synchro Monsters capable of establishing multiple interruptions while maintaining excellent follow-up.

The result is a deck that can consistently produce strong end boards while still having enough resources to grind through longer games.

⚙️ Core Strategy

  • 🌲 White Forest Engine: Provides consistency, resource generation, and easy access to your combo pieces.
  • ♟️ Angelechy Engine: Converts your setup into powerful Synchro summons and layered interactions.
  • ⭐ Diabellstar Package: Adds flexibility and improves access to key cards.
  • 🛡️ Hand Traps: Fuwalos, Ash Blossom, Infinite Impermanence, and Dominus Spark help control the opponent before your combo begins.

🎯 Win Condition

Going first, the deck aims to establish multiple Synchro Monsters backed up by traps and hand traps, forcing the opponent through several layers of interaction.

Going second, the deck can play through disruption thanks to the recursive White Forest engine and efficient extenders before breaking established boards and applying pressure.

💡 Why Play White Forest × Angelechy?

  • ✅ Extremely consistent combo lines.
  • ✅ Strong one-card starters.
  • ✅ Excellent resource management.
  • ✅ Powerful Synchro toolbox for multiple matchups.
  • ✅ Competitive TCG build optimized for the current format.

deck_excerpt: Using Crimson Blade Dragon as a Metalmorph enabler
deck_description:
Red Dragon Archfiend and Metalmorph are 2 archetypes that you wouldn’t really think that they work together too well. And there is some merit to that thought. With the sheer amount of Red Dragon Archfiend and Resonator support that has been released over the years, Red Dragon Archfiend is a pretty competent Deck, albeit kinda one note since it is more or less another Synchro Spam Deck. On the other hand, while Metalmorph is a fully capable archetype, it isn’t the most explosive Deck ever. However, Metalmorph gets better when paired alongside other archetypes. While you can play Metalmorph as its own Deck, the addition of another archetype’s engine can allow for things like greater consistency, a more varied endboard, and possibly even better options either going first or second. Combining Metalmorph with Red Dragon Archfiend only improves the Metalmorph side to a certain degree. It will certainly not be as explosive or perform to the same degree as an actual Red Dragon Archfiend Deck. However, considering the non-engine slots that the Deck has available, it is still decently well-rounded.

The thing that connects Red Dragon Archfiend and Metalmorph is Crimson Blade Dragon. It is a Level 7 Synchro Monster that requires a Resonator Tuner and 1+ non-Tuners. Its name is always treated as Crimson Blader and if it is Synchro Summoned, you can take a Level 8 or higher monster that cannot be Normal Summoned/Set from your Deck or GY and either add it to hand or Special Summon it with its effects negated. Red-Eyes Black Fullmetal Dragon and Metalzoa X are both Level 8 monsters that cannot be Normal Summoned/Set and both have the effect of revealing themselves in your hand to set a Metalmorph Trap from your Deck, shuffling themselves into the Deck afterwards. If you Synchro Summon Crimson Blade Dragon, you can search for either Fullmetal Dragon or Metalzoa X, use it to set Max Metalmorph from Deck, and then, you also have a Level 5 or higher Dragon monster on field to Tribute for Max Metalmorph to Special Summon Red-Eyes Black Fullmetal Dragon from wherever. Again, this does not produce the same explosivity as RDA, but it does help Metalmorph put a Level 5 or higher Dragon readily on the field while also allowing you to further benefit off of the RDA-related cards, which you are able to access since you are playing the searchers and enablers for them. 

For the Main Deck, we are playing 1 Red-Eyes Black Fullmetal Dragon, 1 Metalzoa X, and 1 Metalflame Swordsman for our Metalmorph-related monsters. While Zoa the Fiendish Beast may mostly be replaceable by Red-Eyes Metal Claws Dragon in a Metalmorph Deck, there is nothing wrong with also playing Red-Eyes Metal Claws Dragon or even Metal Illusionist in this Deck. However, I am choosing deliberately not to play them. Metal Claws Dragon is the in-archetype way for Metalmorph to be able to easily put a Level 5 or higher Dragon onto the field while also being able to immediately use Metalmorph Traps the turn they are set, essentially giving you an omni-negate with Fullmetal Dragon as soon as Turn 1. I don’t think that it is necessarily bad to be playing Metal Claws Dragon in this Deck, but the idea behind this Deck is more focused on using the Resonator and/or RDA cards to access the Metalmorph cards alongside other useful RDA-related cards in the process. Metal Claws Dragon kind of favors an opening hand with a greater number of Metalmorph-related cards, which this Deck tries to avoid more or less. In a Deck with a heavier Metalmorph package or a Metalmorph Deck playing Red-Eyes searchers, Metal Claws Dragon would be more at home. As for Metal Illusionist, it acts like more of a Normal Summon going first. However, when it comes to the Normal Summons that we are already playing in this Deck, Incredible Ecclesia, the Virtuous and Fallen of the White Dragon are much better cards to be taking up your Normal Summon for the turn. Back to Fullmetal Dragon, Metalzoa X, and Metalflame Swordsman, each of them must first be Special Summoned with Max Metalmorph that was activated by Tributing a Level 5 or higher monster with their original counterpart’s Type: Dragon for Fullmetal, Fiend for Metalzoa X, and Warrior for Metalflame Swordsman. Each can also reveal themselves in the hand to do something before shuffling themselves back into the Deck afterwards. Fullmetal and Metalzoa both set a Metalmorph Trap from Deck while Metalflame adds 1 card that mentions Max Metalmorph from Deck to hand other than another copy of itself. Lastly, each has a unique effect on the field. Fullmetal can Quick Effect negate the activation of an opponent’s card or effect and then you can inflict damage to them equal to the original ATK of 1 Attack Position monster they control. Metalzoa X can twice per turn Quick Effect target and destroy a face-up monster your opponent controls when they activate a monster effect or Spell Card or effect. Metalflame gains 300 ATK and inflicts 500 damage to your opponent every time your opponent activates and resolves a card or effect. Metalzoa X and Fullmetal are great pieces of interruption while Metalflame kind of just grows bigger and keeps on damaging your opponent the more they activate and resolve cards and effects. Not to say that Metalflame’s effect on field is bad, but it has more limited application than Fullmetal or Metalzoa X. All of their in-hand effects are good, and while it can be argued that Metalflame’s is the best, we are kind of just using their effects, Metalflame included, to search/set a Metalmorph Trap from Deck. If we were playing Metal Claws Dragon or Metal Illusionist, Metalflame Swordsman’s in-hand effect would have more usage and it would probably warrant running 3 or it instead. 3 Power Vice Dragon and 2 Vision Resonator. If you control no monsters or all monsters you control are DARK Synchros, you can Special Summon Power Vice Dragon from hand, and if it is Special Summoned, you can add a Resonator monster from Deck to your hand, locking you into DARK Synchros from the Extra Deck for the rest of the turn. Power Vice Dragon is an excellent card for supporting both RDA and Resonators. And because of how the good Resonators are all of different useful Levels (from 1 to 3), you can pick and choose which one of them to grab and possibly even then Synchro Summon with Power Vice. But, because Power Vice is a Level 5 and we need to access the Level 7 Crimson Blade Dragon, Vision Resonator is the best option, especially in a Deck that actually isn’t RDA. You can Special Summon Vision from your hand once per turn if there is a Level 5 or higher DARK monster on the field and if it is sent to the GY, you can add a Spell/Trap that mentions RDA from Deck to hand. Power Vice and Vision Resonator essentially make Crimson Blade Dragon without using your Normal Summon, which is nice. But, Vision Resonator also can search for an RDA-related Spell/Trap, for which our options are Crimson Gaia, for recursion and a battle trick, or Red Dragon Archfiend’s Chain, which can either function as a good piece of monster negation or a nice boardbreaker of sorts. For our Normal Summons, we have 2 Fallen of the White Dragon and 3 Incredible Ecclesia, the Virtuous. Fallen of the White Dragon is always treated as Fallen of Albaz and he can also send a monster that mentions Fallen of Albaz from your Extra Deck to the GY to Special Summon himself from hand, locking you into Level 8 Fusion and Synchro Monsters from the Extra Deck for the rest of the turn. If Normal or Special Summoned, he can Special Summon an Ecclesia monster from your hand, Deck, or GY. Incredible Ecclesia can be Special Summoned from your hand once per turn if your opponent controls more monsters than you do. During the Main Phase, you can Quick Effect Tribute her to Special Summon a Swordsoul monster or a Fallen of Albaz from hand or Deck and during the End Phase, if a Fusion Monster(s) was sent to your GY this turn, you can add her from GY to your hand. Incredible Ecclesia and Fallen of the White Dragon are just really great cards for accessing Level 8 Synchro Monsters. In this Deck, they will mainly be used to access The Crimson King who searches a card that mentions RDA on Synchro Summon, getting you to Power Vice Dragon to start your combo. However, if you already have access to Power Vice Dragon in one way or another, they can be used to access other Level 8 Synchro Monsters like Stardust Dragon - Victim Sanctuary going first or Scarlight Red Dragon Archfiend going second. A note on Fallen of the White Dragon’s effect to Special Summon himself from hand: if you want to use this effect, you have to do it after making Crimson Blade Dragon due to the restriction. As for the handtraps, we have a ton of room to play non-engine: 3 Fydraulis Harmonia, 3 Mulcharmy Fuwalos, 3 Ash Blossom & Joyous Spring, and 2 Droll & Lock Bird. If you don’t want to be playing Harmonia because of its price, which might remain pretty high depending on how it is reprinted in the future, you can absolutely play Ghost Ogre & Snow Rabbit or Ghost Belle & Haunted Mansion instead. 

For Spell/Traps: 3 Incoming Machine! It adds a monster that mentions Max Metalmorph or a Metalmorph Trap from your Deck or GY to your hand. I’d rather use this as the searcher for Metalmorph-related cards instead of playing 3 Metalflame Swordsman because Incoming Machine also allows you to add them back from GY. Its second effect allows you to banish it from GY while you control a Metalmorph Trap and target a Defense Position monster your opponent controls to change it to face-up Attack Position. This is a pretty niche effect in this Deck. It can absolutely be used to go for damage, but Red Dragon Archfiend and most of its related cards focus on your opponent having Defense Position monsters in order to make use of RDA’s effect to destroy all Defense Position monsters your opponent controls after damage calculation. Just depends on the situation and what you are working with. 3 Crimson Gaia. During your Main Phase, it can add a RDA or a card that mentions it from Deck or GY to hand except another copy of Crimson Gaia. This is the main usage for this card in this Deck, but its other effects are still plenty useful. When your RDA declares an attack, you can change all monsters your opponent controls to face-down Defense Position and if a monster(s) on the field is destroyed by battle or card effect, you can Special Summon an RDA from your GY. 1 Foolish Burial which sends Vision Resonator from Deck to GY, getting Crimson Gaia, and then getting you to Power Vice Dragon. 3 The Fallen & The Virtuous. You can either send a monster that mentions Fallen of Albaz from your Extra Deck to the GY and target and destroy a face-up card on the field or if you have an Ecclesia monster in your field or GY, you can target a monster in either GY and Special Summon it to your field. Just a really good piece of non-engine, and because we are playing an Ecclesia monster in the Deck, this card has even more potential applications. 1 Called by the Grave. 3 Red Dragon Archfiend’s Chain. You can activate it the turn it was set by revealing an RDA in your Extra Deck. On activation, you reveal any number of monsters in your hand and target Effect Monsters your opponent controls equal to the number of Tuners revealed +1. Those monsters lose 100 ATK for each revealed card and their effects are negated. If all of the monsters affected by RDA’s Chain are no longer on the field, RDA’s Chain is destroyed. What an amazing card. Not only is this comically easy to search in RDA, it basically seconds as a non-engine boardbreaker that can be played in most Decks. But, it gets even better here because it is searchable and can be used as interruption as well. 1 Max Metalmorph which Tributes a face-up monster to Special Summon 1 monster that cannot be Normal Summoned/Set and mentions Max Metalmorph from your hand, Deck, or GY. Max Metalmorph can then be equipped to it, giving it 400 ATK/DEF, protection from being destroyed by Spell or monster effects, and your opponent cannot target it with monster effects or Spell Cards or effects. Just a really good card that not only accesses good monsters, it provides them with great protection as well. 1 Flame Coating Metalmorph which Tributes a face-up monster that mentions Max Metalmorph to Special Summon a monster that cannot be Normal Summoned/Set and mentions Max Metalmorph from your Deck, ignoring its Summoning Conditions. Then, you can equip it to the Summoned monster, giving it the effect that if it would be destroyed by battle or card effect, you can send Flame Coating Metalmorph to GY instead. This was actually a great addition to the Metalmorph archetype. It essentially tags any monster that mentions Max Metalmorph into one of their monsters that cannot be Normal Summoned/Set. This means that  you can tag out a spent Metalmorph monster into a different one, ignoring its Summoning Conditions. If you were wondering how we’d get to Metalzoa X or Metalflame Swordsman in this Deck, this is the way: by tagging out Red-Eyes Black Fullmetal Dragon into one of them with Flame Coating Metalmorph. 

Extra Deck: 1 Albion the Branded Dragon and 1 The Dragon that Devours the Dogma as our Fusion Monsters that mention Fallen of Albaz to be sent off of The Fallen & The Virtuous. Albion can set a Branded Spell/Trap from Deck if it is in the GY during the End Phase of the turn it was sent there and Dragon Devouring the Dogma can add a Dogmatika or Tri-Brigade card from Deck to your hand under the same pretenses. 1 Red Nova Dragon - Burning Soul. While it is a Level 12 Synchro Monster that requires 2 Tuners and 1 non-Tuner, you can also Special Summon it from your Extra Deck by banishing 2 Tuners and Red Dragon Archfiend from your GY. It cannot be destroyed by card effects and as a hard once per Duel in which you have Synchro Summoned RDA, if Red Nova Dragon - Burning Soul is Special Summoned, you can add 1 card from your GY to your hand, and if you do, it gains 2000 ATK. Adding a card back from GY and becoming a 5500 ATK monster that cannot be destroyed by card effects is so good. Not only do you have an indestructible huge beater, you also get to add a card back from GY. Ideally, you’d want to add back some kind of non-engine card that could immediately be used, but grabbing any card for potential follow-up if you can’t win this turn is still good. 1 Chaos Angel. This realistically could also be Hot Red Dragon Archfiend Bane or Bystial Dis Pater, but I just felt like as a follow-up option, Vision Resonator Synchroing with a Level 8 LIGHT or DARK Synchro Monster into Chaos Angel for a monster with good ATK and removal on-Summon is a little bit better. Chaos Angel can treat a LIGHT or DARK monster you control as a Tuner, which doesn’t really come up in this Deck. On Special Summon, you can target and banish a card on the field and it also gains effects based on the original Attributes of the monsters used for its Synchro Summon. LIGHT makes Synchro Monsters you control unaffected by your opponent’s activated monster effects while DARK makes monsters you control unable to be destroyed by battle. Bane can’t exactly be used as removal in the same way, but it stil does have 1 effect that we could use which tributes a monster to target and Special Summon an RDA monster in your GY, helping you to potentially go for an OTK. Dis Pater has a similar usage as it can target a banished LIGHT or DARK and Special Summon it to your field, which is usually The Crimson King in this Deck. But, it also has its other effect which can be activated as a Quick Effect in response to your opponent’s activated monster effect, shuffling a banished card into the Deck, either destroying that monster if shuffled into your Deck or negating the activation if shuffled into your opponent’s Deck. 1 Stardust Warrior and 1 Stardust Dragon - Victim Sanctuary. Victim Sanctuary can Quick Effect Tribute itself when your opponent activates a card or effect in response to your card or effect activation to negate the effect and destroy that card. Also, if a monster(s) in your possession was Tributed this turn, you can Quick Effect banish it from GY to Special Summon a Stardust Synchro Monster from your Extra Deck. Victim Sanctuary is one of the Level 8 Synchro Monsters that we can alternatively make with Incredible Ecclesia and Fallen of the White Dragon if we already have access to Power Vice Dragon, which provides us with some insulation for our plays. But, it can also be used to bring out a Stardust Synchro Monster from your Extra Deck by banishing itself from GY during the turn in which a monster(s) in your possession was Tributed. Unfortunately, because this isn’t treated as a Synchro Summon, you are limited to Stardust Warrior, Stardust Dragon, and Stardust Spark Dragon as your best options. Nothing wrong with the latter 2, but negating a Special Summon of a monster(s) is a bit more useful than protection from destruction. Stardust Warrior can Quick Effect Tribute itself if your opponent would Special Summon a monster(s) to negate the Summon and destroy those monsters. Can’t bring it back during the End Phase because it wasn’t properly Synchro Summoned, and while it can float into a Level 8 or lower Warrior Synchro Monster from your Extra Deck, that’s kind of a whatever effect. 1 The Crimson King which is a Level 8 Synchro requiring a Tuner and 1+ non-Tuner DARK monsters. If Synchro Summoned, you can add a card that mentions RDA from Deck to hand and when your opponent activates a monster effect, you can Quick Effect banish it to Special Summon 1 Red Dragon Archfiend from your Extra Deck. This is treated as a Synchro Summon, RDA’s ATK is doubled and it cannot be destroyed by your opponent’s card effects. Being able to search an RDA-related card while also tagging out into RDA itself, doubling its ATK and making it indestructible by your opponent’s card effects is really good. 1 Scarlight Red Dragon Archfiend whose name becomes Red Dragon Archfiend on field or in GY and once per turn, you can destroy as many other Special Summoned Effect Monsters on the field with ATK less than or equal to its own ATK and then 500 damage is inflicted to your opponent for each monster destroyed. 1 Scarred Dragon Archfiend whose name also becomes Red Dragon Archfiend while on field or in GY. If it is sent from the Monster Zone to the GY, you can Special Summon 1 RDA from your Extra Deck. This is treated as a Synchro Summon, and then if it was sent to the GY as Synchro Material for a DARK Dragon Synchro Monster, you can destroy all Attack Position monsters your opponent controls. If you want the maximum benefit of that second effect, I’d definitely recommend playing HRDA Bane or Bystial Dis Pater over Chaos Angel. 2 Red Dragon Archfiend, the original one. 1 Ecclesia and the Dark Dragon. It is another good send off of The Fallen & The Virtuous as if it is in your GY,  you can target a Level 8 Fusion Monster in your GY or banishment and 1 card on the field to shuffle both those cards and Ecclesia and the Dark Dragon into the Deck. 1 Crimson Blade Dragon. I already talked about this card already, but its other effect I have yet to mention is that at the start of the Damage Step, if it battles a Level 5 or higher monster, you can destroy that monster. It is a pretty decent effect to have. Lastly, we have 1 Enigmaster Packbit and 1 Golden Cloud Beast - Malong as our sends with Fydraulis Harmonia. Packbit, if sent to the GY, can target 1 monster in your GY or 1 face-up monster your opponent controls to discard 1 card and place that monster in its owner’s Spell/Trap Zone as a Continuous Trap. Malong, if sent to the GY, can target 1 face-up card  your opponent controls and return it to the hand. 

Side Deck: 3 Nibiru the Primal Being. While you do have a couple of ways to deal with the Token, if you are worried about not having readily access to something that can get rid of the Token, you can replace this with Lava Golem or Kaijus. 2 Mulcharmy Purulia. 1 Harpie’s Feather Duster. 3 Spatial Trunade which returns up to 2 cards on the field to the hand, then each player places card from their hand on the bottom of the Deck in any order equal to the number returned to their hand. While OCG Tournament results with Beyond the Brave legal have yet to be seen, I imagine that Spatial Trunade will probably be a decent or pretty good Side Deck card for most Decks. For this Deck, it is mainly here to help against backrow going second, but it can also be used for monster removal as well. I did consider Lightning Storm, but I think that Spatial Trunade feels a bit better to use, especially coming out of testing. 2 Triple Tactics Thrust and 1 Triple Tactics Talent. 3 Infinite Impermanence.

deck_excerpt: A Live Twin, Fiendsmith, Dmod, Shining Sarc, Diabellestar Azamina turbo slop pile with some non-engine for fun
deck_description: This deck at it's core, is an Evil Twin/Fiendsmith deck with some secondary engines to help facilitate your combo and fortify your endboard. Evil Twin/Fiendsmith are famously weak against Nibiru. Thats why We run the DMG magicians apprentice, Diabellestar the Black Witch, and Apprentice Illusion Magician. The idea is to start with one Twin starter and one of these three spellcasters. Your gameplan is to normal summon your twin (Ideally main-deck Ki-sikil), effect to summon the other twin, then summon a spellcaster from hand, banish to summon DMOD, DMOD search gaze, then immediately use gaze on the DMOD on the field to summon Dragoon as your 5th summon. This helps you to secure the remainder of your combo without worry of Nibiru crashing the party. As a bonus, if you started with Diabellestar, you get to go into the small Azamina line ending with an Ilia Silva (extra Omni-negate) then after all that feel free to go crazy with the Evil Twin/Fiendsmith line. I like to end with Ilia Silva, Dragoon, Desirae with sequence attached, Trouble Sunny and A Bao A Qu. This gives 2 omni-negates, 1 negate of 2 cards on field, a pop and draw with the Trouble Sunny tag, and another pop or a revive of any light or dark monster (Ideal targets are Trouble Sunny or Dragoon if he gets removed). You also have a metalfoes fusion as discard fodder (a lot of effects in this deck need a discard) which can get you an extra draw for Dragoon/A Bao, and Duality (I'm experimenting with this one rn, not sure if it's worth it yet but it seems good.) This can either be used as discard fodder or used on your extra spellcasters to summon Fortune Lady Every for some follow up in case your opponent fully or partially cracks your board. Overall it is pretty strong even in the current meta, that being said it has some flaws. 1: every power play is a monster card on field, so the endbaord is VERY susceptible to Dark Ruler No More and Forbidden Droplet and can even have problems if your Silva and Dragoon get Lava Golemed. 2: I couldn't find a way to bring it down to 40 and keep the ratios I like, but the amount of draws you get can make up for the extra main-deck size. That being said it can probably be fine to knock off some of the non-engine and even a couple starters to get it down to 40. At the end of the day, this is just what I've found I have the best success with after trying numerous iterations of this list, so by all means, if you want to give it a go and maybe improve on this then be my guest!

inspired by me being way too slow and way too defensive for most modern yugioh formats

Using the Archfiend Engine

Royal Archfiend can search for any Archfiend card in this deck, except himself, giving you access to Regenesis Archfiend and allowing you to start your Regenesis plays.

Archfiend Playtime can special summon Archfiend's Origin from your hand or deck, while Archfiend's Usurpation can set Archfiend Playtime from your deck or graveyard. Also, the card you set with this effect can be activated this turn.

Archfiend's Origin is a 2500 attack monster that meets the summoning requirements of your Regenesis monsters. It has three useful effects that provide some extra value for this deck.

  1. You can special summon Archfiend's Origin from your hand by tributing 1 other Archfiend monster from your hand or field. This can convert a leftover Regenesis Archfiend sitting on your field into Archfiend's Origin. You can then use the graveyard effect of Regenesis to revive Regenesis Archfiend.
  2. If it is normal or special summoned, it can special summon Summoned Skull from your hand, deck, or graveyard.
  3. If you control a Summoned Skull, Archfiend's Origin can negate a monster's effect and destroy that monster.

Archfiend's Usurpation can also set Archfiend's Ghastly Glitch from your deck or graveyard. Archfiend's Ghastly Glitch can target and destroy a card on the field if you control a fiend, then it can send a fiend from your deck to the graveyard. Regenesis Archfiend meets the requirements for both of Ghastly Glitch's effects, and it can add itself to your hand if it was sent to the graveyard during your opponent's turn.

deck_description:
I was thinking about what I would put in a hypothetical 2-player starter kit to get new players into the game. Reflecting on a lot of the criticism of the most recent 2-player starter kit in real life, I'm not sure that I agree with the idea of giving a new player a truly fine-tuned meta deck right out of the gate. Instead, I've tried to construct something that puts emphasis on the things that they should be focusing on when they start moving over to real games against seasoned opponents, by drawing their eye to simple 2 and 3 step combos that lead to easy-to-understand payoffs revolving around Dark magician, some generic rank 4's, and some small ability to generate generic advantage.

I've chosen to add a suite of 5 basic hand traps to get them used to the idea of handtraps and understand the situations in which you would want to use them or hold them. The intent is for each of these to have an ideal target that you would truly care about in the other deck that comes in this starter kit, and for that to be outlined to the players in the manual.

While many of these cards are not ones that you would run in a truly modern Dark Magician deck, a true DM deck would not give a particularly good first impression to a new player, as it would require a detailed flowchart to teach them how to pilot it correctly. Simple, short, linear combos with easily understood payoffs would be much more important than true optimization.

Most non-handtrap effect monsters were chosen for their ability to tutor or special summon for the sake of emphasizing how important it is for a deck to be able to special summon and tutor.

Super-Poly is to teach about non-traditional removal.

Silent Magician should be a good lesson about the power of negations in a starting endboard.

We're giving Pharaoh's Servant becase it will help the new player understand the expected power level of cards, and obviously, we want them to feel powerful as they're playing.

I don't care that magi-magi magician girl is a bagillion dollars. Reprint it.

Rarities

Secret Overframe:

  • The Dark Magicians

Secret Rare:

  • Pharaoh's Servant
  • Dark Magician Girl
  • Vanilla DM

Ultra Rare:

  • Apprentice Illusion Magician
  • Magi Magi Magician Girl
  • Dark Magical Curtain

Super Rare:

  • Eternal Soul
  • Magician's Salvation
  • Dark Magician the Dragon Knight

All others commons.

Pack-ins:

  • 2 deck boxes themed after the two decks
  • a 6 sided die
  • a coin
  • 4 plastic indicators of different colors and shapes that the players can use to keep track of game state
  • a 2 player play-mat
  • a general "getting-started" rule booklet
  • one booklet per deck that contains infromation about how to pilot the deck and what your win condition is.
  • 3 secret rare tokens depicting a dramatized battle between the two decks in the product.

And obviously the counterpart to this deck would be a Blue-Eyes themed one. Blue-eyes represents a bit of a challenge because it's an actually "good" deck. So we would need to restrain ourselves in building to match this one's power level.
Blue eyes deck who can summon powerfull synchro and fusion monster
Just a wip of what can hopefully be a fairly powerful deck for the future, after more testing and stuff, may be possibly a meta deck
